paper_file: A Simple Transformer-Based Model for Ego4D Natural Language Queries Challenge.md

video_segmentation.answer: Not mentioned. The paper uses pre-extracted features derived from short clips of untrimmed videos but does not specify the method used to create these clips.
quotes: Given an untrimmed video and a text query, the Ego4D Natural Language Queries (NLQ) task seeks to localize the temporal window within the video where the answer to the query is evident" (Section 1)
Our method represents a video as a sequence of feature vectors, where each vector is derived from a short clip using pre-trained video backbones." (Section 2)

frame_sampling_method.answer: Not mentioned. The paper uses pre-extracted video features without detailing the internal frame sampling strategies of the backbone models.

input_video_length.answer: The model processes untrimmed video clips from the Ego4D NLQ dataset, which have fixed durations of either 8 or 20 minutes.
quotes: Given an untrimmed video and a text query..." (Section 1)
The video clips last either 8 or 20 minutes." (Section 3, Dataset and Evaluation Metrics)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Yes, spatiotemporal analysis is performed using video backbones like SlowFast and Omnivore, and a Transformer-based model with local self-attention processes the sequence of video features.
quotes: Further, our solution integrates several strong video features including SlowFast [2], Omnivore [3] and EgoVLP [6]." (Abstract)
It consists of two embedding convolutions followed by seven Transformer blocks with local self-attention." (Section 2, Video Encoder)

visual_analysis.answer: Yes, the model uses a visual-only modality, employing fused features from the SlowFast, Omnivore, and EgoVLP visual backbones.
quotes: We use the official release of Slowfast [2] and Omnivore [3] features as our video representation. We additionally include video features from EgoVLP [6]..." (Section 2, Input Video/Text Representations)
We fuse the features via simple concatenation and feed them into the video branch of our grounding model." (Section 2, Input Video/Text Representations)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned.

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned.

qa_interaction.answer: No, the system performs one-shot temporal localization for a given natural language query without any interactive or conversational capabilities.
quotes: Given an untrimmed video and a text query, the Ego4D Natural Language Queries (NLQ) task seeks to localize the temporal window within the video where the answer to the query is evident [4]." (Section 1)

retrieval_level.answer: The system retrieves and ranks temporal windows (clips/segments) within a video, which are refined using SoftNMS to produce the final predictions.
quotes: ...the Ego4D Natural Language Queries (NLQ) task seeks to localize the temporal window within the video..." (Section 1)

At inference time, the model outputs at most 2,000 event predictions for each video. The initial predictions are further combined and refined with SoftNMS [1], yielding final predictions subject to evaluation." (Section 2, Implementation Details)

inferring_method.answer: A Transformer-based model computes similarity between video and text embeddings, followed by shared classification and regression heads that predict event boundaries from foreground moments.
quotes: The resulting model considers every moment in the video as an event candidate, measures their relevance to the text query, and regresses the event boundaries from foreground moments." (Section 1)

The classification head outputs a binary score for each point on the pyramid, whereas the regression head predicts the distances from a foreground point to the event’s onset and offset." (Section 2, Classification and Regression Heads)

video_representation.answer: Videos are represented as a sequence of concatenated feature vector embeddings from pre-trained SlowFast, Omnivore, and EgoVLP backbones.
quotes: Our method represents a video as a sequence of feature vectors, where each vector is derived from a short clip using pre-trained video backbones." (Section 2)

We use the official release of Slowfast [2] and Omnivore [3] features as our video representation. We additionally include video features from EgoVLP [6]... We fuse the features via simple concatenation..." (Section 2, Input Video/Text Representations)

model_pipeline.answer: An untrimmed video and text query are input; video/text features are extracted, processed by Transformer encoders, fused with AdaAttN, and fed to classification/regression heads to output a localized temporal window.
quotes: Our model consists of a video branch and a text branch, both based on transformers. A similarity score is computed between every video and text embeddings. Those with high similarity are fused and undergo further processing for event boundary regression." (Fig. 1)

dataset.answer: The Ego4D NLQ dataset, which contains 227 hours of video across 5.9K clips with 22.5K event-query pairs.
quotes: The Ego4D NLQ dataset contains 227 hours of videos with 5.9K clips and 22.5K pairs of events and text queries." (Section 3, Dataset and Evaluation Metrics)

comparisons.answer: Compared to baselines like ReLER@ZJU-Alibaba and the top-ranked Red Panda on the Ego4D NLQ dataset, the model surpasses the top-ranked solution by up to 5.5 absolute percentage points in R@5.
quotes: On the test split, our model ultimately achieves 12.64% Mean R@1, 2.12% absolute percentage points higher than the last champion [7]." (Section 3, Results)
Meanwhile, our R@5 scores beat all entries on the leaderboard, and in particular, surpasses the top-ranked solution by up to 5.5 absolute percentage points." (Section 3, Results)

hyperparameters.answer: The model uses an embedding dimension of 512, 16 attention heads, AdamW optimizer, 9 epochs, a mini-batch size of 16, and a learning rate of 1e-3 with linear warm-up and cosine decay.
quotes: Our model maintains an embedding dimension of 512 throughout the network. The Transformer layers employ 16 heads for selfattention and AdaAttN. The model is trained using the AdamW optimizer [9] for 9 epochs. We use a mini-batch size of 16 and a learning rate of 1e-3 with linear warm-up and consine decay." (Section 2, Implementation Details)

environment.answer: Not mentioned.

repository.answer: Not mentioned.

authors.answer: The authors are Sicheng Mo, Fangzhou Mu, and Yin Li from the University of Wisconsin-Madison, for the Ego4D Natural Language Queries (NLQ) Challenge.
quotes: Sicheng Mo, Fangzhou Mu, Yin Li" (Authors list)

paper_file: Audio Does Matter Importance-Aware Multi-Granularity Fusion for Video Moment Retrieval.md

video_segmentation.answer: Not mentioned. The paper uses proposal-free methods on untrimmed videos, which do not require pre-segmentation into shots or scenes.
quotes: For proposal-free [12, 22, 62, 63, 68–70, 74], they eliminate the need for predefined proposals, processing raw visual and textual features directly through cross-modal matching." (Section 2)

Video moment retrieval aims to retrieve the start-end frame pair {fs, fe} of a specific segment that semantically match the textual query Q = {wi}i=1 N from the untrimmed video V = {ft}t=1 T..." (Section 3.1)

frame_sampling_method.answer: Not mentioned.

input_video_length.answer: Not mentioned. The paper processes untrimmed videos from standard datasets but does not specify their durations.
quotes: Video moment retrieval aims to retrieve the start-end frame pair {fs, fe} of a specific segment that semantically match the textual query Q = {wi}i=1 N from the untrimmed video V = {ft}t=1 T..." (Section 3.1)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Yes, spatiotemporal analysis is performed using visual backbones like I3D, SlowFast, and InternVideo2, and temporal modeling is done with 1D convolutions and Bi-GRUs.
quotes: We use I3D [7] as vision backbone with GloVe [43] embeddings." (Table 5)
CLIP+SF" refers to SlowFast [14] combined with CLIP [44] , "IV2" denotes InternVideo2 [55]." (Table 6)
...we construct symmetric multi-kernel 1D convolutional networks and to deeply perceive the local relationships between video frames and audio clips..." (Section 3.3.2)

visual_analysis.answer: No, the model is multimodal, not visual-only. It uses visual backbones including I3D, SlowFast combined with CLIP, and InternVideo2.
quotes: For vision modality, we extract the original visual features V ∈ RT ×dv by a pre-trained visual CNN [7, 52, 76] and further obtain corresponding enhanced features V' ∈ RT ×d by a visual encoder which shares the same structure as audio encoder." (Section 3.2)
We use I3D [7] as vision backbone with GloVe [43] embeddings." (Table 5)
CLIP+SF" refers to SlowFast [14] combined with CLIP [44] , "IV2" denotes InternVideo2 [55]." (Table 6)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned. The paper uses a general pre-trained audio-aware CNN for all audio, without specific analysis for speech content like ASR.
quotes: For audio modality, we firstly use pre-trained audio-aware CNN [21, 27] to extract its original features A ∈ RT ×da, then employ an audio encoder which is composed by an FFN, convolutional and transformer layers on them follow [69]..." (Section 3.2)

sound_audio_analysis.answer: A general pre-trained audio-aware CNN is used to extract features from all audio, including non-speech sounds, but no specific non-speech sound analysis models are mentioned.
quotes: For audio modality, we firstly use pre-trained audio-aware CNN [21, 27] to extract its original features A ∈ RT ×da..." (Section 3.2)
...the audio of some videos may be complete noise or background sound that is meaningless to the moment determination." (Abstract)

qa_interaction.answer: Not mentioned. The system performs video moment retrieval based on a single query and is not an interactive Q&A or chat system.

retrieval_level.answer: The system retrieves timestamps by predicting the start and end logits for a specific moment within a given video.
quotes: Video moment retrieval aims to retrieve the start-end frame pair {fs, fe} of a specific segment that semantically match the textual query..." (Section 3.1)
Following previous works [69], we exploit the moment predictor as the retrieval heads to output the start logits and end logits of the moment, and get the final prediction Ps and Pe." (Section 3.5)

inferring_method.answer: The model infers moments using an end-to-end deep learning model (IMG) that fuses audio, visual, and text features at multiple granularities and predicts start/end logits.
quotes: To this end, we propose a novel Importance-aware Multi-Granularity fusion model (IMG), which learns to dynamically and selectively aggregate the audio-vision-text contexts for VMR." (Abstract)

Finally, the three-level features are concatenated and fed into the predictor to output predictions, while the visual-only feature and audio-only feature are also fed into their respective unimodal predictors." (Section 3.1)

video_representation.answer: Video content is represented as sequences of feature embeddings from pre-trained visual (I3D, SlowFast, InternVideo2) and audio (audio-aware CNN) backbones, which are then fused with text embeddings (GloVe).
quotes: For audio modality, we firstly use pre-trained audio-aware CNN [21, 27] to extract its original features A ∈ RT ×da..." (Section 3.2)
For vision modality, we extract the original visual features V ∈ RT ×dv by a pre-trained visual CNN [7, 52, 76]..." (Section 3.2)
For textual query, we directly initialize it with GloVe embeddings [43]." (Section 3.2)

model_pipeline.answer: Pre-extracted visual, audio, and text features are fused; an audio importance predictor weights the audio modality for a multi-granularity fusion module, which then feeds into a predictor to output start/end timestamps for the moment.
quotes: Given the pre-extracted visual, audio, and text features by the corresponding encoder, our IMG model first employs feed-forward network (FFN) layers to map these features into a common latent space. Then, we employ interactions between vision-text and audiotext pairs, fusing them to derive text-semantic-activated visual and audio features." (Section 3.1)

Then, the visual and audio features will be fed into a multi-granularity fusion module to aggregate the target-moment-related information at local-, event-, and global-levels according to the previously obtained important weight." (Section 3.1)

dataset.answer: The paper uses Charades-STA and ActivityNet Captions, and introduces a new manually curated test set called Charades-AudioMatter, derived from Charades-STA.
quotes: We conduct our experiments on two video moment retrieval benchmark datasets with audio, i.e., Charades-STA [16] and ActivityNet Captions [28], as well as Chardes-AudioMatter dataset reconstructed by ours." (Section 4.1)
Moreover, to further validate the model's capability in integrating audio modality, we introduce a new dataset, Charades-AudioMatter, where audio matters for each test query." (Section 4.1)

comparisons.answer: The model is compared against baselines (visual-only) and SOTA methods like ADPN, EMB, and EAMAT on Charades-STA, ActivityNet Captions, and the new Charades-AudioMatter dataset, reporting significant gains (e.g., +4.71 R1@7 on Charades-STA).
quotes: In Table 5, we evaluate our IMG on Charades-STA and ActivityNet Captions and compare it with existing audio-incorporated VMR methods." (Section 4.4)

In Table 7, we evaluate IMG on Charades-AudioMatter where the audio data are more consistent and reliable. We compare our method with open-source methods that exhibit competitive performance on Charades-STA." (Section 4.4)
On Charades-STA and ActivityNet Captions, our IMG achieves the best performance on all metrics." (Section 4.4)

hyperparameters.answer: Not mentioned. The paper references balancing parameters (λ1, λ2, λ3) and temperature coefficients (γ, τ) in equations but does not state their values or other training hyperparameters.
quotes: ...where λ1, λ2 and λ3 are the balancing parameters." (Section 3.5)
...where L_a_ret and L_v_ret represents the retrieval loss of audio branch and visual branch, respectively, γ is temperature coefficient." (Section 3.3.1)

...where s s/e is start or end logits predicted by the student unimodal branch, t s/e is start or end logits predicted by the teacher fusion branch, τ is temperature coefficient and σ is softmax function." (Section 3.4)

environment.answer: Not mentioned.

repository.answer: Yes, a GitHub repository is provided at https://github.com/HuiGuanLab/IMG.
quotes: Our code is available at https://github.com/HuiGuanLab/IMG." (Abstract)

authors.answer: The authors are affiliated with Zhejiang University, Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, Peking University, University of Science and Technology of China, and Zhejiang Gongshang University. The paper is for the MM '25 conference.
quotes: Junan Lin Zhejiang University Hangzhou, China... Xiaoye Qu Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory Shanghai, China... Daizong Liu Peking University Beijing, China... paper_file: Bridging High-Quality Audio and Video Via Language for Sound Effects Retrieval from Visual Queries.md

video_segmentation.answer: Not mentioned. The paper uses pre-existing short video clips and describes segmenting audio files, not video.

The frame-level captions are then temporally aligned, ensuring a seamless transition between consecutive frames and maintaining continuity in the video caption." (IV. A. Video Pre-processing: Caption Generation)

As an encoder, ViT processes the individual frames extracted from the video. ViT divides each frame into fixedsize non-overlapping patches and flattens them into sequences." (IV. A. Video Pre-processing: Vision Transformer (ViT) Encoder)

(ABSTRACT)

To evaluate the effectiveness of CLAMR in a downstream scenario, we test on Long Video Question Answering (QA) tasks using two benchmarks: the long-video subset (30 - 60 minutes in length) of Video-MME [] and the (900, 3600s] duration group from the dev set of LongVideoBench []." (Sec. 6.4)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: A Vision-Language Model (VLM) jointly encodes a sequence of video frames and other modalities, enabling implicit spatiotemporal analysis via its internal attention mechanisms over the combined input sequence.
quotes: all input modalities are first concatenated into a single sequence – with visual inputs preceding textual inputs, based on the model’s training regime." (Sec. 3.2)
The VLM then processes this combined sequence to generate contextualized hidden states for all tokens and patches." (Sec. 3.2)

visual_analysis.answer: The model performs visual analysis using video frames as a primary modality, employing the Qwen-VL-2.5-3B2 model as its vision-language backbone.
quotes: CLAMR, a multimodal, late-interaction retriever that jointly indexes four modalities: video frames, transcribed speech, on-screen text, and other metadata." (Abstract)
We use Qwen-VL-2.5-3B2 [] as the backbone for CLAMR with VLM, which offers strong multimodal accuracy at a modest size." (Sec. 5)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Speech is analyzed using ASR transcripts as text input for the primary VLM-based model, while an alternate Omni-model variant processes raw audio directly using a Whisper audio encoder.
quotes: Unlike VLMs, which require an initial conversion of ASR output to text, omni models such as Qwen-Omni [41] can directly process raw audio." (Sec. 3.2)
For the Omni-model variant, we experimented with Qwen-Omni-3B3 [], which utilizes Whisper [] as its underlying audio encoder." (Sec. 5)

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned. The paper mentions "ambient audio" conceptually, but the implementation only details models for speech analysis (ASR/Whisper) and does not specify any models for non-speech sound analysis.
quotes: Online video web content is richly multimodal: a single video blends vision, speech, ambient audio, and on-screen text." (Abstract)

qa_interaction.answer: The system is used in a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline for long-video question answering, where it retrieves relevant frames for a VLM (Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Inst) to generate a final answer.
quotes: we set up a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline: given a long video, the retriever first selects key frames relevant to the question, which are subsequently provided as input to a VLM (Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Inst) answerer." (Sec. 6.4)

retrieval_level.answer: The system retrieves entire documents (videos) in its primary retrieval task and selects relevant key frames for the downstream long-video question answering task.
quotes: Given a query q, the retriever must identify the most relevant document d." (Sec. 3.1)
the retriever first selects key frames relevant to the question, which are subsequently provided as input to a VLM" (Sec. 6.4)

inferring_method.answer: Relevance is inferred via a contextualized late-interaction mechanism, which computes fine-grained, token-level maximum cosine similarities between query tokens and document tokens across all modalities.
quotes: late-interaction (LI) models first independently encode queries and retrieval targets, then compute lightweight but fine-grained token-level similarity, facilitating precise relevance judgments" (Sec. 1)
for each query token, its maximum cosine similarity to any document token is computed, and these maximum similarities are then summed over all query tokens." (Sec. 3.3)

video_representation.answer: Content is represented as multiple contextualized token- and patch-level embeddings for each modality, generated by a unified VLM backbone, which are then used for fine-grained late-interaction similarity scoring.
quotes: late-interaction models preserve multiple embeddings per item for detailed matching." (Sec. 2)

The VLM then processes this combined sequence to generate contextualized hidden states for all tokens and patches. Finally, these hidden states are passed through a projection layer to produce the final representation for each token." (Sec. 3.2)

model_pipeline.answer: A text query and multimodal video documents (frames, ASR, OCR, metadata) are fed into the CLAMR model, which uses a VLM to generate contextualized embeddings and a late-interaction mechanism to produce a ranked list of relevant documents.
quotes: A text query and multimodal video document consisting of video's visual, audio, OCR, and metadata signals are encoded by the model. Then, token-level late interaction yields a similarity score for each modality; the highest of these scores becomes the query-document similarity." (Figure 2 caption)

dataset.answer: The paper introduces the synthetically generated MULTIVENT 2.0++ dataset for training and evaluates on MULTIVENT 2.0, MSR-VTT, and, for downstream tasks, Video-MME and LongVideoBench.
quotes: we introduce MULTIVENT 2.0++, a large-scale synthetic training dataset built on MULTIVENT 2.0" (Abstract)
We also include MSR-VTT [42], a standard text-video retrieval benchmark used in several prior works" (Sec. 5)

we test on Long Video Question Answering (QA) tasks using two benchmarks: the long-video subset (30 - 60 minutes in length) of Video-MME [] and the (900, 3600s] duration group from the dev set of LongVideoBench []" (Sec. 6.4)

comparisons.answer: CLAMR is compared against single- and multi-modality baselines including mCLIP, ImageBind, and LanguageBind on MULTIVENT 2.0++ and MSR-VTT, improving nDCG@10 by 35.4 points over the best multimodal baseline on MULTIVENT 2.0++.
quotes: on MULTIVENT 2.0++, CLAMR improves nDCG@10 by 25.6 points over the bestperforming single-modality retriever and by 35.4 points over the best-performing multi-modality retriever." (Abstract)

As single-modality baselines, we use multilingual CLIP (mCLIP)4 from Reimers and Gurevych [] by processing only their corresponding modality (video, audio, OCR, or metadata). For multi-modality baselines, we use several strong encoders: ImageBind [], and LanguageBind []." (Sec. 5)

hyperparameters.answer: Key hyperparameters include a batch size of 16, a learning rate of 1e-5, 1 to 5 training epochs, an 8-bit Adam optimizer, and QLoRA with a rank of 128.
quotes: We train separate versions of CLAMR on MULTIVENT 2.0++ and MSRVTT for 1 and 5 epochs, respectively." (Sec. 5)
Training is performed using a batch size of 16, distributed across 8 A100 80GB GPUs." (Sec. 5)
we employ 4-bit quantization with QLoRA [], setting the LoRA rank r = 128 and α = 128." (Sec. 5)
we keep default hyper-parameters, train with the 8-bit Adam optimizer, and set the learning rate to 1 × 10−5 for all experiments." (Sec. 5)

environment.answer: Training was conducted on 8 A100 80GB GPUs, requiring approximately 10 hours for the MULTIVENT 2.0++ dataset and 4 hours for the MSRVTT dataset.
quotes: Training is performed using a batch size of 16, distributed across 8 A100 80GB GPUs." (Sec. 5)
Training on MULTIVENT 2.0++ required approximately 10 hours, while training on MSRVTT took about 4 hours." (Sec. 5)

repository.answer: The paper states that code and data are made available, but a direct link is not provided in the text.
quotes: Code and data are available in ." (Footnote 1)

authors.answer: The authors are affiliated with UNC Chapel Hill; the publication venue and year are not mentioned in the provided text.
quotes: David Wan Han Wang Elias Stengel-Eskin Jaemin Cho Mohit Bansal UNC Chapel Hill {davidwan, hwang, esteng, jmincho, mbansal}@cs.unc.edu" (Title section)

paper_file: Condensed Movies Story Based Retrieval with Contextual Embeddings.md

video_segmentation.answer: The dataset uses pre-segmented "key scenes" or clips from the MovieClips YouTube channel, which represent the salient parts of a movie's storyline.
quotes: In a similar spirit, videos from the MovieClips channel on YouTube1 , which contains the key scenes or clips from numerous movies, are also accompanied by a semantic text description describing the content of each clip." (Section 1)

The video data consists of over 33,000 clips from 3,600 movies (see Table 2). For each movie there is a set of ordered clips (typically 10 or so) covering the salient parts of the film (examples can be seen in Fig. 2, top row)." (Section 3)

frame_sampling_method.answer: Frames are sampled uniformly at 25 fps for RGB object embeddings and 1 fps for scene features.
quotes: RGB object frame-level embeddings of the visual data are generated with an SENet-154 model [] pretrained on ImageNet for the task of image classification. Frames are extracted at 25 fps, where each frame is resized to 224×224 pixels." (Section 5.3)
Scene features of 2208 dimensions are encoded using a DenseNet161 model [] pretrained on the Places365 dataset [], applied to 224×224 pixel centre crops of frames extracted at 1fps." (Section 5.3)

input_video_length.answer: The dataset consists of long video clips with a median duration of 132 seconds, each around two minutes. The condensed movies are roughly 20 minutes each, and no processed video is ≥ 60 minutes.
quotes: Each around two minutes in length, the clips contain the same rich and complex story as full-length films but an order of magnitude shorter." (Section 3)
The distribution of video lengths in our dataset can be seen in Fig. 2 – with just the key scenes, each movie has been condensed into roughly 20 minutes each." (Section 3)
Median clip len. (secs) | 132" (Table 2)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Spatiotemporal analysis is performed using motion embeddings generated from an I3D inception model pre-trained on the Kinetics dataset.
quotes: Motion embeddings are generated using the I3D inception model [] trained on Kinetics [], following the procedure described by []." (Section 5.3)

visual_analysis.answer: No, it is a multi-modal approach, but it uses several visual backbones including SENet-154 for objects, I3D for motion, SENet50 for faces, and DenseNet161 for scenes.
quotes: RGB object frame-level embeddings of the visual data are generated with an SENet-154 model [] pretrained on ImageNet for the task of image classification." (Section 5.3)
Motion embeddings are generated using the I3D inception model [] trained on Kinetics []." (Section 5.3)
Each box is then passed through an SENet50 [] trained on the VGGFace2 dataset [] for the task of face verification, to extract a facial feature embedding, which is L2 normalised." (Section 5.3)
Scene features of 2208 dimensions are encoded using a DenseNet161 model [] pretrained on the Places365 dataset []." (Section 5.3)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Yes, speech content is analyzed via subtitles, which are encoded using BERT embeddings averaged across all words.
quotes: Subtitles are encoded using BERT embeddings [] averaged across all words." (Section 5.3)
The highest boosts are obtained from the face features and the speech features, as expected, since we hypothesize that these are crucial for following human-centric storylines." (Section 5.4)

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned. The paper focuses on visual modalities and speech content from subtitles, with no mention of non-speech audio analysis.

qa_interaction.answer: Not mentioned. The system is designed for text-to-video retrieval, not interactive question-answering or chat.
quotes: In contrast, we propose to evaluate story understanding through the task of text-to-video retrieval, from a set of key scenes in a movie that condense most of the salient parts of the storyline." (Section 2)
retrieval via text queries can be a more intuitive way for a human to interact with an intelligent system, and might help avoid some of the biases present inherently in VQA datasets []." (Section 2)

retrieval_level.answer: The system performs text-to-video retrieval, returning a ranked list of video clips from a gallery set in response to a text query.
quotes: Our second objective is to explore the role of context in enabling video retrieval. We define a text-to-video retrieval task on the CMD..." (Section 1)

For the case of cross-movie retrieval, the metrics are reported over the entire test set of videos, i.e. given a text query, there is a 'gallery' set of 6,581 possible matching videos (Table 3)." (Section 5.1)

inferring_method.answer: The system infers semantic similarity by learning a joint embedding space where multi-modal video features (scene, face, object, action, speech) are projected alongside text query embeddings using a Mixture of Embedding Experts model.
quotes: We provide a deep network baseline for text-to-video retrieval on our dataset, combining character, speech and visual cues into a single video embedding" (Abstract)

The MoEE (Mixture of Embedded Experts) model proposed by [] comprises a multi-modal video model in combination with a system of context gates that learn to fuse together different pretrained experts." (Section 5.2)

video_representation.answer: Videos are represented using a collection of aggregated feature embeddings from pre-trained expert models for objects (SENet-154), motion (I3D), faces (SENet50), scenes (DenseNet161), and speech (BERT on subtitles).
quotes: In order to capture the rich content of a video, we draw on existing powerful representations for a number of different semantic tasks. These are first extracted at a frame-level, then aggregated by taking the mean to produce a single feature vector per modality per video." (Section 5.3)

model_pipeline.answer: Video clips and a text query → Multi-modal expert feature extraction (visual, speech, face) → MoEE with Contextual Boost Module computes similarity in a joint embedding space → Ranked list of relevant video clips.
quotes: We provide a deep network baseline for text-to-video retrieval on our dataset, combining character, speech and visual cues into a single video embedding; and finally (iii) We demonstrate how the addition of context from other video clips improves retrieval performance." (Abstract)
we extend this formulation in order to incorporate past context into the retrieval model." (Section 4)

dataset.answer: The paper introduces the Condensed Movies Dataset (CMD), created from the MovieClips YouTube channel, consisting of over 3,600 movies.
quotes: We create the Condensed Movies Dataset (CMD) consisting of the key scenes from over 3K movies: each key scene is accompanied by a high level semantic description of the scene, character face-tracks, and metadata about the movie." (Abstract)
The dataset is scalable, obtained automatically from YouTube, and is freely available for anybody to download and use." (Abstract)
Data, code, models and features can be found at ." (Section 1)

comparisons.answer: The proposed MoEE + CBM model is compared against baselines including E2EWS, CE, and MoEE on the CMD dataset, improving upon the MoEE baseline by +0.9 in R@1 for cross-movie retrieval.
quotes: We show that the Contextual Boost module can be effectively used in conjunction with existing video retrieval architectures, improving performance for both CE and MoEE, with the latter being the best performing model." (Section 5.4)

hyperparameters.answer: Adam optimizer with LR 0.001, batch size 32, margin 0.121, projection dimension 512, 10 NetVLAD clusters, and N=3 context videos. Input frames are resized to 224×224 pixels.
quotes: Optimization is performed with Adam [], using a learning rate of 0.001, and a batch size of 32. The margin hyperparameter m for the bidirectional ranking loss is set to a value of 0.121, the common projection dimension D to 512, and the description NetVLAD clusters to 10." (Section 5.3)
For CBM, we select the number of past and future context videos to be N=3, ablations for hyperparameters and using different amounts of context are given in the supplementary material." (Section 5.3)

environment.answer: Not mentioned. The paper only states that the models are implemented with PyTorch.
quotes: All baselines and CBM are implemented with PyTorch []." (Section 5.3)

repository.answer: A repository link is mentioned as a placeholder "Data, code, models and features can be found at ." but the actual URL is not provided.
quotes: Data, code, models and features can be found at ." (Section 1)

authors.answer: The authors are from the Visual Geometry Group, Department of Engineering Science, University of Oxford. The venue and year are not mentioned in the provided text.
quotes: Max Bain, Arsha Nagrani, Andrew Brown, and Andrew Zisserman" (Title Block)

Quotes:
- "We provide a deep network baseline for text-to-video retrieval on our dataset, combining character, speech and visual cues into a single video embedding; and finally (iii) We demonstrate how the addition of context from other video clips improves retrieval performance." (Abstract)
- "we extend this formulation in order to incorporate past context into the retrieval model." (Section 4)

13) dataset
Answer: The paper introduces the Condensed Movies Dataset (CMD), created from the MovieClips YouTube channel, consisting of over 3,600 movies.
Quotes:
- "We create the Condensed Movies Dataset (CMD) consisting of the key scenes from over 3K movies: each key scene is accompanied by a high level semantic description of the scene, character face-tracks, and metadata about the movie." (Abstract)
- "The dataset is scalable, obtained automatically from YouTube, and is freely available for anybody to download and use." (Abstract)
- "Data, code, models and features can be found at ." (Section 1)

14) comparisons
Answer: The proposed MoEE + CBM model is compared against baselines including E2EWS, CE, and MoEE on the CMD dataset, improving upon the MoEE baseline by +0.9 in R@1 for cross-movie retrieval.
Quotes:
- "We show that the Contextual Boost module can be effectively used in conjunction with existing video retrieval architectures, improving performance for both CE and MoEE, with the latter being the best performing model." (Section 5.4)
- "MoEE + CBM (ours) | 5.6 | 17.6 | 26.1 | 50 | 243.9" (Table 4)

15) hyperparameters
Answer: Adam optimizer with LR 0.001, batch size 32, margin 0.121, projection dimension 512, 10 NetVLAD clusters, and N=3 context videos. Input frames are resized to 224×224 pixels.
Quotes:
- "Optimization is performed with Adam [], using a learning rate of 0.001, and a batch size of 32. The margin hyperparameter m for the bidirectional ranking loss is set to a value of 0.121, the common projection dimension D to 512, and the description NetVLAD clusters to 10." (Section 5.3)
- "For CBM, we select the number of past and future context videos to be N=3, ablations for hyperparameters and using different amounts of context are given in the supplementary material." (Section 5.3)

16) environment
Answer: Not mentioned. The paper only states that the models are implemented with PyTorch.
Quotes:
- "All baselines and CBM are implemented with PyTorch []." (Section 5.3)

17) repository
Answer: A repository link is mentioned as a placeholder "Data, code, models and features can be found at ." but the actual URL is not provided.
Quotes:
- "Data, code, models and features can be found at ." (Section 1)

18) authors
Answer: The authors are from the Visual Geometry Group, Department of Engineering Science, University of Oxford. The venue and year are not mentioned in the provided text.

model_pipeline.answer: Video and question inputs are encoded by S3D and DistilBERT, processed by a cross-modal correlation module, fused by a 2-layer transformer, and classified by an MLP to output an answer.
quotes: The synoptic scheme of the proposed method is illustrated in Fig.1. It contains two key components: (1) the cross-modal correlation module, and (2) the multimodal fusion module. As a pre-processing step, we start by extracting the video and text embeddings." (Section III. METHODOLOGY)

dataset.answer: The model is pre-trained on a 164,148-sample subset of HowToVQA 69M and fine-tuned on MSVD-QA; the rephrasing model for attacks is trained on Quora, MSRP, and PAWS.
quotes: Under the framework of a pre-training, then fine-tuning paradigm, we have trained the model on the HowToVQA 69M task-specific dataset. ... We randomly select 164148 training samples to reduce memory and computational requirements." (Section IV. A. Datasets)
For fine-tuning, we have retained the popular MSVD-QA videoQA dataset, which represents a smaller dataset automatically derived from MSVD." (Section IV. A. Datasets)
For training, we use three datasets: Quora [52] (400k training samples), MSRP 53 and PAWS [54] (108k training samples)." (Section III. D. Rephrasing attacks)

comparisons.answer: The model is compared against Co-Mem, HCRN, B2A, and CoMVT on the MSVD-QA dataset, achieving a 4.61% accuracy gain over the state-of-the-art CoMVT.
quotes: We have compared our approach to various state of the art methods on the MSVD-QA dataset [5]. Table III summarizes the accuracy of the different VideoQA models retained for comparison. More precisely, we have considered the following methods Co-Mem [19], HCRN[23] , B2A [57] and CoMVT [2]." (Section IV. D. Comparison with the state of the art)
The proposed method achieves the highest accuracy of 43.57%. In particular, it outperforms the state of the art CoMVT model by 4.61%..." (Section IV. D. Comparison with the state of the art)

hyperparameters.answer: Batch size: 16 (pre-train)/32 (fine-tune); LR: 1e-4 (pre-train)/1e-5 (fine-tune); Epochs: 20; Clips: 20; Frames/clip: 32; Max tokens: 20; Embedding size: 512; Attention heads: 8.
quotes: For pre-processing, we uniformly sample the video into N=20 clips. Similarly, we set the maximum number of tokens in the question to T=20. We project the video features and text features into a common embedding space of size d=512. For the multimodal transformer, a number of H=8 attention heads are retained." (Section IV. B. Implementation details)

A cosine annealing learning rate schedule has been used, with initial values of 10−4 for pre-training and 10−5 for finetuning respectively. For optimization, we have adopted the Adam approach with batch size of 16 for pre-training and 32 for fine-tuning. The training process has been run on 2 NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2080 GPUs and for 20 epochs." (Section IV. B. Implementation details)

environment.answer: Training was performed on 2 NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2080 GPUs for 20 epochs.

paper_file: ContextIQ A Multimodal Expert-Based Video Retrieval System for Contextual Advertising.md

video_segmentation.answer: Long-form videos are segmented into scenes using PySceneDetect with default parameters.
quotes: Long-form content, such as movies and shows, is processed by ContextIQ by breaking them into shorter videos using scene detection. We use PySceneDetect [16] for scene detection with default parameters." (Sec. 6)

frame_sampling_method.answer: Frames are sampled uniformly at a rate of one every ten frames for both visual embedding generation and metadata extraction.
quotes: we split the video into equal segments of 15 seconds each and sample one out of ten frames for embedding generation (ref. Sec. 3.1.1)." (Sec. 4.1)
For both place and object detection, predictions are sampled from every 10th frame." (Sec. 4.2)

input_video_length.answer: The system processes long-form content like movies and TV shows (≥ 60 minutes) by segmenting them into shorter scenes, with processed clips ranging from 15 seconds to 1 minute.
quotes: processing long-form streaming content" (Sec. 1)
utilize an internal dataset (Val-2) comprising over 2,000 long-form videos (movies, TV, and OTT contents)" (Sec. 5.2)
For evaluation, we randomly sample 600 scene clips and extract the first minute of each." (Sec. 4.3)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Spatiotemporal analysis is performed for action recognition using a fine-tuned VideoMAE2 model, while frame-level features are aggregated using temporal mean pooling for the main video representation.
quotes: We use a fine-tuned video masked autoencoder model (VideoMAE2) [67] on the Kinetics 710 dataset [14, 15, 35] for video action recognition." (Sec. 4.2)

The aggregation functions and used during audio and video embedding generation (Sec. 3.1) are temporal mean pooling functions, shown to be effective over other temporal aggregation techniques [10,46]." (Sec. 4.1)

visual_analysis.answer: No, it is a multimodal system; visual analysis uses a BLIP2 Qformer for embeddings, YOLOv5 for objects, a fine-tuned ResNet50 for places, and VideoMAE2 for actions.
quotes: ContextIQ utilizes modality-specific experts—video, audio, transcript (captions), and metadata such as objects, actions, emotion, etc.—to create semantically rich video representations." (Abstract)
The vision-text model is a pre-trained BLIP2 Qformer [42], and we use the implementation provided by LAVIS [40]." (Sec. 4.1)
We use the YOLOv5 model [64], trained on the Objects 365 dataset [56], to detect objects within videos" (Sec. 4.2)
For place detection, we finetune a ResNet50 model [32] on the Places365 dataset [76]." (Sec. 4.2)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Speech content is analyzed via video transcripts (captions), which are encoded using a pre-trained MPNet model and processed for named entities with RoBERTa.
quotes: To incorporate textual and speech information from a video, we encode the caption (transcript) for a given video using a text encoder" (Sec. 3.1.3)
The text encoder is a pre-trained MPNet model [58] fine-tuned on a set of 1 billion text-text pairs" (Sec. 4.1)
For named entity recognition, we utilize the text-based RoBERTa model, en_core_web_trf from Spacy [33] to extract the named entities from the captions of each video scene." (Sec. 4.2)

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Non-speech audio like sound effects, music, and ambient noise is analyzed using the CLAP audio encoder.
quotes: To capture the non-verbal audio elements in a video, such as sound effects, music, and ambient noise, we utilize the audio encoder from an audio-text model [71]." (Sec. 3.1.2)
The audio encoder is the CLAP [71] model as implemented in [2]." (Sec. 4.1)

qa_interaction.answer: Not mentioned.

retrieval_level.answer: The system retrieves relevant video scenes or clips for contextual ad placement, storing the results in a scene-to-context lookup database.
quotes: ContextIQ's multimodal search (ref. Sec. 3.2) identifies scenes where creatives can be contextually served." (Sec. 6)
The selected scenes are stored in the scene-to-context lookup DB." (Sec. 6)

inferring_method.answer: Roles and activities are inferred using a suite of expert models: VideoMAE2 for actions, YOLOv5 for objects, ResNet50 for places, and an ensemble of LLAMA 3 8B and BERT for hate speech.
quotes: We use a fine-tuned video masked autoencoder model (VideoMAE2) [67] on the Kinetics 710 dataset [14, 15, 35] for video action recognition." (Sec. 4.2)

For hate speech detection, we use a weighted ensemble of two models. First, we use a pre-trained LLAMA 3 8B Instruct model [7]... Secondly, we use a pre-trained BERT classifier [36] trained on the HateXplain dataset [49]" (Sec. 4.2)

video_representation.answer: Video content is represented by separate embeddings for video (BLIP2), audio (CLAP), captions (MPNet), and a metadata sentence (MPNet), all stored in a multimodal database.
quotes: We extract embeddings from multiple modalities, including raw video, audio, captions (speech transcripts), and combined visual (objects, places, actions, emotion) and textual (NER, emotion, profanity, hate speech) metadata" (Sec. 3)
After inference with the task-specific models, we store the extracted information into a metadata sentence M." (Sec. 3.1.4)
These embeddings are then stored in a multimodal embeddings database." (Sec. 3)

model_pipeline.answer: Input videos → Expert models extract features and metadata → Modality-specific encoders generate embeddings stored in a database → A text query is encoded, compared to embeddings, and scores are aggregated and filtered for brand safety to retrieve relevant video scenes.
quotes: Input videos are processed by the metadata extracting module, which uses expert models for extracting objects, actions, places, etc., and converts it into a metadata sentence . Four modality encoders then encode the video frames, audio, caption (transcripts) and metadata in parallel and store the embeddings in a multimodal embeddings DB." (Figure 1 Caption)

Text query t is first encoded by different text encoders and the multimodal embedding DB is searched to find similar videos. The aggregation module combines the results obtained from different modalities, and the final results are obtained after applying brand-safety filters" (Figure 2 Caption)

dataset.answer: The system was evaluated on MSR-VTT, Condensed Movies, and a new *Val-1* dataset, with an internal *Val-2* dataset for ablations. Expert models used Objects 365, Places365, and Kinetics 710.
quotes: We evaluate the effectiveness of our ContextIQ system across several video retrieval benchmarks, such as MSR-VTT [], Condensed Movies [], and a newly curated dataset (Val-1) of high-production content that we make public." (Sec. 1)
We release our datasets, retrieval queries and annotations publicly at https://github.com/AnokiAI/ContextIQ-Paper." (Sec. 1)

comparisons.answer: Compared against Google Vertex API, LanguageBind, TwelveLabs Marengo, CLIP, and One-Peace on MSR-VTT, Condensed Movies, and Val-1 datasets, showing better or comparable performance.
quotes: we show that combining modality-specific experts can yield better or comparable results with state-of-the-art joint multimodal models without the need for joint training, large-scale multimodal datasets, or significant computational resources." (Sec. 1)
The results show that our expert-based approach is better or comparable to these solutions (ref. Sec. 5.1)." (Sec. 1)
ContextIQ performs significantly better than the baselines, especially when we check the precision at higher k values." (Sec. 5.1)

hyperparameters.answer: Video segments are 15s, audio chunks are 5s, frames are sampled 1-in-10. YOLOv5 uses 0.45 IOU and 0.35 confidence thresholds. LLAMA 3 uses a temperature of 0.6.
quotes: We split the video into equal segments of 15 seconds each and sample one out of ten frames for embedding generation" (Sec. 4.1)
Since CLAP is trained on 5-second audio segments, we divide the audio into 5-second chunks for inference." (Sec. 4.1)
detect objects within videos with an IOU threshold of 0.45 and a confidence threshold of 0.35." (Sec. 4.2)
we use a pre-trained LLAMA 3 8B Instruct model [7] with a temperature of 0.6" (Sec. 4.2)

environment.answer: Experiments were run on four NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs.
quotes: we use four NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs to run all our experiments." (Sec. 4.1)

repository.answer: https://github.com/AnokiAI/ContextIQ-Paper
quotes: We release our datasets, retrieval queries and annotations publicly at https://github.com/AnokiAI/ContextIQ-Paper." (Sec. 1)

authors.answer: Authors are from Anoki Inc. and the University of Southern California.
quotes: Ashutosh Chaubey2∗†, Anoubhav Agarwaal1∗ , Sartaki Sinha Roy1∗ , Aayush Agrawal1∗ , Susmita Ghose1∗ 1Anoki Inc. 2University of Southern California" (Title Block)
