8) qa_interaction
Answer: Not mentioned.
Quotes: None.

9) retrieval_level
Answer: The system retrieves relevant video scenes or clips for contextual ad placement, storing the results in a scene-to-context lookup database.
Quotes:
- "ContextIQ's multimodal search (ref. Sec. 3.2) identifies scenes where creatives can be contextually served." (Sec. 6)
- "The selected scenes are stored in the scene-to-context lookup DB." (Sec. 6)

10) inferring_method
Answer: Roles and activities are inferred using a suite of expert models: VideoMAE2 for actions, YOLOv5 for objects, ResNet50 for places, and an ensemble of LLAMA 3 8B and BERT for hate speech.
Quotes:
- "We use a fine-tuned video masked autoencoder model (VideoMAE2) [67] on the Kinetics 710 dataset [14, 15, 35] for video action recognition." (Sec. 4.2)
- "For hate speech detection, we use a weighted ensemble of two models. First, we use a pre-trained LLAMA 3 8B Instruct model [7]... Secondly, we use a pre-trained BERT classifier [36] trained on the HateXplain dataset [49]" (Sec. 4.2)

11) video_representation
Answer: Video content is represented by separate embeddings for video (BLIP2), audio (CLAP), captions (MPNet), and a metadata sentence (MPNet), all stored in a multimodal database.
Quotes:
- "We extract embeddings from multiple modalities, including raw video, audio, captions (speech transcripts), and combined visual (objects, places, actions, emotion) and textual (NER, emotion, profanity, hate speech) metadata" (Sec. 3)
- "After inference with the task-specific models, we store the extracted information into a metadata sentence M." (Sec. 3.1.4)
- "These embeddings are then stored in a multimodal embeddings database." (Sec. 3)

12) model_pipeline
Answer: Input videos → Expert models extract features and metadata → Modality-specific encoders generate embeddings stored in a database → A text query is encoded, compared to embeddings, and scores are aggregated and filtered for brand safety to retrieve relevant video scenes.
Quotes:
- "Input videos are processed by the metadata extracting module, which uses expert models for extracting objects, actions, places, etc., and converts it into a metadata sentence . Four modality encoders then encode the video frames, audio, caption (transcripts) and metadata in parallel and store the embeddings in a multimodal embeddings DB." (Figure 1 Caption)
- "Text query t is first encoded by different text encoders and the multimodal embedding DB is searched to find similar videos. The aggregation module combines the results obtained from different modalities, and the final results are obtained after applying brand-safety filters" (Figure 2 Caption)

13) dataset
Answer: The system was evaluated on MSR-VTT, Condensed Movies, and a new *Val-1* dataset, with an internal *Val-2* dataset for ablations. Expert models used Objects 365, Places365, and Kinetics 710.
Quotes:
- "We evaluate the effectiveness of our ContextIQ system across several video retrieval benchmarks, such as MSR-VTT [], Condensed Movies [], and a newly curated dataset (Val-1) of high-production content that we make public." (Sec. 1)
- "We release our datasets, retrieval queries and annotations publicly at https://github.com/AnokiAI/ContextIQ-Paper." (Sec. 1)

14) comparisons
Answer: Compared against Google Vertex API, LanguageBind, TwelveLabs Marengo, CLIP, and One-Peace on MSR-VTT, Condensed Movies, and Val-1 datasets, showing better or comparable performance.
Quotes:
- "we show that combining modality-specific experts can yield better or comparable results with state-of-the-art joint multimodal models without the need for joint training, large-scale multimodal datasets, or significant computational resources." (Sec. 1)
- "The results show that our expert-based approach is better or comparable to these solutions (ref. Sec. 5.1)." (Sec. 1)
- "ContextIQ performs significantly better than the baselines, especially when we check the precision at higher k values." (Sec. 5.1)

15) hyperparameters
Answer: Video segments are 15s, audio chunks are 5s, frames are sampled 1-in-10. YOLOv5 uses 0.45 IOU and 0.35 confidence thresholds. LLAMA 3 uses a temperature of 0.6.
Quotes:
- "We split the video into equal segments of 15 seconds each and sample one out of ten frames for embedding generation" (Sec. 4.1)
- "Since CLAP is trained on 5-second audio segments, we divide the audio into 5-second chunks for inference." (Sec. 4.1)
- "detect objects within videos with an IOU threshold of 0.45 and a confidence threshold of 0.35." (Sec. 4.2)
- "we use a pre-trained LLAMA 3 8B Instruct model [7] with a temperature of 0.6" (Sec. 4.2)

16) environment
Answer: Experiments were run on four NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs.
Quotes:
- "we use four NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs to run all our experiments." (Sec. 4.1)

17) repository
Answer: https://github.com/AnokiAI/ContextIQ-Paper
Quotes:
- "We release our datasets, retrieval queries and annotations publicly at https://github.com/AnokiAI/ContextIQ-Paper." (Sec. 1)

18) authors
Answer: Authors are from Anoki Inc. and the University of Southern California.
Quotes:
- "Ashutosh Chaubey2∗†, Anoubhav Agarwaal1∗ , Sartaki Sinha Roy1∗ , Aayush Agrawal1∗ , Susmita Ghose1∗ 1Anoki Inc. 2University of Southern California" (Title Block)

paper_file: ECLIPSE Efficient Long-range Video Retrieval using Sight and Sound.md

video_segmentation.answer: Not mentioned. The method processes whole long-form videos for retrieval rather than pre-segmenting them into shots or scenes.

frame_sampling_method.answer: Uniformly sampled frames at 3 fps spanning the entire input video.
quotes: For video modality, we consider video clips X ∈ R T ×H×W×3 consisting of T RGB frames of size H ×W, sampled uniformly from the whole input video." (Section 3.1)
The visual frames are extracted at 3 fps." (Section 3.4)
For uniform sampling, we sample the frames uniformly throughout the entire input video." (Appendix C)

input_video_length.answer: The method targets long-range, minute-long videos, processing datasets with average lengths from 30 seconds to 8 minutes, with some videos potentially lasting hours.
quotes: ...our approach aims to retrieve minute-long videos that capture complex human actions." (Abstract)
...the majority of real-world videos often capture complex human actions, which may last several minutes or even hours." (Section 1)
The average video length is 180 seconds..." (Section 4.1, ActivityNet Captions)
The average video length is around 8 minutes." (Section 4.1, QVHighlights)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Yes, using a dual-pathway audiovisual attention block within a Transformer to incorporate long-range temporal audio cues into visual representations, followed by temporal pooling over frame-level CLS tokens.
quotes: ...incorporate long-range temporal cues from the audio stream into the visual representation..." (Section 1)
To efficiently incorporate temporal audio cues into static video frame representation, we use an audio-to-video (A2V) attention mechanism..." (Section 3.2)
Afterward, we perform temporal pooling over the CLS tokens across all video frames, to obtain the final audiovisual representation f ∈ R d ." (Section 3.2)

visual_analysis.answer: No, it is an audiovisual method; the visual backbone is a ViT-B/32 or ViT-B/16 initialized from CLIP.
quotes: We introduce an audiovisual method for long-range text-tovideo retrieval." (Abstract)
Our EclipSE follows CLIP4Clip [] setting where text encoder and visual encoder are initialized with CLIP weights []." (Section 3.4)
Unless otherwise noted, for all of our experiments, we use a ViT-B/32 with uniformly sampled 32-frame inputs spanning the whole input video." (Section 3.4)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned. The model processes general audio events which can include speech, but does not use specific speech recognition or speech embedding models.
quotes: ...textual queries include audio event descriptions, e.g., "a woman speaking to the camera", "a person playing the violin," etc." (Figure 6)
For audio encoder, we use ResNet-18 [] pre-trained on VGGSound []." (Section 3.4)

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Yes, using audio spectrograms from 10-second clips processed by an audio encoder like ResNet-18 (pre-trained on VGGSound), VGGish, or AST.
quotes: ...we propose to replace parts of the video with compact audio cues that succinctly summarize dynamic audio events and are cheap to process." (Abstract)
For audio encoder, we use ResNet-18 [] pre-trained on VGGSound []." (Section 3.4)
Specifically, we experiment with CNN-based audio encoders such as VGGish [] and VGGSound [], and also a transformer-based audio encoder AST []." (Section 5.3)

qa_interaction.answer: Not mentioned. The system is designed for text-to-video retrieval, not interactive Q&A.

retrieval_level.answer: The system performs paragraph-to-video retrieval, returning a ranked list of full videos to the user.
quotes: We introduce an audiovisual method for long-range text-tovideo retrieval." (Abstract)
We follow ] to evaluate paragraph-to-video retrieval, where we concatenate all the sentence descriptions to form a paragraph." (Section 4.1)
We use standard video retrieval evaluation metrics ] such as text-to-video R@1, R@5, R@10, and mean rank (MnR) to validate the effectiveness of our EclipSE model." (Section 4.2)

inferring_method.answer: Not mentioned. The system performs retrieval by matching text and audiovisual embeddings via dot product similarity within a learned joint space.
quotes: Specifically, we compute the similarity between text and video using a normalized dot product between the two embeddings f and g." (Section 3.3)
To train our model, we minimize the sum of the video-to-text and text-to-video matching losses []." (Section 3.3)

video_representation.answer: A final audiovisual embedding is created by processing visual patch embeddings and audio embeddings through an audiovisual Transformer, followed by temporal pooling of frame-level CLS tokens.
quotes: Afterward, we perform temporal pooling over the CLS tokens across all video frames, to obtain the final audiovisual representation f ∈ R d ." (Section 3.2)
The visual and audio embeddings are fed into our EclipSE audiovisual backbone, which injects relevant audio information to video and vice-versa." (Figure 3 caption)

model_pipeline.answer: Sparsely sampled frames and dense audio are encoded and fused by an audiovisual Transformer; the resulting embedding is matched against a text query embedding using a contrastive loss to retrieve videos.
quotes: Our video retrieval framework consists of three high-level components: (i) multimodal input embeddings, (ii) an audiovisual backbone for processing video and audio modalities, and (iii) a contrastive video-to-text matching objective." (Section 3)

Afterward, the audiovisual video segments are aggregated using temporal pooling, and the model is optimized by maximizing the similarity between audiovisual and textual embeddings using a contrastive loss function." (Figure 3 caption)

dataset.answer: ActivityNet Captions, QVHighlights, YouCook2, DiDeMo, and Charades were used for evaluation; the audio encoder was pretrained on VGGSound.
quotes: We evaluate EclipSE on five diverse long-range datasets: ActivityNet Captions [], QVHighlights [], DiDeMo [], YouCook2 [], and Charades []." (Section 4.1)
For audio encoder, we use ResNet-18 [] pre-trained on VGGSound []." (Section 3.4)

comparisons.answer: The primary baseline is CLIP4Clip, with comparisons to FiT and others on five datasets, showing improved R@1 accuracy (e.g., +1.6% over CLIP4Clip on ActivityNet) while being more computationally efficient.
quotes: ...we use a publicly available state-of-the-art CLIP4Clip [16] video retrieval system as our primary baseline." (Section 4.2)
Based on these results, we observe that EclipSE uses less GPU memory, runs 2.92× faster, and achieves better accuracy (42.3 vs. 41.7) than CLIP4Clip." (Section 5.1)
In Table 3, we show that a 32-frame EclipSE with a ViT-B/32 backbone outperforms prior methods on all four datasets." (Section 5.2)

hyperparameters.answer: Batch size 64, Adam optimizer, LR 1e-7, 32 uniformly sampled 224x224 frames, 10-second audio clips, and max text length of 64 or 128 tokens.
quotes: For a fair comparison with the baselines, we set the batch size to 64." (Section 3.4)
We train our model with Adam optimizer [] and set the learning rate to 1e − 7 for text encoder and spatial attention in Eq. 2." (Section 3.4)
...we use a ViT-B/32 with uniformly sampled 32-frame inputs spanning the whole input video." (Section 3.4)
We sample 10-second audio clips in the neighborhood around the sampled video frame..." (Section 3.4)
For visual representations, we first "patchify" 224 × 224 video frames..." (Appendix B)

environment.answer: Training was conducted on four NVIDIA A6000 GPUs.
quotes: We implement EclipSE using Pytorch [] and conduct the training on four NVIDIA A6000 GPUs." (Section 3.4)

repository.answer: A code repository link is provided in the abstract.
quotes: Our code is available at" (Abstract)

authors.answer: Yan-Bo Lin, Jie Lei, Mohit Bansal, and Gedas Bertasius from the Department of Computer Science, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill; the venue and year are not mentioned.
quotes: Yan-Bo Lin, Jie Lei, Mohit Bansal, and Gedas Bertasius" (Title section)
Department of Computer Science University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill {yblin,jielei,mbansal,gedas}@cs.unc.edu" (Title section)

raw_output:
1) video_segmentation
Answer: Not mentioned. The method processes whole long-form videos for retrieval rather than pre-segmenting them into shots or scenes.
Quotes: None.

2) frame_sampling_method
Answer: Uniformly sampled frames at 3 fps spanning the entire input video.
Quotes:
- "For video modality, we consider video clips X ∈ R T ×H×W×3 consisting of T RGB frames of size H ×W, sampled uniformly from the whole input video." (Section 3.1)
- "The visual frames are extracted at 3 fps." (Section 3.4)
- "For uniform sampling, we sample the frames uniformly throughout the entire input video." (Appendix C)

3) input_video_length
Answer: The method targets long-range, minute-long videos, processing datasets with average lengths from 30 seconds to 8 minutes, with some videos potentially lasting hours.
Quotes:
- "...our approach aims to retrieve minute-long videos that capture complex human actions." (Abstract)
- "...the majority of real-world videos often capture complex human actions, which may last several minutes or even hours." (Section 1)
- "The average video length is 180 seconds..." (Section 4.1, ActivityNet Captions)
- "The average video length is around 8 minutes." (Section 4.1, QVHighlights)

4) spatiotemporal_analysis
Answer: Yes, using a dual-pathway audiovisual attention block within a Transformer to incorporate long-range temporal audio cues into visual representations, followed by temporal pooling over frame-level CLS tokens.
Quotes:
- "...incorporate long-range temporal cues from the audio stream into the visual representation..." (Section 1)
- "To efficiently incorporate temporal audio cues into static video frame representation, we use an audio-to-video (A2V) attention mechanism..." (Section 3.2)
- "Afterward, we perform temporal pooling over the CLS tokens across all video frames, to obtain the final audiovisual representation f ∈ R d ." (Section 3.2)

5) visual_analysis
Answer: No, it is an audiovisual method; the visual backbone is a ViT-B/32 or ViT-B/16 initialized from CLIP.
Quotes:
- "We introduce an audiovisual method for long-range text-tovideo retrieval." (Abstract)
- "Our EclipSE follows CLIP4Clip [] setting where text encoder and visual encoder are initialized with CLIP weights []." (Section 3.4)
- "Unless otherwise noted, for all of our experiments, we use a ViT-B/32 with uniformly sampled 32-frame inputs spanning the whole input video." (Section 3.4)

6) speech_audio_analysis
Answer: Not mentioned. The model processes general audio events which can include speech, but does not use specific speech recognition or speech embedding models.
Quotes:
- "...textual queries include audio event descriptions, e.g., "a woman speaking to the camera", "a person playing the violin," etc." (Figure 6)
- "For audio encoder, we use ResNet-18 [] pre-trained on VGGSound []." (Section 3.4)

7) sound_audio_analysis
Answer: Yes, using audio spectrograms from 10-second clips processed by an audio encoder like ResNet-18 (pre-trained on VGGSound), VGGish, or AST.
Quotes:
- "...we propose to replace parts of the video with compact audio cues that succinctly summarize dynamic audio events and are cheap to process." (Abstract)
- "For audio encoder, we use ResNet-18 [] pre-trained on VGGSound []." (Section 3.4)
- "Specifically, we experiment with CNN-based audio encoders such as VGGish [] and VGGSound [], and also a transformer-based audio encoder AST []." (Section 5.3)

8) qa_interaction
Answer: Not mentioned. The system is designed for text-to-video retrieval, not interactive Q&A.
Quotes: None.

9) retrieval_level
Answer: The system performs paragraph-to-video retrieval, returning a ranked list of full videos to the user.
Quotes:
- "We introduce an audiovisual method for long-range text-tovideo retrieval." (Abstract)
- "We follow ] to evaluate paragraph-to-video retrieval, where we concatenate all the sentence descriptions to form a paragraph." (Section 4.1)
- "We use standard video retrieval evaluation metrics ] such as text-to-video R@1, R@5, R@10, and mean rank (MnR) to validate the effectiveness of our EclipSE model." (Section 4.2)

10) inferring_method
Answer: Not mentioned. The system performs retrieval by matching text and audiovisual embeddings via dot product similarity within a learned joint space.
Quotes:
- "Specifically, we compute the similarity between text and video using a normalized dot product between the two embeddings f and g." (Section 3.3)
- "To train our model, we minimize the sum of the video-to-text and text-to-video matching losses []." (Section 3.3)

11) video_representation
Answer: A final audiovisual embedding is created by processing visual patch embeddings and audio embeddings through an audiovisual Transformer, followed by temporal pooling of frame-level CLS tokens.
Quotes:
- "Afterward, we perform temporal pooling over the CLS tokens across all video frames, to obtain the final audiovisual representation f ∈ R d ." (Section 3.2)
- "The visual and audio embeddings are fed into our EclipSE audiovisual backbone, which injects relevant audio information to video and vice-versa." (Figure 3 caption)

12) model_pipeline
Answer: Sparsely sampled frames and dense audio are encoded and fused by an audiovisual Transformer; the resulting embedding is matched against a text query embedding using a contrastive loss to retrieve videos.
Quotes:
- "Our video retrieval framework consists of three high-level components: (i) multimodal input embeddings, (ii) an audiovisual backbone for processing video and audio modalities, and (iii) a contrastive video-to-text matching objective." (Section 3)
- "Afterward, the audiovisual video segments are aggregated using temporal pooling, and the model is optimized by maximizing the similarity between audiovisual and textual embeddings using a contrastive loss function." (Figure 3 caption)

13) dataset
Answer: ActivityNet Captions, QVHighlights, YouCook2, DiDeMo, and Charades were used for evaluation; the audio encoder was pretrained on VGGSound.
Quotes:
- "We evaluate EclipSE on five diverse long-range datasets: ActivityNet Captions [], QVHighlights [], DiDeMo [], YouCook2 [], and Charades []." (Section 4.1)
- "For audio encoder, we use ResNet-18 [] pre-trained on VGGSound []." (Section 3.4)

14) comparisons
Answer: The primary baseline is CLIP4Clip, with comparisons to FiT and others on five datasets, showing improved R@1 accuracy (e.g., +1.6% over CLIP4Clip on ActivityNet) while being more computationally efficient.
Quotes:
- "...we use a publicly available state-of-the-art CLIP4Clip [16] video retrieval system as our primary baseline." (Section 4.2)
- "Based on these results, we observe that EclipSE uses less GPU memory, runs 2.92× faster, and achieves better accuracy (42.3 vs. 41.7) than CLIP4Clip." (Section 5.1)
- "In Table 3, we show that a 32-frame EclipSE with a ViT-B/32 backbone outperforms prior methods on all four datasets." (Section 5.2)

15) hyperparameters
Answer: Batch size 64, Adam optimizer, LR 1e-7, 32 uniformly sampled 224x224 frames, 10-second audio clips, and max text length of 64 or 128 tokens.
Quotes:
- "For a fair comparison with the baselines, we set the batch size to 64." (Section 3.4)
- "We train our model with Adam optimizer [] and set the learning rate to 1e − 7 for text encoder and spatial attention in Eq. 2." (Section 3.4)
- "...we use a ViT-B/32 with uniformly sampled 32-frame inputs spanning the whole input video." (Section 3.4)
- "We sample 10-second audio clips in the neighborhood around the sampled video frame..." (Section 3.4)
- "For visual representations, we first "patchify" 224 × 224 video frames..." (Appendix B)

16) environment
Answer: Training was conducted on four NVIDIA A6000 GPUs.
Quotes:
- "We implement EclipSE using Pytorch [] and conduct the training on four NVIDIA A6000 GPUs." (Section 3.4)

17) repository
Answer: A code repository link is provided in the abstract.
Quotes:
- "Our code is available at" (Abstract)

18) authors
Answer: Yan-Bo Lin, Jie Lei, Mohit Bansal, and Gedas Bertasius from the Department of Computer Science, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill; the venue and year are not mentioned.
Quotes:
- "Yan-Bo Lin, Jie Lei, Mohit Bansal, and Gedas Bertasius" (Title section)
- "Department of Computer Science University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill {yblin,jielei,mbansal,gedas}@cs.unc.edu" (Title section)

paper_file: End-to-End Audio Visual Scene-Aware Dialog using Multimodal Attention-Based Video Features.md

video_segmentation.answer: Not mentioned. The paper uses pre-existing video clip datasets like Charades, which is described as untrimmed, without detailing any new segmentation process.
quotes: We collected text-based conversations data about short videos for Audio Visual Scene-Aware Dialog (AVSD) as described in [] using from an existing video description dataset, Charades []." (Section 2)
Charades is an untrimmed and multi-action dataset, containing 11,848 videos split into 7985 for training, 1863 for validation, and 2,000 for testing." (Section 2)
We evaluated our proposed feature fusion using the MSVD (YouTube2Text) [], MSR-VTT [], and Charades [] video data sets." (Section 4.1)

frame_sampling_method.answer: Features are extracted from sequential frames; the C3D network processes every 16 frames, while the I3D model processes a stack of 16 video frames or 16 optical flow frames.
quotes: The C3D network reads sequential frames in the video and outputs a fixed-length feature vector every 16 frames." (Section 4.2)

In the experiments in this paper, we treated I3D-rgb (I3D features computed on a stack of 16 video frame images) and I3D-flow (I3D features computed on a stack of 16 frames of optical flow fields) as two separate modalities that are input to our multimodal attention model." (Section 4.2)

input_video_length.answer: The system is designed for short video clips from datasets like Charades, MSVD, and MSR-VTT; specific durations are not provided, and no videos are mentioned to be ≥ 60 minutes.
quotes: We collected text-based conversations data about short videos for Audio Visual Scene-Aware Dialog (AVSD)..." (Section 2)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Yes, spatiotemporal analysis is performed using C3D and I3D models to extract features for motion and short-term activity, which are then integrated via a multimodal attention mechanism.
quotes: The pretrained C3D [] model is used to generate features for model motion and short-term spatiotemporal activity." (Section 4.2)
In addition to the VGG-16 and C3D features, we also adopted the state-of-the-art I3D features [], spatiotemporal features that were developed for action recognition." (Section 4.2)
The I3D model inflates the 2D filters and pooling kernels in the Inception V3 network along their temporal dimension, building 3D spatiotemporal ones." (Section 4.2)

visual_analysis.answer: The system uses both visual-only and multimodal analysis, with visual backbones including VGG-16 for static image features and C3D and I3D (rgb-flow) for spatiotemporal features.
quotes: We used a sequence of 4096-dimensional feature vectors of the output from the fully-connected fc7 layer of a VGG-16 network pretrained on the ImageNet dataset for the image features." (Section 4.2)
The pretrained C3D [] model is used to generate features for model motion and short-term spatiotemporal activity." (Section 4.2)
...we also adopted the state-of-the-art I3D features [], spatiotemporal features that were developed for action recognition." (Section 4.2)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned. The paper analyzes non-speech audio but does not perform automatic speech recognition (ASR) or analyze speech content from the video audio tracks.

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Yes, non-speech audio is analyzed using MFCC features, SoundNet, and Audio Set VGGish to extract embeddings for sounds like music and background noise.
quotes: ...we used two different types of audio features: concatenated mel-frequency cepstral coefficient (MFCC) features [], and SoundNet [] features []. In this paper, we also evaluate features extracted using a new state-of-the-art model, Audio Set VGGish []." (Section 4.3)
Ground truth: there is audio , i can hear music and background noise ." (Section 5.2)

qa_interaction.answer: The system is an end-to-end dialog model that generates answers to questions based on a video and dialog history; it is not an interactive chat system and does not use LLMs.
quotes: We built an end-to-end dialog system that can generate answers in response to user questions about events in a video sequence." (Section 3)

The question, visual features, and the dialog history are fed into corresponding LSTM-based encoders to build up a context embedding, and then the outputs of the encoders are fed into a LSTM-based decoder to generate an answer." (Section 3)

retrieval_level.answer: Not mentioned. The system is a generative model that produces text answers based on a given video and dialog history, rather than retrieving videos, clips, or timestamps.

inferring_method.answer: Inferences are made by an end-to-end LSTM-based encoder-decoder model with a multimodal attention mechanism that directly generates a natural language response.
quotes: ...the outputs of the encoders are fed into a LSTM-based decoder to generate an answer." (Section 3)
The approach can pay attention to specific modalities of input based on the current state of the decoder to predict the word sequence in video description." (Section 3.2)

video_representation.answer: Videos are represented as sequences of embedding vectors extracted from pretrained models, including VGG-16, C3D, and I3D for visual content, and VGGish, SoundNet, and MFCCs for audio content.
quotes: We used a sequence of 4096-dimensional feature vectors of the output from the fully-connected fc7 layer of a VGG-16 network..." (Section 4.2)
4096-dimensional features of activation vectors from fully-connected fc6-1 layer was applied to spatiotemporal features." (Section 4.2)
...the Audio Set VGGish model operates on 0.96 s log Mel spectrogram patches extracted from 16 kHz audio, and outputs a 128-dimensional embedding vector." (Section 4.3)

model_pipeline.answer: Video features, a question, and dialog history are input to LSTM encoders and a multimodal attention module, whose combined context is fed to an LSTM decoder to generate a text answer.
quotes: The question, visual features, and the dialog history are fed into corresponding LSTM-based encoders to build up a context embedding, and then the outputs of the encoders are fed into a LSTM-based decoder to generate an answer." (Section 3)
Figure 1: Our multimodal-attention based video scene-aware dialog system" (Figure 1)

dataset.answer: A new Audio Visual Scene-Aware Dialog (AVSD) dataset was created using videos from the Charades dataset; MSVD (YouTube2Text) and MSR-VTT were also used for video description experiments.
quotes: We collected text-based conversations data about short videos for Audio Visual Scene-Aware Dialog (AVSD) as described in [] using from an existing video description dataset, Charades [], for Dialog System Technology Challenge the 7th edition (DSTC7)1 ." (Section 2)
We evaluated our proposed feature fusion using the MSVD (YouTube2Text) [], MSR-VTT [], and Charades [] video data sets." (Section 4.1)

comparisons.answer: The primary model (QA + I3D + VGGish with attention) is compared to baselines using only QA history, QA+Captions, QA+VGG16, and QA+I3D on the AVSD dataset, showing improved BLEU, METEOR, ROUGE_L, and CIDEr scores.
quotes: Table 6: System response generation evaluation results with objective measures." (Table 6)
First we evaluated response generation quality with only QA features as a baseline without any video scene features." (Section 5.2)
Finally, we examined the efficacy of audio features. The table shows that VGGish obviously contributed to increasing the response quality especially when using the attentional fusion." (Section 5.2)

hyperparameters.answer: Optimizer: ADAM; Epochs: up to 20; LSTM layers: 2; LSTM cells: 128; Video feature projection: 256 dimensions; Word embedding: 256 dimensions.
quotes: We employed an ADAM optimizer [] with the cross-entropy criterion and iterated the training process up to 20 epochs." (Section 5.1)

We used the parameters of the LSTMs with #layer=2 and #cells=128 for encoding history and question sentences. Video features were projected to 256 dimensional feature space before modality fusion. The decoder LSTM had a structure of #layer=2 and #cells=128 as well." (Section 5.1)
Each word is embedded to a 256-dimensional vector when it is fed to the LSTM layer." (Section 4.4)

environment.answer: Not mentioned.

repository.answer: The dataset, model code, and pretrained models are stated to be publicly available for a new Video Scene-Aware Dialog challenge, with a link provided in a footnote.
quotes: Our dataset, model code and pretrained models will be publicly available for a new Video Scene-Aware Dialog challenge." (Abstract)
1 http://workshop.colips.org/dstc7/call.html" (Footnote 1, Section 2)

authors.answer: The authors are from Mitsubishi Electric Research Laboratories (MERL) and the School of Interactive Computing, Georgia Tech; the paper is associated with the Dialog System Technology Challenge the 7th edition (DSTC7).
quotes: Chiori Hori†, Huda Alamri*†, Jue Wang†, Gordon Wichern†, Takaaki Hori†, Anoop Cherian†, Tim K. Marks†, Vincent Cartillier*, Raphael Gontijo Lopes*, Abhishek Das*, Irfan Essa*, Dhruv Batra* Devi Parikh*, †Mitsubishi Electric Research Laboratories (MERL), Cambridge, MA, USA *School of Interactive Computing, Georgia Tech" (Author Block)

raw_output:
1) video_segmentation
Answer: Not mentioned. The paper uses pre-existing video clip datasets like Charades, which is described as untrimmed, without detailing any new segmentation process.
Quotes:
- "We collected text-based conversations data about short videos for Audio Visual Scene-Aware Dialog (AVSD) as described in [] using from an existing video description dataset, Charades []." (Section 2)
- "Charades is an untrimmed and multi-action dataset, containing 11,848 videos split into 7985 for training, 1863 for validation, and 2,000 for testing." (Section 2)
- "We evaluated our proposed feature fusion using the MSVD (YouTube2Text) [], MSR-VTT [], and Charades [] video data sets." (Section 4.1)

2) frame_sampling_method
Answer: Features are extracted from sequential frames; the C3D network processes every 16 frames, while the I3D model processes a stack of 16 video frames or 16 optical flow frames.
Quotes:
- "The C3D network reads sequential frames in the video and outputs a fixed-length feature vector every 16 frames." (Section 4.2)
- "In the experiments in this paper, we treated I3D-rgb (I3D features computed on a stack of 16 video frame images) and I3D-flow (I3D features computed on a stack of 16 frames of optical flow fields) as two separate modalities that are input to our multimodal attention model." (Section 4.2)

3) input_video_length
Answer: The system is designed for short video clips from datasets like Charades, MSVD, and MSR-VTT; specific durations are not provided, and no videos are mentioned to be ≥ 60 minutes.
Quotes:
- "We collected text-based conversations data about short videos for Audio Visual Scene-Aware Dialog (AVSD)..." (Section 2)

4) spatiotemporal_analysis
Answer: Yes, spatiotemporal analysis is performed using C3D and I3D models to extract features for motion and short-term activity, which are then integrated via a multimodal attention mechanism.
Quotes:
- "The pretrained C3D [] model is used to generate features for model motion and short-term spatiotemporal activity." (Section 4.2)
- "In addition to the VGG-16 and C3D features, we also adopted the state-of-the-art I3D features [], spatiotemporal features that were developed for action recognition." (Section 4.2)
- "The I3D model inflates the 2D filters and pooling kernels in the Inception V3 network along their temporal dimension, building 3D spatiotemporal ones." (Section 4.2)

5) visual_analysis
Answer: The system uses both visual-only and multimodal analysis, with visual backbones including VGG-16 for static image features and C3D and I3D (rgb-flow) for spatiotemporal features.
Quotes:
- "We used a sequence of 4096-dimensional feature vectors of the output from the fully-connected fc7 layer of a VGG-16 network pretrained on the ImageNet dataset for the image features." (Section 4.2)
- "The pretrained C3D [] model is used to generate features for model motion and short-term spatiotemporal activity." (Section 4.2)
- "...we also adopted the state-of-the-art I3D features [], spatiotemporal features that were developed for action recognition." (Section 4.2)

6) speech_audio_analysis
Answer: Not mentioned. The paper analyzes non-speech audio but does not perform automatic speech recognition (ASR) or analyze speech content from the video audio tracks.
Quotes: None.

7) sound_audio_analysis
Answer: Yes, non-speech audio is analyzed using MFCC features, SoundNet, and Audio Set VGGish to extract embeddings for sounds like music and background noise.
Quotes:
- "...we used two different types of audio features: concatenated mel-frequency cepstral coefficient (MFCC) features [], and SoundNet [] features []. In this paper, we also evaluate features extracted using a new state-of-the-art model, Audio Set VGGish []." (Section 4.3)
- "Ground truth: there is audio , i can hear music and background noise ." (Section 5.2)

8) qa_interaction
Answer: The system is an end-to-end dialog model that generates answers to questions based on a video and dialog history; it is not an interactive chat system and does not use LLMs.
Quotes:
- "We built an end-to-end dialog system that can generate answers in response to user questions about events in a video sequence." (Section 3)
- "The question, visual features, and the dialog history are fed into corresponding LSTM-based encoders to build up a context embedding, and then the outputs of the encoders are fed into a LSTM-based decoder to generate an answer." (Section 3)

9) retrieval_level
Answer: Not mentioned. The system is a generative model that produces text answers based on a given video and dialog history, rather than retrieving videos, clips, or timestamps.
Quotes: None.

10) inferring_method
Answer: Inferences are made by an end-to-end LSTM-based encoder-decoder model with a multimodal attention mechanism that directly generates a natural language response.
Quotes:
- "...the outputs of the encoders are fed into a LSTM-based decoder to generate an answer." (Section 3)
- "The approach can pay attention to specific modalities of input based on the current state of the decoder to predict the word sequence in video description." (Section 3.2)

11) video_representation
Answer: Videos are represented as sequences of embedding vectors extracted from pretrained models, including VGG-16, C3D, and I3D for visual content, and VGGish, SoundNet, and MFCCs for audio content.
Quotes:
- "We used a sequence of 4096-dimensional feature vectors of the output from the fully-connected fc7 layer of a VGG-16 network..." (Section 4.2)
- "4096-dimensional features of activation vectors from fully-connected fc6-1 layer was applied to spatiotemporal features." (Section 4.2)
- "...the Audio Set VGGish model operates on 0.96 s log Mel spectrogram patches extracted from 16 kHz audio, and outputs a 128-dimensional embedding vector." (Section 4.3)

12) model_pipeline
Answer: Video features, a question, and dialog history are input to LSTM encoders and a multimodal attention module, whose combined context is fed to an LSTM decoder to generate a text answer.
Quotes:
- "The question, visual features, and the dialog history are fed into corresponding LSTM-based encoders to build up a context embedding, and then the outputs of the encoders are fed into a LSTM-based decoder to generate an answer." (Section 3)
- "Figure 1: Our multimodal-attention based video scene-aware dialog system" (Figure 1)

13) dataset
Answer: A new Audio Visual Scene-Aware Dialog (AVSD) dataset was created using videos from the Charades dataset; MSVD (YouTube2Text) and MSR-VTT were also used for video description experiments.
Quotes:
- "We collected text-based conversations data about short videos for Audio Visual Scene-Aware Dialog (AVSD) as described in [] using from an existing video description dataset, Charades [], for Dialog System Technology Challenge the 7th edition (DSTC7)1 ." (Section 2)
- "We evaluated our proposed feature fusion using the MSVD (YouTube2Text) [], MSR-VTT [], and Charades [] video data sets." (Section 4.1)

14) comparisons
Answer: The primary model (QA + I3D + VGGish with attention) is compared to baselines using only QA history, QA+Captions, QA+VGG16, and QA+I3D on the AVSD dataset, showing improved BLEU, METEOR, ROUGE_L, and CIDEr scores.
Quotes:
- "Table 6: System response generation evaluation results with objective measures." (Table 6)
- "First we evaluated response generation quality with only QA features as a baseline without any video scene features." (Section 5.2)
- "Finally, we examined the efficacy of audio features. The table shows that VGGish obviously contributed to increasing the response quality especially when using the attentional fusion." (Section 5.2)

15) hyperparameters
Answer: Optimizer: ADAM; Epochs: up to 20; LSTM layers: 2; LSTM cells: 128; Video feature projection: 256 dimensions; Word embedding: 256 dimensions.
Quotes:
- "We employed an ADAM optimizer [] with the cross-entropy criterion and iterated the training process up to 20 epochs." (Section 5.1)
- "We used the parameters of the LSTMs with #layer=2 and #cells=128 for encoding history and question sentences. Video features were projected to 256 dimensional feature space before modality fusion. The decoder LSTM had a structure of #layer=2 and #cells=128 as well." (Section 5.1)
- "Each word is embedded to a 256-dimensional vector when it is fed to the LSTM layer." (Section 4.4)

16) environment
Answer: Not mentioned.
Quotes: None.

17) repository
Answer: The dataset, model code, and pretrained models are stated to be publicly available for a new Video Scene-Aware Dialog challenge, with a link provided in a footnote.
Quotes:
- "Our dataset, model code and pretrained models will be publicly available for a new Video Scene-Aware Dialog challenge." (Abstract)
- "1 http://workshop.colips.org/dstc7/call.html" (Footnote 1, Section 2)

18) authors
Answer: The authors are from Mitsubishi Electric Research Laboratories (MERL) and the School of Interactive Computing, Georgia Tech; the paper is associated with the Dialog System Technology Challenge the 7th edition (DSTC7).
Quotes:
- "Chiori Hori†, Huda Alamri*†, Jue Wang†, Gordon Wichern†, Takaaki Hori†, Anoop Cherian†, Tim K. Marks†, Vincent Cartillier*, Raphael Gontijo Lopes*, Abhishek Das*, Irfan Essa*, Dhruv Batra* Devi Parikh*, †Mitsubishi Electric Research Laboratories (MERL), Cambridge, MA, USA *School of Interactive Computing, Georgia Tech" (Author Block)

paper_file: Florence - A New Foundation Model for Computer Vision.md

video_segmentation.answer: Not mentioned.

frame_sampling_method.answer: Not mentioned.

input_video_length.answer: Not mentioned.

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Yes, a Video CoSwin adapter uses 3D convolutional layers and 3D shifted local windows in self-attention layers to model video dynamics.
quotes: Our Video CoSwin adapter can borrow the image encoder from CoSwin for the video domain with minimum changes..." (Section 2.6)
...video CoSwin replaces the tokenization layer of CoSwin (in Section 2.3) from 2D convolutional layers to 3D convolutional layers, which converts each 3D tube into one token." (Section 2.6)
Third, we follow prior work to replace the 2D shifted window design with 3D shifted local windows in self-attention layers." (Section 2.6)

visual_analysis.answer: Yes, the model is used for visual-only tasks like classification and object detection using a CoSwin Transformer, a modified Swin Transformer, as the visual backbone.
quotes: For the image encoder, we chose hierarchical Vision Transformers (e.g. , Swin , CvT , Vision Longformer , Focal Transformer , and CSwin )." (Section 1. Introduction)

Our Florence pretrained model uses a two-tower architecture: a 12-layer transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) as language encoder, similar to CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), and a hierarchical Vision Transformer as the image encoder." (Section 2.3)
The hierarchical Vision Transformer is a modified Swin Transformer (Liu et al., 2021a) with convolutional embedding, called CoSwin Transformer." (Section 2.3)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned.

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned.

qa_interaction.answer: No, the model performs static Visual Question Answering (VQA) by treating it as a classification task over a fixed set of answers, not as an interactive chat system.
quotes: To evaluate the performance, we fine-tune the pre-trained model on the challenging VQA task, which is to answer a question based on the image context." (Section 3.7)
As a common practice, the problem is cast as a classification task where each class corresponds to an answer." (Section 3.7)
The final pooling representations are fed into a randomly-initialized multilayer perceptron (MLP) network to predict the answer over 3, 129 answers." (Section 3.7)

retrieval_level.answer: The system retrieves full videos or images based on text queries, or vice-versa, for end-user tasks.
quotes: Table 5 presents the zero-shot transfer and fine-tuning performance of Florence for both text and image retrieval on the Flickr30k (Plummer et al., 2016) and MSCOCO (Lin et al., 2015) datasets." (Section 3.5)
Although Florence is pre-trained on image-text pairs, it can be easily adapted to video tasks (shown in Section 2.6), such as text-video retrieval." (Section 3.8)

inferring_method.answer: Inference is performed via standard forward pass of deep learning models, using a Dynamic Head adapter for object detection and classification heads for recognition tasks.
quotes: For this goal, we add an adaptor Dynamic Head (Dai et al., 2021a) (or Dynamic DETR (Dai et al., 2021b)), a unified attention mechanism for the detection head, to the pretrained image encoder (i.e., CoSwin)." (Section 2.4)
To predict the class, we append a single linear layer as an adapter head to our image encoder CoSwin." (Section 3.4)
The final pooling representations are fed into a randomly-initialized multilayer perceptron (MLP) network to predict the answer over 3, 129 answers." (Section 3.7)

video_representation.answer: Video content is represented using a Video CoSwin adapter, which extends the CoSwin image encoder with 3D convolutions and 3D shifted local attention windows.
quotes: We expand the input 2D patch embeddings and positional embeddings to 3D so that the encoder can process video inputs, following." (Section 3.8)
...video CoSwin replaces the tokenization layer of CoSwin (in Section 2.3) from 2D convolutional layers to 3D convolutional layers, which converts each 3D tube into one token." (Section 2.6)
...we follow prior work to replace the 2D shifted window design with 3D shifted local windows in self-attention layers." (Section 2.6)

model_pipeline.answer: Image/Text/Video inputs are processed by a two-tower architecture (CoSwin image encoder, Transformer language encoder) with task-specific adapters (Dynamic Head, METER, Video CoSwin) to produce outputs for classification, detection, retrieval, VQA, and action recognition.
quotes: Overview of building Florence. Our workflow consists of data curation, unified learning, Transformer architectures and adaption. It shows the foundation model can be adapted to various downstream tasks..." (Figure 2)

We extended the learned feature representation along space (from scene to objects) using the dynamic head adapter , time (from static image to videos) via proposed video CoSwin adapter, and modality (from images to language) via METER adapter." (Section 1. Introduction)

dataset.answer: A new 900M image-text pair dataset (FLD-900M) and a 9M object detection dataset (FLOD-9M) were created; evaluation used ImageNet-1K, COCO, Kinetics-400/600, VQA, MSR-VTT, Flickr30K, and others.
quotes: Specifically, we construct a 900 million image-text-pair dataset, called FLD-900M (FLD stands for FLorenceDataset)..." (Section 2.1)
We have constructed a large-scale object detection dataset, called FLOD-9M (for FLorence Object detection Dataset), for object detection pre-training." (Section 2.4)

Florence achieves new state-of-the-art results in majority of 44 representative benchmarks, e.g. ImageNet-1K zero-shot classification..., 62.4 mAP on COCO fine tuning, 80.36 on VQA, and 87.8 on Kinetics-600." (Abstract)

comparisons.answer: Florence was compared against baselines like CLIP and ALIGN, achieving state-of-the-art on ImageNet-1K zero-shot (83.74% top-1 accuracy), COCO detection (62.4 mAP), VQA (80.36), and Kinetics-600 (87.8% top-1 accuracy).
quotes: Florence outperforms on 9/12 tasks compared with stateof-the-art methods. We achieved a remarkable improvement in the zero-shot transfer on ImageNet-1K – the top-1 accuracy of 83.74% (+5.6% over SOTA result), and the top-5 accuracy of 97.18%." (Section 3.1)
COCO test-Dev ... Florence 62.4" (Table 6)
Compare our model with the existing state-of-the-art methods on VQA. ... Florence 80.16 | 80.36" (Table 8)
Comparison to state-of-the-art methods... on Kinetics-400 and Kinetics-600. ... Florence | FLD-900M | 86.5 | 97.3 | 87.8 | 97.8" (Table 10)

hyperparameters.answer: Pre-training used a batch size of 24,576, image resolutions of 224x224 and 384x384, and a maximum language description length of 76.
quotes: The image size is 224 × 224 and the maximum language description length is truncated at 76. The batch size is 24,576." (Section 2.2)
We further trained 80K iterations at a higher resolution of 384 × 384 to boost the performance, which follows existing pre-training approaches." (Section 2.2)

environment.answer: The model was trained for 10 days on 512 NVIDIA-A100 GPUs with 40GB memory each.
quotes: The model takes 10 days to train on 512 NVIDIA-A100 GPUs with 40GB memory per GPU." (Section 2.3)
Thanks to these above optimizations, we can achieve consistent improvement in reducing GPU memory for variable batch sizes on various numbers of NVIDIA-A100s, shown in Figure 5." (Section 2.7)

raw_output:
1) video_segmentation
Answer: Not mentioned.
Quotes: None.

2) frame_sampling_method
Answer: Not mentioned.
Quotes: None.

3) input_video_length
Answer: Not mentioned.
Quotes: None.

4) spatiotemporal_analysis
Answer: Yes, a Video CoSwin adapter uses 3D convolutional layers and 3D shifted local windows in self-attention layers to model video dynamics.
Quotes:
- "Our Video CoSwin adapter can borrow the image encoder from CoSwin for the video domain with minimum changes..." (Section 2.6)
- "...video CoSwin replaces the tokenization layer of CoSwin (in Section 2.3) from 2D convolutional layers to 3D convolutional layers, which converts each 3D tube into one token." (Section 2.6)
- "Third, we follow prior work to replace the 2D shifted window design with 3D shifted local windows in self-attention layers." (Section 2.6)

5) visual_analysis
Answer: Yes, the model is used for visual-only tasks like classification and object detection using a CoSwin Transformer, a modified Swin Transformer, as the visual backbone.
Quotes:
- "For the image encoder, we chose hierarchical Vision Transformers (e.g. , Swin , CvT , Vision Longformer , Focal Transformer , and CSwin )." (Section 1. Introduction)
- "Our Florence pretrained model uses a two-tower architecture: a 12-layer transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) as language encoder, similar to CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), and a hierarchical Vision Transformer as the image encoder." (Section 2.3)
- "The hierarchical Vision Transformer is a modified Swin Transformer (Liu et al., 2021a) with convolutional embedding, called CoSwin Transformer." (Section 2.3)

6) speech_audio_analysis
Answer: Not mentioned.
Quotes: None.

7) sound_audio_analysis
Answer: Not mentioned.
Quotes: None.

8) qa_interaction
Answer: No, the model performs static Visual Question Answering (VQA) by treating it as a classification task over a fixed set of answers, not as an interactive chat system.
Quotes:
- "To evaluate the performance, we fine-tune the pre-trained model on the challenging VQA task, which is to answer a question based on the image context." (Section 3.7)
- "As a common practice, the problem is cast as a classification task where each class corresponds to an answer." (Section 3.7)
- "The final pooling representations are fed into a randomly-initialized multilayer perceptron (MLP) network to predict the answer over 3, 129 answers." (Section 3.7)

9) retrieval_level
Answer: The system retrieves full videos or images based on text queries, or vice-versa, for end-user tasks.
Quotes:
- "Table 5 presents the zero-shot transfer and fine-tuning performance of Florence for both text and image retrieval on the Flickr30k (Plummer et al., 2016) and MSCOCO (Lin et al., 2015) datasets." (Section 3.5)
- "Although Florence is pre-trained on image-text pairs, it can be easily adapted to video tasks (shown in Section 2.6), such as text-video retrieval." (Section 3.8)

10) inferring_method
Answer: Inference is performed via standard forward pass of deep learning models, using a Dynamic Head adapter for object detection and classification heads for recognition tasks.
Quotes:
- "For this goal, we add an adaptor Dynamic Head (Dai et al., 2021a) (or Dynamic DETR (Dai et al., 2021b)), a unified attention mechanism for the detection head, to the pretrained image encoder (i.e., CoSwin)." (Section 2.4)
- "To predict the class, we append a single linear layer as an adapter head to our image encoder CoSwin." (Section 3.4)
- "The final pooling representations are fed into a randomly-initialized multilayer perceptron (MLP) network to predict the answer over 3, 129 answers." (Section 3.7)

11) video_representation
Answer: Video content is represented using a Video CoSwin adapter, which extends the CoSwin image encoder with 3D convolutions and 3D shifted local attention windows.
Quotes:
- "We expand the input 2D patch embeddings and positional embeddings to 3D so that the encoder can process video inputs, following." (Section 3.8)
- "...video CoSwin replaces the tokenization layer of CoSwin (in Section 2.3) from 2D convolutional layers to 3D convolutional layers, which converts each 3D tube into one token." (Section 2.6)
- "...we follow prior work to replace the 2D shifted window design with 3D shifted local windows in self-attention layers." (Section 2.6)

12) model_pipeline
Answer: Image/Text/Video inputs are processed by a two-tower architecture (CoSwin image encoder, Transformer language encoder) with task-specific adapters (Dynamic Head, METER, Video CoSwin) to produce outputs for classification, detection, retrieval, VQA, and action recognition.
Quotes:
- "Overview of building Florence. Our workflow consists of data curation, unified learning, Transformer architectures and adaption. It shows the foundation model can be adapted to various downstream tasks..." (Figure 2)
- "We extended the learned feature representation along space (from scene to objects) using the dynamic head adapter , time (from static image to videos) via proposed video CoSwin adapter, and modality (from images to language) via METER adapter." (Section 1. Introduction)

13) dataset
Answer: A new 900M image-text pair dataset (FLD-900M) and a 9M object detection dataset (FLOD-9M) were created; evaluation used ImageNet-1K, COCO, Kinetics-400/600, VQA, MSR-VTT, Flickr30K, and others.
Quotes:
- "Specifically, we construct a 900 million image-text-pair dataset, called FLD-900M (FLD stands for FLorenceDataset)..." (Section 2.1)
- "We have constructed a large-scale object detection dataset, called FLOD-9M (for FLorence Object detection Dataset), for object detection pre-training." (Section 2.4)
- "Florence achieves new state-of-the-art results in majority of 44 representative benchmarks, e.g. ImageNet-1K zero-shot classification..., 62.4 mAP on COCO fine tuning, 80.36 on VQA, and 87.8 on Kinetics-600." (Abstract)

14) comparisons
Answer: Florence was compared against baselines like CLIP and ALIGN, achieving state-of-the-art on ImageNet-1K zero-shot (83.74% top-1 accuracy), COCO detection (62.4 mAP), VQA (80.36), and Kinetics-600 (87.8% top-1 accuracy).
Quotes:
- "Florence outperforms on 9/12 tasks compared with stateof-the-art methods. We achieved a remarkable improvement in the zero-shot transfer on ImageNet-1K – the top-1 accuracy of 83.74% (+5.6% over SOTA result), and the top-5 accuracy of 97.18%." (Section 3.1)
- "COCO test-Dev ... Florence 62.4" (Table 6)
- "Compare our model with the existing state-of-the-art methods on VQA. ... Florence 80.16 | 80.36" (Table 8)
- "Comparison to state-of-the-art methods... on Kinetics-400 and Kinetics-600. ... Florence | FLD-900M | 86.5 | 97.3 | 87.8 | 97.8" (Table 10)

15) hyperparameters
Answer: Pre-training used a batch size of 24,576, image resolutions of 224x224 and 384x384, and a maximum language description length of 76.
Quotes:
- "The image size is 224 × 224 and the maximum language description length is truncated at 76. The batch size is 24,576." (Section 2.2)
- "We further trained 80K iterations at a higher resolution of 384 × 384 to boost the performance, which follows existing pre-training approaches." (Section 2.2)

16) environment
Answer: The model was trained for 10 days on 512 NVIDIA-A100 GPUs with 40GB memory each.
Quotes:
- "The model takes 10 days to train on 512 NVIDIA-A100 GPUs with 40GB memory per GPU." (Section 2.3)
- "Thanks to these above optimizations, we can achieve consistent improvement in reducing GPU memory for variable batch sizes on various numbers of NVIDIA-A100s, shown in Figure 5." (Section 2.7)

17) repository
Answer: Not mentioned.
Quotes: None.

18) authors
Answer: Authors are from Microsoft Cloud and AI and Microsoft Research Redmond; no venue or year is explicitly stated.
Quotes:
- "Lu Yuan 1 Dongdong Chen *1 ... Pengchuan Zhang *2" (Title Block)
- "*Florence Team member in alphabetic order 1Microsoft Cloud and AI 2Microsoft Research Redmond. Correspondence to: Lu Yuan ." (Footnote on Page 1)

paper_file: HumanOmni - A Large Vision-Speech Language Model for Human-Centric Video Understanding.md

video_segmentation.answer: The paper uses scene recognition algorithms to segment long videos into shorter clips at scene boundaries to ensure temporal smoothness.
quotes: To extract naturally smooth temporal changes in the video, we employed scene recognition algorithms to identify multiple scenes within videos and segment them into clips, as shown in the center of Fig. 2." (Section 3.1, Stage 1)
Scene detection and segmentation avoid drastic changes from multiple different perspectives in the video clip." (Section 3.1, Stage 1)

frame_sampling_method.answer: Not mentioned. The paper describes using keyframe detection to filter out videos with minimal temporal changes, but not as the frame sampling method for model input.
quotes: Furthermore, we extracted keyframes for each clip to find the temporal variations. The clips with minimal temporal changes would have very few keyframes identified, allowing us to screen out such videos based on the number of detected keyframes." (Section 3.1, Stage 1)

input_video_length.answer: Not mentioned. The paper processes "video clips" segmented from a large-scale dataset but does not specify their duration or mention processing videos longer than 60 minutes.

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Yes, spatiotemporal analysis is performed using a spatial-temporal projector (STC) for the body-related and interaction-related branches to handle continuous actions and interaction scenes.
quotes: In contrast, the body-related branch and interaction-related branch utilize a spatial-temporal projector STC [], handling continuous actions and interaction scenes." (Section 2)

...enabling the model to learn rich spatio-temporal feature representations and patterns of human behavior, thereby achieving a deep understanding of human-related details in video content." (Section 4)

visual_analysis.answer: The model is not visual-only; it processes vision, audio, and text. The visual backbone used is the SigLIP visual encoder.
quotes: HumanOmni is a vision-speech language model that focus on human-centric scenes." (Figure 1 caption)

Leveraging advanced visual encoders SigLIP [] and large language models Qwen2.5 [], which exhibit strong feature extraction and representation capabilities, our branch architectures remain flexible and do not require task-specific modifications." (Section 2)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Yes, speech audio is analyzed using the audio preprocessor and encoder from Whisper-large-v3, and the model is trained and evaluated on automatic speech recognition (ASR) tasks.
quotes: For the auditory component, we follow [54] utilizing the audio preprocessor and encoder from Whisper-large-v3 [40] to process audio data." (Section 2)
HumanOmni excels in audio-only tasks like automatic speech recognition, delivering results comparable to leading models in this field." (Section 1)
We also select multiple comprehensive ASR datasets including WenetSpeech [], GigaSpeech [], CommonVoice15 [], and LibriSpeech []." (Section 4.2)

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Yes, non-speech audio is analyzed for tasks like automatic audio captioning and sound event classification using datasets such as WavCaps and VGGSound.
quotes: we exclusively sample audio data from tasks such as automatic audio captioning, automatic speech recognition, and sound event classification, resulting in a total of approximately 18,000 hours of data used to train the audio projector." (Section 4.2)
Specifically, we utilize the WavCaps [] dataset, which provides around 7,500 hours of annotated audio, offering detailed captions that describe the audio events." (Section 4.2)
For SEC, the VGGSound [] dataset is chosen due to its extensive collection of audio events." (Section 4.2)

qa_interaction.answer: Yes, the model has Q&A capabilities, processing user instructions and generating text responses using the Qwen2.5 large language model as its decoder.
quotes: HumanOmni includes three specialized branches for understanding different types of scenes. It adaptively fuses features from these branches based on user instructions, significantly enhancing visual understanding in scenes centered around individuals." (Abstract)
consequently, the audio tokens, along with visual and text tokens, are concatenated within a unified representation space... and then fed into the LLM decoder for further processing." (Section 2)

retrieval_level.answer: Not mentioned. The system is a generative model that outputs textual answers to user instructions about video content, rather than retrieving videos, clips, or timestamps.

inferring_method.answer: Inferences about activities, emotions, and expressions are made by a large language model (Qwen2.5) which processes fused visual, audio, and text instruction tokens to generate a textual response.
quotes: HumanOmni dynamically adjusts its fusion weights based on input instructions, ensuring accurate responses across various scenes." (Section 1)

consequently, the audio tokens, along with visual and text tokens, are concatenated within a unified representation space using specific tokens to distinguish between features from different modalities, and then fed into the LLM decoder for further processing." (Section 2)

video_representation.answer: Video content is represented by feature embeddings from three specialized visual branches (face, body, interaction), which are dynamically fused, projected, and concatenated with audio and text tokens before being fed to an LLM.
quotes: We use three branches to handle face-related, body-related, and interaction-related scenes separately in HumanOmni. An instruction-driven fusion module then integrates features from these branches." (Section 1)

Suppose the three projectors extract features denoted as F_face, F_body, and F_inter, and the generated weights are w_face, w_body, and w_inter, respectively. Then, the final visual representation F is given by: F = w_face * F_face + w_body * F_body + w_inter * F_inter" (Section 2)

model_pipeline.answer: Multimodal inputs (video, audio, text) → Video is processed by three specialized branches (SigLIP encoder), audio by a Whisper encoder, and text instructions by BERT to create fusion weights → Fused features are projected and fed into a Qwen2.5 LLM decoder → Textual response.
quotes: Pipeline of HumanOmni. HumanOmni is a vision-speech language model that focus on human-centric scenes. For the visual component, we pre-trained three distinct branches using separate data. The features from these branches are fused based on user instructions. HumanOmni also supports audio input, enhancing its ability to fully understand complex human-centric scenes." (Figure 1 caption)

dataset.answer: A new dataset of 2.4M human-centric video clips was created from Panda-70M. The model was also trained on Oryx, WavCaps, WenetSpeech, GigaSpeech, CommonVoice15, LibriSpeech, and VGGSound, and evaluated on DFEW, MAFW, DFEC, MVBench, and Fleurs.
quotes: We have constructed a dataset containing over 2.4M human-centric video clips, providing rich and detailed information about individuals." (Section 1)
We form our dataset based on the existing web-scale dataset: Panda-70M [] which covers various scenes and contents." (Section 3.1)
Additionally, we manually annotated a subset of our human-centric video data by randomly sampling 50K video clips containing both vision and speech." (Section 3.2)

comparisons.answer: HumanOmni is compared against specialized models (Emotion-LLaMA, FaceTrack-MM), VLMs (Qwen2-VL), and Omni models (GPT4-O, VITA) on benchmarks like DFEW, MAFW, DFEC, and MVBench, showing significant gains such as +5.03 WAR on DFEW over MMA-DFER.
quotes: Experimental results demonstrate that HumanOmni significantly outperforms existing video-language multimodal models, audio-language multimodal large models, recently proposed omni model and specialized methods in this field." (Section 5.1)

...our experimental results show that the HumanOmni model with combined video and audio input not only outperforms other open-source models but also surpasses the FaceTrack-MM [] method proposed in DFEC, achieving superior performance in facial expression description tasks." (Section 5.2)

hyperparameters.answer: Not mentioned.

environment.answer: Not mentioned.

To promote community-driven development and further research, we will open-source our code and model." (Section 6)
