quotes: This collection contains 140 broadcast news video clips from U.S., Arabic, and Chinese sources, with durations of 30 minutes to 1 hour each, pre-segmented into 45,765 shots." (Section 3. PERFORMANCE EVALUATION)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Not mentioned.

visual_analysis.answer: Yes, the system uses visual-only analysis via support vector machine (SVM) based models to detect 39 semantic concepts.
quotes: Successful concept modeling and detection approaches have been developed in TRECVID, relying predominantly on visual analysis and statistical machine learning methods [1,2,6]." (Section 1. INTRODUCTION)

As a first step we built support vector machine based semantic concept models [6] for all the annotated concepts of the LSCOM-lite lexicon based on some visual features from the training collection." (Section 2.1. Semantic concept lexicon)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Yes, automatic speech recognition (ASR) transcripts are used for the text-based retrieval baseline.
quotes: Each video comes with a speech transcript obtained through automatic speech recognition, as well as machine translation for the non-English sources." (Section 3. PERFORMANCE EVALUATION)
...retrieval results based on other modalities, such as closed caption (CC) or automatic speech recognition (ASR) transcripts, video OCR, etc." (Section 2.5 Model-based re-ranking)

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned.

qa_interaction.answer: Not mentioned. The system performs search and retrieval, not interactive Q&A.

retrieval_level.answer: The system returns a ranked list of video shots; the text search baseline uses the JuruXML search engine.
quotes: Text search is performed via queries of words or fully qualified sentences, and results are returned in the form of ranked video clips." (ABSTRACT)
The text search baseline is obtained with JuruXML—a text search engine available as part of the IBM UIMA SDK3." (Section 3. PERFORMANCE EVALUATION)

inferring_method.answer: Relevance is inferred by fusing SVM-based concept detection scores with query-to-concept relevance scores, which are calculated using WordNet and an adapted Lesk semantic relatedness measure.
quotes: In this step, query concept vectors (concept weight scores) are fused with shot concept vectors (concept detection results) to determine a ranked list of matching shots." (Section 2.4. Model-based retrieval)
We use an adapted Lesk semantic relatedness score [3], one of many available for WordNet, to compute similarities between pairs of words." (Section 2.3. Query to concept mapping)

video_representation.answer: Video shots are represented by a vector of detection confidence scores for 39 semantic concepts.
quotes: ...we have a set of models that can be applied to automatically detect a corresponding set of concepts such that each video shot can be annotated with a detection confidence score for each concept." (Section 1. INTRODUCTION)

model_pipeline.answer: Text query → Lexical expansion using WordNet → Query-to-concept mapping via Lesk similarity → Fusion of concept detection scores with text search results → Ranked list of video shots.
quotes: Our approach is split into four parts: 1. Query term extraction of words and qualified phrases, 2. Calculation of semantic relatedness scores between extracted query terms and concepts, 3. Model-based retrieval of shots through fusion across relevant concept detection results, and 4. Model-based re-ranking of shots through fusion of textbased and model-based retrieval results." (Section 2. APPROACH)

dataset.answer: The TRECVID 2005 broadcast news dataset was used, containing 140 video clips, with 39 concepts from the LSCOM-lite lexicon.
quotes: We validate our approach on the TRECVID 2005 broadcast news data with 39 concepts specifically designed for this genre of video." (ABSTRACT)

We have evaluated the proposed approach on the TRECVID 2005 test corpus and query topics2. This collection contains 140 broadcast news video clips from U.S., Arabic, and Chinese sources..." (Section 3. PERFORMANCE EVALUATION)

comparisons.answer: The model-based re-ranking approach was compared to a text-search baseline on TRECVID 2005 topics, achieving a 49% improvement in Mean Average Precision across all topics.
quotes: We observe that concept models improve search results by nearly 50% after model-based re-ranking of text-only search." (ABSTRACT)
Table 4. Performance summary (Mean Average Precision scores) for text-based retrieval baseline vs. proposed model-based retrieval and model-based re-ranking approaches." (Table 4)

hyperparameters.answer: The system uses the top 3 most similar concepts per query and simple non-weighted score averaging for fusion.
quotes: Of these, we currently consider only the top 3 most similar concepts (and their weights) for each query, and set all other weights to 0 to reduce noise effects." (Section 2.3. Query to concept mapping)
For simplicity, we consider only simple nonweighted score averaging (after global range normalization) as the preferred fusion method." (Section 2.5 Model-based re-ranking)

paper_file: Smart Routing for Multimodal Video Retrieval When to Search What.md

video_segmentation.answer: The ASR index is built using sentence-level segmentation, but the method for segmenting source videos into clips is not specified.
quotes: The ASR index uses sentence-level segmentation to capture semantic units while maintaining temporal context." (Section 3.2)

frame_sampling_method.answer: Visual embeddings are generated from frames sampled at 1 frame per second, which are then mean-pooled.
quotes: Our implementation uses SigLIP SoViT-400m large with mean-pooled 1 frame per second for visual embeddings..." (Section 3.1)

input_video_length.answer: Not mentioned.

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Spatiotemporal analysis is performed by mean-pooling frame-level visual embeddings from the SigLIP SoViT-400m model.
quotes: Our implementation uses SigLIP SoViT-400m large with mean-pooled 1 frame per second for visual embeddings..." (Section 3.1)

visual_analysis.answer: Yes, the system analyzes the visual-only modality using SigLIP SoViT-400m large as the visual backbone.
quotes: Our implementation uses SigLIP SoViT-400m large with mean-pooled 1 frame per second for visual embeddings..." (Section 3.1)
VISUAL: Queries about visual scenes ('what color is...', 'what object is...', 'describe the gesture...')" (Section 3.1)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Yes, the system uses ASR-generated transcripts of spoken content, which are then encoded using OpenAI text-embedding-3-large.
quotes: Speech Transcript: ASR-generated transcriptions of spoken content" (Section 4.1)
...and OpenAI text-embedding-3-large for ASR and OCR text encoding." (Section 3.1)

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned; the paper identifies fine-grained audio analysis of music and sound effects as a direction for future work.
quotes: ...this routing approach becomes increasingly valuable as multimodal systems expand to include... fine-grained audio analysis (music, sound effects, speaker identification)..." (Section 1)

qa_interaction.answer: The system uses GPT-4.1 to analyze a user's natural language query for one-shot modality routing, not for conversational Q&A.
quotes: We introduce ModaRoute, an LLM-based intelligent routing system that dynamically selects optimal modalities for multimodal video retrieval." (Abstract)
Our routing component uses GPT-4.1 with a carefully engineered prompt that performs dual functions: modality selection and query optimization." (Section 3.1)

retrieval_level.answer: The system retrieves a ranked list of video clips by combining results from selected modality indices using rank-based fusion.
quotes: Evaluation on 1.8M video clips demonstrates that intelligent routing provides a practical solution for scaling multimodal retrieval systems..." (Abstract)
When multiple modalities are selected, ModaRoute employs rank-based fusion to combine results." (Section 3.3)

inferring_method.answer: An LLM router (GPT-4.1) infers user intent by analyzing the linguistic structure of a query to predict the most relevant modalities.
quotes: By analyzing the linguistic structure and semantic content of natural language queries, our LLM-based router predicts which modalities are most likely to contain relevant information, then searches only those selected indices." (Section 1)

video_representation.answer: Content is represented via separate indices: text embeddings (OpenAI text-embedding-3-large) for ASR/OCR and visual embeddings (SigLIP SoViT-400m) for visual content.
quotes: Our implementation uses SigLIP SoViT-400m large with mean-pooled 1 frame per second for visual embeddings and OpenAI text-embedding-3-large for ASR and OCR text encoding." (Section 3.1)

model_pipeline.answer: Natural language query → GPT-4.1 router selects modalities (ASR, OCR, Visual) → Parallel index search → Rank-based fusion of results → Ranked list of video clips.
quotes: The process begins with natural language queries that are analyzed by an LLM router to determine modality relevance. Selected modalities are queried in parallel, and results are combined through rank-based fusion to produce the final ranking." (Section 3)

dataset.answer: An unnamed, newly created dataset of 1.8 million video clips from 29,259 source videos, along with 2,195 hand-annotated retrieval queries.
quotes: We evaluate on a 1.8M-clip subset from a large-scale multimodal video dataset with comprehensive annotations... spanning 29,259 source videos across diverse content categories." (Section 4.1)
Query Annotations: 2,195 hand-annotated retrieval queries with ground-truth relevance judgments" (Section 4.1)

comparisons.answer: Compared to All-Text, Late Fusion, ASR Only, Visual Only (SigLIP), and OCR Only baselines, achieving 60.9% Recall@5 while reducing computational cost by 41%.
quotes: The results demonstrate that ModaRoute's intelligent routing strategy achieves 40.5% Recall@1 and 60.9% Recall@5 while querying an average of only 1.78 modalities per request. This represents a 41% reduction in computational cost compared to exhaustive search (3.0 modalities)..." (Section 5.1)

raw_output:
1) video_segmentation
Answer: The ASR index is built using sentence-level segmentation, but the method for segmenting source videos into clips is not specified.
Quotes:
- "The ASR index uses sentence-level segmentation to capture semantic units while maintaining temporal context." (Section 3.2)

2) frame_sampling_method
Answer: Visual embeddings are generated from frames sampled at 1 frame per second, which are then mean-pooled.
Quotes:
- "Our implementation uses SigLIP SoViT-400m large with mean-pooled 1 frame per second for visual embeddings..." (Section 3.1)

3) input_video_length
Answer: Not mentioned.
Quotes: None.

4) spatiotemporal_analysis
Answer: Spatiotemporal analysis is performed by mean-pooling frame-level visual embeddings from the SigLIP SoViT-400m model.
Quotes:
- "Our implementation uses SigLIP SoViT-400m large with mean-pooled 1 frame per second for visual embeddings..." (Section 3.1)

5) visual_analysis
Answer: Yes, the system analyzes the visual-only modality using SigLIP SoViT-400m large as the visual backbone.
Quotes:
- "Our implementation uses SigLIP SoViT-400m large with mean-pooled 1 frame per second for visual embeddings..." (Section 3.1)
- "VISUAL: Queries about visual scenes ('what color is...', 'what object is...', 'describe the gesture...')" (Section 3.1)

6) speech_audio_analysis
Answer: Yes, the system uses ASR-generated transcripts of spoken content, which are then encoded using OpenAI text-embedding-3-large.
Quotes:
- "Speech Transcript: ASR-generated transcriptions of spoken content" (Section 4.1)
- "...and OpenAI text-embedding-3-large for ASR and OCR text encoding." (Section 3.1)

7) sound_audio_analysis
Answer: Not mentioned; the paper identifies fine-grained audio analysis of music and sound effects as a direction for future work.
Quotes:
- "...this routing approach becomes increasingly valuable as multimodal systems expand to include... fine-grained audio analysis (music, sound effects, speaker identification)..." (Section 1)

8) qa_interaction
Answer: The system uses GPT-4.1 to analyze a user's natural language query for one-shot modality routing, not for conversational Q&A.
Quotes:
- "We introduce ModaRoute, an LLM-based intelligent routing system that dynamically selects optimal modalities for multimodal video retrieval." (Abstract)
- "Our routing component uses GPT-4.1 with a carefully engineered prompt that performs dual functions: modality selection and query optimization." (Section 3.1)

9) retrieval_level
Answer: The system retrieves a ranked list of video clips by combining results from selected modality indices using rank-based fusion.
Quotes:
- "Evaluation on 1.8M video clips demonstrates that intelligent routing provides a practical solution for scaling multimodal retrieval systems..." (Abstract)
- "When multiple modalities are selected, ModaRoute employs rank-based fusion to combine results." (Section 3.3)

10) inferring_method
Answer: An LLM router (GPT-4.1) infers user intent by analyzing the linguistic structure of a query to predict the most relevant modalities.
Quotes:
- "By analyzing the linguistic structure and semantic content of natural language queries, our LLM-based router predicts which modalities are most likely to contain relevant information, then searches only those selected indices." (Section 1)

11) video_representation
Answer: Content is represented via separate indices: text embeddings (OpenAI text-embedding-3-large) for ASR/OCR and visual embeddings (SigLIP SoViT-400m) for visual content.
Quotes:
- "Our implementation uses SigLIP SoViT-400m large with mean-pooled 1 frame per second for visual embeddings and OpenAI text-embedding-3-large for ASR and OCR text encoding." (Section 3.1)

12) model_pipeline
Answer: Natural language query → GPT-4.1 router selects modalities (ASR, OCR, Visual) → Parallel index search → Rank-based fusion of results → Ranked list of video clips.
Quotes:
- "The process begins with natural language queries that are analyzed by an LLM router to determine modality relevance. Selected modalities are queried in parallel, and results are combined through rank-based fusion to produce the final ranking." (Section 3)

13) dataset
Answer: An unnamed, newly created dataset of 1.8 million video clips from 29,259 source videos, along with 2,195 hand-annotated retrieval queries.
Quotes:
- "We evaluate on a 1.8M-clip subset from a large-scale multimodal video dataset with comprehensive annotations... spanning 29,259 source videos across diverse content categories." (Section 4.1)
- "Query Annotations: 2,195 hand-annotated retrieval queries with ground-truth relevance judgments" (Section 4.1)

14) comparisons
Answer: Compared to All-Text, Late Fusion, ASR Only, Visual Only (SigLIP), and OCR Only baselines, achieving 60.9% Recall@5 while reducing computational cost by 41%.
Quotes:
- "The results demonstrate that ModaRoute's intelligent routing strategy achieves 40.5% Recall@1 and 60.9% Recall@5 while querying an average of only 1.78 modalities per request. This represents a 41% reduction in computational cost compared to exhaustive search (3.0 modalities)..." (Section 5.1)
- "| Method | R@1 | R@5 | ... | All-Text (Optimal) | 48.2 | 75.9 | ... | ModaRoute (Ours) | 40.5 | 60.9 | ... | Late Fusion (All 3) | 17.1 | 38.7 | ... | ASR Only | 34.2 | 46.2 | ... | Visual Only (SigLIP) | 13.1 | 31.7 | ... | OCR Only | 16.8 | 28.3 |" (Table 2)

video_segmentation.answer: The paper uses pre-segmented 3-second video clips sourced from the Moments in Time dataset.
quotes: Further, the videos are all cut to 3 seconds allowing for a concise description to effectively capture the localized information of each event." (Section 3)

In this work, we present a new dataset, Spoken Moments in Time (S-MiT), which includes spoken audio captions for 500k unique three second clips each with different source videos from the Moments in Time dataset []." (Section 2.2)

frame_sampling_method.answer: The video model samples 8 frames for the TSM model at approximately 3 fps and 3 frames for the image model at 1 fps.
quotes: Each encoder outputs a 2048-D feature vector after max-pooling over the temporal dimension (8 frames for the TSM (∼3 fps) and 3 frames for the image model (1 fps))." (Section 4.1)

input_video_length.answer: The paper primarily processes unique 3-second video clips from the Spoken Moments in Time dataset; no processed videos are longer than 60 minutes.
quotes: Further, the videos are all cut to 3 seconds allowing for a concise description to effectively capture the localized information of each event." (Section 3)
Additionally, the restriction to 3-second videos in S-MiT does not hinder it's ability to generalize to the much longer videos of the other datasets." (Section 5.2)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Spatiotemporal analysis is performed using a temporal shift module (TSM) ResNet-50 model to integrate temporal information from frame sequences.
quotes: Specifically, we use a ResNet-152 [] pretrained on ImageNet [] and a temporal shift module (TSM) ResNet-50 model [] pretrained on M-MiT []." (Section 4.1)
More recently a temporal shift module has been proposed to integrate temporal information into 2D models by shifting frame representations across the temporal dimension []." (Section 2.1)

visual_analysis.answer: The paper is not visual-only; it uses a ResNet-152 for image features and a temporal shift module (TSM) ResNet-50 for video features as visual backbones.
quotes: Following prior work [], we use two encoders to represent input videos: image & video encoders. Specifically, we use a ResNet-152 [] pretrained on ImageNet [] and a temporal shift module (TSM) ResNet-50 model [] pretrained on M-MiT []." (Section 4.1)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Speech is analyzed using the public Google ASR engine for transcriptions that are fed into language models (BART, BERT), and also directly using spoken caption models like ResDavenet and wav2vec.
quotes: We transcribed each audio recording using the public Google Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) engine to generate text captions for each video." (Section 3.1)

We also train caption models with raw spoken captions instead of the corresponding transcription... we consider ResDavenet []... and two ImageNet ResNet [] models... the wav2vec [] model, which takes raw waveform as the input, is also involved in our experiments." (Section 4.2.2)

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned. The paper analyzes spoken captions from annotators, not the background sound, music, or effects from the original video audio streams.
quotes: For the audio streams of the videos, roughly 51% include natural sound, 5% have music as the audio and 44% have no audio." (Section 3.1)
For this reason we have chosen to focus our approach on learning a strong visual model in Section 4." (Section 3.1)

qa_interaction.answer: Not mentioned. The paper focuses on video and caption retrieval tasks, not interactive question-answering or chat capabilities.

retrieval_level.answer: The system performs retrieval at the video clip level, enabling both caption-to-video and video-to-caption retrieval tasks.
quotes: We evaluate our models on multiple datasets for video/caption retrieval and show that a model trained using AMM on S-MiT achieves the best general performance across four datasets." (Section 1)
In Tables 1, 2 and 3 we show results of R@k recall scores (for k = 1, 5, 10) and mean average precision (mAP) on both caption to video and video to caption retrieval." (Section 5.1)

inferring_method.answer: Not mentioned. The system aligns video and caption representations in a joint embedding space via contrastive learning for retrieval, rather than explicitly inferring roles or activities.
quotes: We then jointly train caption and visual models (based on concatenated video and image features) using a novel Adaptive Mean Margin (AMM) approach to contrastive learning to align the visual and caption representations." (Section 1)

video_representation.answer: Videos are represented as 4096-D embedding vectors, created by concatenating features from a ResNet-152 and a TSM ResNet-50, which are then passed through an MLP projection head.
quotes: we take N video-caption pairs as input and encode each modality into a 4096-D feature vector." (Section 4)
We concatenate the two 2048-D vectors and feed the concatenated vector into an MLP projection head to get the final 4096-D visual representation." (Section 4.1)

model_pipeline.answer: Video clips and spoken captions are encoded by separate models (TSM ResNet-50 and BART) into feature vectors, which are aligned in a joint embedding space using an Adaptive Mean Margin contrastive loss for cross-modal retrieval.
quotes: Videos and captions are fed into the video/caption models where the outputs are used to compute a similarity matrix, S, which is used to compute a loss, L." (Figure 3)

dataset.answer: The paper introduces the Spoken Moments in Time (S-MiT) dataset and uses Vatex-en, MSR-VTT, and ActivityNet Captions for cross-dataset evaluation and comparison.
quotes: To address this, we present the Spoken Moments (S-MiT) dataset of 500k spoken captions each attributed to a unique short video depicting a broad range of different events." (Abstract)

we compare the results on the NCE, SHN, MMS and AMM loss functions described in Sections 2.3.1 and 4.3 on four different datasets (the proposed Spoken Moments in Time dataset (S-MiT) as well as Vatex-en [], MSR-VTT [] and ActivityNet Captions1 [])." (Section 5.1)

comparisons.answer: The proposed Adaptive Mean Margin (AMM) loss is compared against NCE, SHN, and MMS baselines, consistently showing improved performance. Models trained on S-MiT show better generalization than models trained on Vatex, ActivityNet, and MSR-VTT.
quotes: The proposed AMM loss function consistently achieves the best results across each dataset in Table 2 and the BART language model provides the strongest representations for the retrieval task in Table 1." (Section 5.1)
In Table 4, we can see that the S-MiT model generalizes better than the other models in spite of the additional noise introduced by the ASR model." (Section 5.2)

hyperparameters.answer: Not mentioned. The paper specifies the number of frames sampled (8 for TSM, 3 for image model) and caption input details (10 words or 10 seconds of speech), but not training hyperparameters like batch size, learning rate, or epochs.
quotes: Each encoder outputs a 2048-D feature vector after max-pooling over the temporal dimension (8 frames for the TSM (∼3 fps) and 3 frames for the image model (1 fps))." (Section 4.1)
During training, we randomly select 10 words from each caption to be included in training." (Section 4.2.1)
For each caption, we randomly sample 10 seconds of speech for training and compute the 40-dimensional log Mel spectrogram to serve as the input of spoken caption model." (Section 4.2.2)

paper_file: TACo Token-aware Cascade Contrastive Learning for Video-Text Alignment.md

video_segmentation.answer: Not mentioned, but for the Howto100M dataset, short adjacent clips are merged until the corresponding text has at least 10 words.
quotes: Since the original annotated video clips in Howto100M are usually short with a few seconds, we merge the adjacent clips so that the resulted text has at least 10 words." (Section 4.3. Pretraining and finetuning)

frame_sampling_method.answer: For 2D features, one frame per second is sampled; for 3D features, frames are sampled at 24 fps and one feature is extracted every 16 frames.
quotes: The 2D R-152 feature is extracted for one frame per second, and then globally pooled to 2048-d." (Section 4.3. Implementation details)

For 3D CNN features, we follow [35] to sample video frames at 24 fps and extract an I3D-X101 feature every 16 frames. This results in 1.5 2048-d feature per second." (Section 4.3. Implementation details)

input_video_length.answer: The model processes short video clips, typically a few seconds long, from standard datasets; no videos longer than 60 minutes are mentioned.
quotes: Since the original annotated video clips in Howto100M are usually short with a few seconds, we merge the adjacent clips so that the resulted text has at least 10 words." (Section 4.3. Pretraining and finetuning)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Yes, spatiotemporal analysis is performed using 3D CNNs like I3D and S3D for feature extraction, followed by a video encoder with stacked self-attention layers.
quotes: For 3D features, commonly used models are I3D [5], R(2+1)D [46] and S3D [51]." (Section 4.2. Settings)
Video encoding module . It is implemented by a stack of self-attention layers parameterized by θv." (Section 3.1. Framework)

visual_analysis.answer: The model is multi-modal (video-text), not visual-only, and uses visual backbones including ResNet-152 for 2D features and I3D/S3D for 3D features.
quotes: For 2D CNN, Resnet-152 [18] is used to extract feature map and then globally pooled to 2048-d [35, 33]." (Section 4.2. Settings)
For 3D CNN features, we use I3D (with Resnext-101 backbone) pretrained on Kinetics-400 [23] and S3D [51] pretrained on Howto100M [34]." (Section 4.2. Settings)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned. The model uses video and text modalities, not speech audio analysis.

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned. The model uses video and text modalities, not sound audio analysis.

qa_interaction.answer: Not mentioned. The paper focuses on retrieval, localization, and segmentation tasks without interactive Q&A capabilities.

retrieval_level.answer: The system performs text-to-video clip retrieval and also supports finer-grained action step localization and segmentation, implying timestamp-level retrieval.
quotes: To validate the effectiveness of TACo, in our experiments we finetune pretrained models for a set of downstream tasks including text-video retrieval (YouCook2, MSR-VTT and ActivityNet), video action step localization (CrossTask), video action segmentation (COIN)." (Abstract)

inferring_method.answer: Inference is performed by computing and summing alignment scores from three different contrastive scoring functions between video and text representations.
quotes: During inference, we make the prediction by summing the alignment scores from all the three scoring functions." (Section 3.4. Objective)
we use our model to compute the similarity between each frame and the action step descriptions, which results in a score matrix." (Section 5.2. Other video-related tasks)

video_representation.answer: Video clips are represented as a sequence of features from a video encoder (self-attention layers) applied to pre-extracted 2D/3D CNN embeddings, which are then averaged for sentence-level comparison.
quotes: Given the input video embeddings, video encoder starts with a linear layer to project them to the same dimension d as following self-attention layers. We denote the output of our video encoder for a video clip by a sequence of m features, v_f = {v_f1, ..., v_fm}." (Section 3.1. Framework)
Then, we average all tokens of a video clip to get v_i, and take the first '[CLS]' token for each text to get t_i." (Section 3.3. TACo: our approach)

model_pipeline.answer: Video/text inputs → Separate video (self-attention) and language (BERT) encoders → Multi-modal fusion module (self-attention) → Trained with three contrastive losses → Output alignment scores for retrieval/localization.
quotes: As depicted in Fig. 1, our model has three components: Video encoding module . ... Language encoding module . ... Multi-modal fusion module . ... The above three components comprise our video-text alignment model which is then trained with the proposed token-aware cascade contrastive loss." (Section 3.1. Framework)

dataset.answer: YouCook2, MSR-VTT, ActivityNet, Howto100M (for pretraining), CrossTask, and COIN.
quotes: In our experiments, we train and evaluate our model on the following established benchmarks: YouCook2, MSR-VTT, ActivityNet, Howto100M." (Section 4.1. Datasets)
we also evaluate the action step localization and action segmentation on CrossTask [61] and COIN [43], respectively." (Section 4.1. Datasets)

comparisons.answer: Outperforms baselines like UniVL and MMT on text-video retrieval across YouCook2, MSR-VTT, and ActivityNet, achieving state-of-the-art results (e.g., R@1 of 30.4 on ActivityNet vs. MMT's 28.7).
quotes: The results show that our models attain consistent improvements across different experimental settings over previous methods, setting new state-of-the-art on three public text-video retrieval benchmarks of YouCook2, MSR-VTT and ActivityNet." (Abstract)
As we can see, our model using S3D-HM outperforms UniVL [33] using the same feature but more video encoder layers (6)." (Section 5.1.2 Comparing with state-of-the-art)

we enrich our video representation by adding 2D R-152 feature, which achieves better performance on MSR-VTT, and better Recall@1 and Median Rank on ActivityNet." (Table 8, Section 5.1.2 Comparing with state-of-the-art)

hyperparameters.answer: Batch size: 128 (training) or 64 (pretraining); LR: 1e-4 (training) or 5e-5 (pretraining); Optimizer: Adam; Iterations: 30k (training), 500k (pretraining); Hard negatives: 8; Max tokens: 48 video/30 text.
quotes: We train the model for 30k iterations with batch size 128. For each training sample, we use our cascade sampling strategy to sample 8 hard negatives. We use Adam [24] as the optimizer with initial learning rate 1e-4." (Section 4.3. Training on separate datasets)
We train the model for 500k iterations with batch size 64, and also sample 8 hard negatives for each sample... optimizer with initial learning rate 5e-5." (Section 4.3. Pretraining and finetuning)

For YouCook2 and MSR-VTT, the maximum number of video and text tokens are set to 48 and 30, respectively. For paragraph-video retrieval on ActivityNet, we set them both to 256." (Section 4.3. Implementation details)

- "For 3D CNN features, we follow [35] to sample video frames at 24 fps and extract an I3D-X101 feature every 16 frames. This results in 1.5 2048-d feature per second." (Section 4.3. Implementation details)

Answer: The model processes short video clips, typically a few seconds long, from standard datasets; no videos longer than 60 minutes are mentioned.
Quotes:
- "Since the original annotated video clips in Howto100M are usually short with a few seconds, we merge the adjacent clips so that the resulted text has at least 10 words." (Section 4.3. Pretraining and finetuning)

4) spatiotemporal_analysis
Answer: Yes, spatiotemporal analysis is performed using 3D CNNs like I3D and S3D for feature extraction, followed by a video encoder with stacked self-attention layers.
Quotes:
- "For 3D features, commonly used models are I3D [5], R(2+1)D [46] and S3D [51]." (Section 4.2. Settings)
- "Video encoding module . It is implemented by a stack of self-attention layers parameterized by θv." (Section 3.1. Framework)

5) visual_analysis
Answer: The model is multi-modal (video-text), not visual-only, and uses visual backbones including ResNet-152 for 2D features and I3D/S3D for 3D features.
Quotes:
- "For 2D CNN, Resnet-152 [18] is used to extract feature map and then globally pooled to 2048-d [35, 33]." (Section 4.2. Settings)
- "For 3D CNN features, we use I3D (with Resnext-101 backbone) pretrained on Kinetics-400 [23] and S3D [51] pretrained on Howto100M [34]." (Section 4.2. Settings)

6) speech_audio_analysis
Answer: Not mentioned. The model uses video and text modalities, not speech audio analysis.
Quotes: None.

7) sound_audio_analysis
Answer: Not mentioned. The model uses video and text modalities, not sound audio analysis.
Quotes: None.

8) qa_interaction
Answer: Not mentioned. The paper focuses on retrieval, localization, and segmentation tasks without interactive Q&A capabilities.
Quotes: None.

9) retrieval_level
Answer: The system performs text-to-video clip retrieval and also supports finer-grained action step localization and segmentation, implying timestamp-level retrieval.
Quotes:
- "To validate the effectiveness of TACo, in our experiments we finetune pretrained models for a set of downstream tasks including text-video retrieval (YouCook2, MSR-VTT and ActivityNet), video action step localization (CrossTask), video action segmentation (COIN)." (Abstract)

10) inferring_method
Answer: Inference is performed by computing and summing alignment scores from three different contrastive scoring functions between video and text representations.
Quotes:
- "During inference, we make the prediction by summing the alignment scores from all the three scoring functions." (Section 3.4. Objective)
- "we use our model to compute the similarity between each frame and the action step descriptions, which results in a score matrix." (Section 5.2. Other video-related tasks)

11) video_representation
Answer: Video clips are represented as a sequence of features from a video encoder (self-attention layers) applied to pre-extracted 2D/3D CNN embeddings, which are then averaged for sentence-level comparison.
Quotes:
- "Given the input video embeddings, video encoder starts with a linear layer to project them to the same dimension d as following self-attention layers. We denote the output of our video encoder for a video clip by a sequence of m features, v_f = {v_f1, ..., v_fm}." (Section 3.1. Framework)
- "Then, we average all tokens of a video clip to get v_i, and take the first '[CLS]' token for each text to get t_i." (Section 3.3. TACo: our approach)

12) model_pipeline
Answer: Video/text inputs → Separate video (self-attention) and language (BERT) encoders → Multi-modal fusion module (self-attention) → Trained with three contrastive losses → Output alignment scores for retrieval/localization.
Quotes:
- "As depicted in Fig. 1, our model has three components: Video encoding module . ... Language encoding module . ... Multi-modal fusion module . ... The above three components comprise our video-text alignment model which is then trained with the proposed token-aware cascade contrastive loss." (Section 3.1. Framework)

13) dataset
Answer: YouCook2, MSR-VTT, ActivityNet, Howto100M (for pretraining), CrossTask, and COIN.
Quotes:
- "In our experiments, we train and evaluate our model on the following established benchmarks: YouCook2, MSR-VTT, ActivityNet, Howto100M." (Section 4.1. Datasets)
- "we also evaluate the action step localization and action segmentation on CrossTask [61] and COIN [43], respectively." (Section 4.1. Datasets)

14) comparisons
Answer: Outperforms baselines like UniVL and MMT on text-video retrieval across YouCook2, MSR-VTT, and ActivityNet, achieving state-of-the-art results (e.g., R@1 of 30.4 on ActivityNet vs. MMT's 28.7).
Quotes:
- "The results show that our models attain consistent improvements across different experimental settings over previous methods, setting new state-of-the-art on three public text-video retrieval benchmarks of YouCook2, MSR-VTT and ActivityNet." (Abstract)
- "As we can see, our model using S3D-HM outperforms UniVL [33] using the same feature but more video encoder layers (6)." (Section 5.1.2 Comparing with state-of-the-art)
- "we enrich our video representation by adding 2D R-152 feature, which achieves better performance on MSR-VTT, and better Recall@1 and Median Rank on ActivityNet." (Table 8, Section 5.1.2 Comparing with state-of-the-art)

15) hyperparameters
Answer: Batch size: 128 (training) or 64 (pretraining); LR: 1e-4 (training) or 5e-5 (pretraining); Optimizer: Adam; Iterations: 30k (training), 500k (pretraining); Hard negatives: 8; Max tokens: 48 video/30 text.
Quotes:
- "We train the model for 30k iterations with batch size 128. For each training sample, we use our cascade sampling strategy to sample 8 hard negatives. We use Adam [24] as the optimizer with initial learning rate 1e-4." (Section 4.3. Training on separate datasets)
- "We train the model for 500k iterations with batch size 64, and also sample 8 hard negatives for each sample... optimizer with initial learning rate 5e-5." (Section 4.3. Pretraining and finetuning)
- "For YouCook2 and MSR-VTT, the maximum number of video and text tokens are set to 48 and 30, respectively. For paragraph-video retrieval on ActivityNet, we set them both to 256." (Section 4.3. Implementation details)

16) environment
Answer: Not mentioned.
Quotes: None.

17) repository
Answer: Not mentioned.
Quotes: None.

18) authors
Answer: The authors are from Microsoft Research and Carnegie Mellon University; the venue and year are not stated.
Quotes:
- "Jianwei Yang Microsoft Research" (Page 1)
- "Yonatan Bisk Carnegie Mellon University" (Page 1)
- "Jianfeng Gao Microsoft Research" (Page 1)

video_segmentation.answer: Videos are divided into equally sized, non-overlapping segments of 32 frames. For pseudo-summary generation, these segments are further merged into "steps" based on dot-product similarity if it exceeds a heuristic threshold.
quotes: For a given video, we divide it into N equally sized non-overlapping segments si , i ∈ [1, . . . N ] and embed each segment using a pre-trained 3D CNN video encoder gvid []." (Section 3.1)
We merge segments along the time axis based on their dot-product similarity... We call these merged segments steps, as they typically correspond to semantic steps..." (Section 3.1)
For generating pseudo summaries and for training IV-Sum, the videos are down-sampled to 8 FPS, and divided into non-overlapping segments of size 32 frames..." (Supplementary Materials, Section 8)

frame_sampling_method.answer: Videos are uniformly down-sampled to 8 FPS. During training, a contiguous sequence of 28 segments (896 frames) is randomly sampled from a chosen start location in each video.
quotes: For generating pseudo summaries and for training IV-Sum, the videos are down-sampled to 8 FPS..." (Supplementary Materials, Section 8)

While training IV-Sum, we fix the number of segments sampled from a video to be 28 (i.e. 896 frames) which are selected as a contiguous sequence from a randomly chosen start location." (Supplementary Materials, Section 8)

input_video_length.answer: The model processes long instructional videos. The training dataset has an average video length of 3.09 minutes. Whether any processed video is over 60 minutes is not mentioned.
quotes: In this work, we propose a method to create such succinct visual summaries from long instructional videos." (Section 1)
They span 263 different tasks, have an average length of 3.09 minutes, and in total comprise of 628.53 hours of content." (Section 4)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Yes, spatiotemporal analysis is performed using a 3D CNN (S3D) video encoder to learn context-aware temporal representations for segments, and a segment scoring transformer to contextualize information across multiple segments.
quotes: ...IV-Sum operates on non-overlapping segments, and learns context-aware temporal representations using a 3D CNN video encoder." (Section 3.2)

To contextualize information across several segments, we use a segment scoring encoder-only transformer [37] with positional embeddings, that assigns importance scores to each segment..." (Section 3.2)
For the video and text encoders, we use an S3D [] network, initialized with weights from pre-training on HowTo100M [] using the MIL-NCE loss []." (Section 5, Implementation Details)

visual_analysis.answer: The model is not visual-only; it uses both video and speech. The primary visual backbone is an S3D network. ResNet50 and CLIP are used for dataset creation and baselines, respectively.
quotes: Our method is weaklysupervised (it only requires the task labels for videos), multimodal – uses both video and speech transcripts, and is scalable to large online corpora of instructional videos." (Section 1)
For the video and text encoders, we use an S3D [] network, initialized with weights from pre-training on HowTo100M [] using the MIL-NCE loss []." (Section 5, Implementation Details)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Yes, the model uses speech content via Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) transcripts, which are encoded and concatenated with visual features to inform the summarization process.
quotes: ...subtitles for the video obtained using Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) will likely reference these key steps (cross-modal saliency)." (Section 3.1)
Next, given an input video and transcribed speech, we train an instructional video summarization network (IV-Sum)." (Section 1)
The transcript is projected onto the same embedding space using a text encoder, and the text representations are concatenated individually to each of the segments." (Section 3.2)

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned. The paper focuses exclusively on speech content via ASR transcripts and does not mention analyzing non-speech audio.

qa_interaction.answer: Not mentioned. The system is designed for automatic video summarization and does not include any interactive Q&A or chat capabilities.

retrieval_level.answer: The system returns a summarized video, which is constructed by selecting and concatenating the highest-scoring video segments from the original input video.
quotes: The summary is constructed by stitching together all the frames with label 1." (Section 3.2)
Finally, we concatenate the highest scoring segments to form the final video summary." (Section 1)
Creating a visual summary (abridged version of a video) provides viewers with a quick overview and massively reduces search time." (Abstract)

inferring_method.answer: The importance of activities is inferred by the IV-Sum model, which uses a 3D CNN and a segment scoring transformer to assign scores to video segments based on visual and speech transcript inputs.
quotes: IV-Sum learns to assign scores to short video segments using 3D video features which capture temporal context." (Section 1)

Our network consists of a video encoder that learns context-aware temporal representations for each segment and a segment scoring transformer (SST) that then assigns importance scores to each segment." (Section 1)

video_representation.answer: Video content is represented as a sequence of embeddings for non-overlapping segments, generated by a pre-trained S3D network that captures context-aware temporal representations.
quotes: ...embed each segment using a pre-trained 3D CNN video encoder gvid []." (Section 3.1)
IV-Sum operates on non-overlapping segments, and learns context-aware temporal representations using a 3D CNN video encoder." (Section 3.2)
For the video and text encoders, we use an S3D [] network, initialized with weights from pre-training on HowTo100M [] using the MIL-NCE loss []." (Section 5, Implementation Details)

model_pipeline.answer: Input video and ASR transcript → Video segments are encoded by S3D, concatenated with text embeddings, and fed to a segment scoring transformer → The transformer outputs importance scores, and high-scoring segments are concatenated to form the summary video.
quotes: It takes an input video along with the corresponding ASR transcript and learns to assign importance scores to each segment in the video." (Fig. 2 caption)

The network consists of a video encoder that learns context-aware temporal representations for each segment and a segment scoring transformer (SST) that then assigns importance scores to each segment." (Section 1)
The final summary is a compilation of the high scoring video segments." (Fig. 2 caption)

dataset.answer: For training, the paper creates a "Pseudo Summaries" dataset from COIN and CrossTask. For evaluation, it introduces the new "WikiHow Summaries" dataset. Comparisons are also made on instructional videos from TVSum.
quotes: We use the videos and task annotations from COIN [36] and CrossTask [45] datasets for creating our training datasets." (Section 4)

To evaluate our model, we collect a high-quality test set, WikiHow Summaries, by scraping WikiHow articles that contain video demonstrations and visual depictions of steps allowing us to obtain the ground-truth summaries." (Abstract)
Generic video summarization dataset TVSum [35] has 15 videos pertaining to the categories changing a car tire, getting a car unstuck, and making a sandwich..." (Supplementary Materials, Section 9)

comparisons.answer: The model is compared against CLIP-It and three unsupervised cross-modal similarity baselines on the WikiHow Summaries dataset. IV-Sum outperforms the best baseline (CLIP-It trained on pseudo summaries) with a 5.5 point gain in F-Score (67.3 vs 61.8).
quotes: We compare our method to several baselines, including CLIP-It [], the state-of-the-art on generic and query-focused video summarization." (Section 5)
IV-Sum | 67.3 | 0.101 | 0.212" vs "CLIP-It with ASR [trained on Pseudo] | 61.8 | 0.093 | 0.191" (Table 2, Test results)

hyperparameters.answer: The model was trained with a batch size of 24 for 300 epochs. The learning rate was 1e-3 for IV-Sum and 1e-4 for S3D fine-tuning, with 1e-4 weight decay. Videos were processed in 32-frame segments.
quotes: The network is trained using the Adam optimizer [], with learning rate of 0.01, and a batch size of 24. We use Distributed Data Parallel to train for 300 epochs..." (Section 5, Implementation Details)

Batch size | 24", "Epochs | 300", "Learning rate IV-Sum | 1e-3", "Learning rate S3D fine-tuning | 1e-4", "#frames per segment | 32", "#frames per video during training | 768" (Supplementary Materials, Table 4)

environment.answer: The model was trained for approximately 2 days on 8 NVIDIA RTX 2080 GPUs using Distributed Data Parallel.
quotes: We use Distributed Data Parallel to train for 300 epochs across 8 NVIDIA RTX 2080 GPUs." (Section 5, Implementation Details)
The training time is approximately 2 days using Distributed Data Parallel to train for 300 epochs on 8 NVIDIA RTX 2080 GPUs." (Supplementary Materials, Section 8)

quotes: We collect a high-quality test set, WikiHow Summaries for benchmarking instructional video summarization, which will be publicly released." (Section 1)

paper_file: Towards Fast Adaptation of Pretrained Contrastive Models for Multi-channel Video-Language Retrieval.md

video_segmentation.answer: Videos are processed by generating one feature vector for every 1.5-second segment.
quotes: The resulted video feature vector is at 1 vector per 1.5 seconds." (Section 3.4. Implementation Details)
we first directly use the pretrained video encoder to represent input video segment v as continuous feature vectors." (Section 3.2. a. Continuous Features + Multimodal Transformer)
We further apply a max pooling with kernel size as 5 to sub-sample the retrieved words to avoid too many repetitions of words of neighbouring segmenting." (Section 3.4. Implementation Details)

frame_sampling_method.answer: Not mentioned. The paper uses pretrained video encoders but does not specify their internal frame sampling methods.

input_video_length.answer: Not mentioned. The paper notes a sub-sampling strategy for videos that are "too long to fit the memory" but does not specify target or processed video durations.
quotes: We follow [40] to sub-sample video feature vectors when the video is too long to fit the memory." (Section 3.4. Implementation Details)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Yes, spatiotemporal analysis is performed using the S3D visual backbone, which is a 3D convolutional neural network.

visual_analysis.answer: The system is multimodal, using visual backbones S3D and the CLIP vision encoder to extract video features.
quotes: We use the pretrained models from [21] for F_V and F_T when not specified. When using CLIP [26], we follow FrozenBiLM [42]." (Section 3.4. Implementation Details)
Text + Text (Ours) | CLIP [26] | 0 | 0 | 36.9 | 41.4 | 92.4" (Table 2)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Yes, Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) text is optionally used as an additional text input channel alongside the question.
quotes: *+ ASR* indicates the use of ASR texts of the video as additional inputs." (Table 2)

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned.

qa_interaction.answer: No, the system performs retrieval-style question answering from a candidate pool, not interactive chat.
quotes: In open-ended video question answering, given a video v and question t as input, the model is required to retrieve the correct answer from a large answer corpus." (Section 3.1. Preliminaries)
retrieval-style ] accomplishes the tasks in a discriminative way, where discriminative embeddings are produced to select the best answer from an answer candidate pool." (Section 2. Related Work)

retrieval_level.answer: The system retrieves text answers from a corpus for video question answering tasks and retrieves videos for text-to-video retrieval tasks.
quotes: In open-ended video question answering, given a video v and question t as input, the model is required to retrieve the correct answer from a large answer corpus." (Section 3.1. Preliminaries)

In multi-channel text-video retrieval, given a text query a, the model is required to retrieve the most relevant video with associated speech text, which are from a corpus of multi-channel videos." (Section 3.1. Preliminaries)

inferring_method.answer: Inference is performed by a pretrained contrastive text transformer that fuses text tokens representing the video with the text query to produce a discriminative embedding for retrieval.
quotes: we follow the same text token retrieval process and then further simplify the model to use pretrained text model for modality fusion." (Section 3.2. d. Text Tokens + Text Transformer)
where the question t and all the retrieved words are concatenated as a whole text sequence and then input to the pretrained text model." (Section 3.2. d. Text Tokens + Text Transformer)

video_representation.answer: The paper explores two representations: continuous feature vectors from S3D or CLIP, and discrete text tokens (top-k nouns and verbs) retrieved from a vocabulary based on visual similarity.
quotes: To represent video information, we could either adopt Continuous Features which is commonly used in existing work ], or project videos into unified Text Tokens [] from various modalities." (Section 1. Introduction)
Then we compare the similarity between the video and each of the words to retrieve k most similar words" (Section 3.2. c. Text Tokens + Multimodal Transformer)

model_pipeline.answer: Video and text query → Video is converted to text tokens via a frozen video-text model, concatenated with the query, and fed to a contrastive text transformer → A final embedding is produced for retrieving an answer or video from a corpus.
quotes: Its fast adaptation ability not only comes from the ability of linking entities across modalities of the contrastive multimodal model, but it also enjoys the natural alignment with contrastive text models to produce discriminative embeddings." (Section 1. Introduction)

dataset.answer: The primary datasets are iVQA, How2QA, ActivityNet-QA, YouCook II, and VATEX. MSRVTT-QA and MSVD-QA were also used for analysis. No new datasets were created.
quotes: We select 5 commonly used multi-channel retrieval-style datasets: iVQA [40], How2QA [13], ActivityNet-QA [45], YouCook II [49], and VATEX [37] as the main evaluation datasets." (Section 4.1. Dataset and Evaluation Metric)
MSRVTT-QA and MSVD-QA [38]. These two datasets are automatically generated from video-caption pairs." (Section 4.1. Dataset and Evaluation Metric)

comparisons.answer: Compared to state-of-the-art models like FrozenBiLM on How2QA, the proposed Text Tokens + Text Transformer model achieves a 6.5% accuracy improvement (93.2% vs 86.7%) without requiring additional pretraining.

raw_output:
1) video_segmentation
Answer: Videos are processed by generating one feature vector for every 1.5-second segment.
Quotes:
- "The resulted video feature vector is at 1 vector per 1.5 seconds." (Section 3.4. Implementation Details)
- "we first directly use the pretrained video encoder to represent input video segment v as continuous feature vectors." (Section 3.2. a. Continuous Features + Multimodal Transformer)
- "We further apply a max pooling with kernel size as 5 to sub-sample the retrieved words to avoid too many repetitions of words of neighbouring segmenting." (Section 3.4. Implementation Details)

2) frame_sampling_method
Answer: Not mentioned. The paper uses pretrained video encoders but does not specify their internal frame sampling methods.
Quotes: None.

3) input_video_length
Answer: Not mentioned. The paper notes a sub-sampling strategy for videos that are "too long to fit the memory" but does not specify target or processed video durations.
Quotes:
- "We follow [40] to sub-sample video feature vectors when the video is too long to fit the memory." (Section 3.4. Implementation Details)

4) spatiotemporal_analysis
Answer: Yes, spatiotemporal analysis is performed using the S3D visual backbone, which is a 3D convolutional neural network.
Quotes:
- "VQA-T [41] | S3D [21] | 69M + 3M | 350 + 30 | 35.2 | 39.0 | 85.3" (Table 2)
- "Text + Text (Ours) | S3D [21] | 0 | 0 | 31.6 | 38.7 | 82.9" (Table 2)

5) visual_analysis
Answer: The system is multimodal, using visual backbones S3D and the CLIP vision encoder to extract video features.
Quotes:
- "We use the pretrained models from [21] for F_V and F_T when not specified. When using CLIP [26], we follow FrozenBiLM [42]." (Section 3.4. Implementation Details)
- "Text + Text (Ours) | S3D [21] | 0 | 0 | 31.6 | 38.7 | 82.9" (Table 2)
- "Text + Text (Ours) | CLIP [26] | 0 | 0 | 36.9 | 41.4 | 92.4" (Table 2)

6) speech_audio_analysis
Answer: Yes, Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) text is optionally used as an additional text input channel alongside the question.
Quotes:
- "*+ ASR* indicates the use of ASR texts of the video as additional inputs." (Table 2)
- "Text + Text (+ ASR, Ours) | S3D [21] | 0 | 0 | 36.8 | 38.8 | 84.6" (Table 2)

7) sound_audio_analysis
Answer: Not mentioned.
Quotes: None.

8) qa_interaction
Answer: No, the system performs retrieval-style question answering from a candidate pool, not interactive chat.
Quotes:
- "In open-ended video question answering, given a video v and question t as input, the model is required to retrieve the correct answer from a large answer corpus." (Section 3.1. Preliminaries)
- "retrieval-style ] accomplishes the tasks in a discriminative way, where discriminative embeddings are produced to select the best answer from an answer candidate pool." (Section 2. Related Work)

9) retrieval_level
Answer: The system retrieves text answers from a corpus for video question answering tasks and retrieves videos for text-to-video retrieval tasks.
Quotes:
- "In open-ended video question answering, given a video v and question t as input, the model is required to retrieve the correct answer from a large answer corpus." (Section 3.1. Preliminaries)
- "In multi-channel text-video retrieval, given a text query a, the model is required to retrieve the most relevant video with associated speech text, which are from a corpus of multi-channel videos." (Section 3.1. Preliminaries)

10) inferring_method
Answer: Inference is performed by a pretrained contrastive text transformer that fuses text tokens representing the video with the text query to produce a discriminative embedding for retrieval.
Quotes:
- "we follow the same text token retrieval process and then further simplify the model to use pretrained text model for modality fusion." (Section 3.2. d. Text Tokens + Text Transformer)
- "where the question t and all the retrieved words are concatenated as a whole text sequence and then input to the pretrained text model." (Section 3.2. d. Text Tokens + Text Transformer)

11) video_representation
Answer: The paper explores two representations: continuous feature vectors from S3D or CLIP, and discrete text tokens (top-k nouns and verbs) retrieved from a vocabulary based on visual similarity.
Quotes:
- "To represent video information, we could either adopt Continuous Features which is commonly used in existing work ], or project videos into unified Text Tokens [] from various modalities." (Section 1. Introduction)
- "Then we compare the similarity between the video and each of the words to retrieve k most similar words" (Section 3.2. c. Text Tokens + Multimodal Transformer)

12) model_pipeline
Answer: Video and text query → Video is converted to text tokens via a frozen video-text model, concatenated with the query, and fed to a contrastive text transformer → A final embedding is produced for retrieving an answer or video from a corpus.
Quotes:
- "Its fast adaptation ability not only comes from the ability of linking entities across modalities of the contrastive multimodal model, but it also enjoys the natural alignment with contrastive text models to produce discriminative embeddings." (Section 1. Introduction)

13) dataset
Answer: The primary datasets are iVQA, How2QA, ActivityNet-QA, YouCook II, and VATEX. MSRVTT-QA and MSVD-QA were also used for analysis. No new datasets were created.
Quotes:
- "We select 5 commonly used multi-channel retrieval-style datasets: iVQA [40], How2QA [13], ActivityNet-QA [45], YouCook II [49], and VATEX [37] as the main evaluation datasets." (Section 4.1. Dataset and Evaluation Metric)
- "MSRVTT-QA and MSVD-QA [38]. These two datasets are automatically generated from video-caption pairs." (Section 4.1. Dataset and Evaluation Metric)

14) comparisons
Answer: Compared to state-of-the-art models like FrozenBiLM on How2QA, the proposed Text Tokens + Text Transformer model achieves a 6.5% accuracy improvement (93.2% vs 86.7%) without requiring additional pretraining.
Quotes:
- "Most encouragingly, our model also outperforms the state-of-the-art FrozenBiLM on How2QA (+6.5%) and iVQA (+0.5%), which uses 10 million samples for secondround 'pretraining' and uses a much larger text model." (Section 4.3. Comparison with state-of-the-art on accuracyefficiency trade-off)
- "FrozenBiLM [42] (+ ASR) | CLIP [26] | 10M | 160 | 39.6 | 43.2 | 86.7" (Table 2)
- "Text + Text (+ ASR, Ours) | CLIP [26] | 0 | 0 | 40.2 | 41.4 | 93.2" (Table 2)

15) hyperparameters
Answer: The model uses k=15 retrieved words per video vector, a max pooling kernel size of 5 for words, and a multimodal transformer with 2 blocks and 512/768 embedding dimension. Other details were deferred to supplementary material.
Quotes:
- "We use k=15 words retrieved from the vocabulary for each feature vector in the video." (Section 3.4. Implementation Details)
- "We further apply a max pooling with kernel size as 5 to sub-sample the retrieved words to avoid too many repetitions of words of neighbouring segmenting." (Section 3.4. Implementation Details)
- "we use the same shallow multimodal transformer as in [40], which has 2 transformer blocks with 512/768 as the embedding dimension." (Section 3.4. Implementation Details)

16) environment
Answer: The model requires 0.5 GPU hours for training, but the specific type of GPU is not mentioned.
Quotes:
- "Note that our variant typically requires 0.5 GPU hours for training." (Table 2)
- "As shown in Table 2, with three orders of magnitude smaller amount GPU training time, our model can actually achieve comparable or even better results than the state-of-the-art." (Section 4.3. Comparison with state-of-the-art on accuracyefficiency trade-off)

17) repository
Answer: A GitHub repository is provided for the code.
Quotes:
- "The code will be released at https://github.com/ XudongLinthu / upgradable - multimodal intelligence to facilitate future research." (Section 1. Introduction)

18) authors
Answer: Authors are from Columbia University, UIUC, and National University of Singapore. The paper appears to be a preprint from late 2022, with no conference or journal venue mentioned.
Quotes:
- "Xudong Lin1 Simran Tiwari1 , Shiyuan Huang1 , Manling Li2 Mike Zheng Shou3 , Heng Ji2 , Shih-Fu Chang1" (Title Block)

paper_file: Towards Holistic Language-video Representation - the language model-enhanced MSR-Video to Text Dataset.md

video_segmentation.answer: The paper uses the pre-segmented video clips from the MSR-VTT dataset, which are each 10 to 20 seconds long, and does not apply any new segmentation methods.
quotes: MSR-VTT o!ers in total 10,000 video clips each lasting 10 to 20 seconds." (Section 3.1 The MSR-VTT Dataset)

frame_sampling_method.answer: Frames are sampled at a rate of 2 frames per second for both tone captioning with EmoNet and style captioning via K-means dominant color detection.
quotes: Our approach for this model consists of extracting 2 frames per second and annotating the emotion of each frame, then counting the total number of times for each emotion to appear." (Section 3.2.3 Tone Captionning)

In our case, after extracting two frames per second from the video, we use K-means to cluster the pixels of each image into two clusters, which will represent the dominant colours of the image frame in RGB format." (Section 3.2.4 Style Captioning)

input_video_length.answer: The paper processes short video clips from the MSR-VTT dataset, with each clip's duration being between 10 to 20 seconds; no videos longer than 60 minutes are mentioned.
quotes: MSR-VTT o!ers in total 10,000 video clips each lasting 10 to 20 seconds." (Section 3.1 The MSR-VTT Dataset)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Spatiotemporal analysis is performed for tone detection using EmoNet, which utilizes a 3D CNN to extract features from facial images and an RNN to model temporal dynamics.
quotes: In this work, we implement EmoNet?? for tone detection in videos. It is a deep learning-based framework for estimating continuous valence and arousal levels from faces in naturalistic conditions. this approach utilizes a 3D CNN to extract features from facial images and an RNN to model the temporal dynamics of facial expressions." (Section 3.2.3 Tone Captionning)

visual_analysis.answer: The paper performs visual-only analysis using multiple models: BLIP for sequential image captioning, EmoNet for tone detection from faces, and K-means clustering for dominant color extraction.
quotes: The task of getting the main visual annotation covering the plot, setting, and characters is converted into a sequential image captioning task." (Section 3.2.1 Visual Captioning)
In this work, we implement EmoNet?? for tone detection in videos." (Section 3.2.3 Tone Captionning)
For this, we employ the K-means clustering algorithm, a powerful tool for grouping data points based on similarity." (Section 3.2.4 Style Captioning)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Speech content is analyzed using Whisper's "small model" to perform automatic speech recognition (ASR), transcribing multilingual audio from videos into English text.
quotes: To transcribe multilingual audio into English, we #rst extract the audio from the video using AudioSegment1. Then, we employ Whisper[27] to identify the language of the audio and transcribe it with the provided "small model"." (Section 3.2.2 Dialogue Captioning)

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned. The paper only discusses audio processing in the context of speech transcription.

qa_interaction.answer: Not mentioned. The paper uses Llama 2 to generate descriptions in a pipeline but does not describe any interactive Q&A or chat capabilities.

retrieval_level.answer: The system is evaluated on a text-to-video retrieval task, where the retrieval level is returning entire video clips in response to a text query.
quotes: We select the popular downstream task, text-to-video retrieval, to test the actual improvement of a more detailed and holistic annotation in the training dataset for language-video representation learning." (Section 4 EXPERIMENT)

inferring_method.answer: Narrative elements are inferred by combining outputs from expert models (BLIP, Whisper, EmoNet, K-means) which are then synthesized into a factual description by the Llama 2 model using a rule-based prompting strategy.
quotes: With the visual, dialogue, tone, and style annotations obtained, we resort to using language to automatically combine them to generate a comprehensive and accurate video description." (Section 3.3 Language Model Enhancement)

After comparing the outcomes of each prompting strategy, we determined that employing the rule strategy would deliver the most desirable results for our video caption generation task as it is in the right length and with the proper amount of factual details." (Section 3.3.4 Rule Example)

video_representation.answer: Video content is represented via multi-modal retrieval models (MMT, HCQ, T2VLAD) that use specialized "expert" encoders to extract and fuse features from modalities like scene, face, motion, and audio.
quotes: Some works, such as MMT[10] and MDMMT[15], have studied the importance of including multi-modal video features. Di!erent "Experts" are incorporated for these modalities, with each "Expert" being a specialized encoder to extract features from a certain modality." (Section 2.3 Multi-modal Text-video Representation)

Most multi-modal models focus on extracting multi-modal representations with di!erent extractors (also known as experts) and then fuse them to obtain the #nal video features for matching with the text side features." (Section 4.1 Model Selection)

model_pipeline.answer: Video clips → Expert models (BLIP, Whisper, EmoNet, K-means) extract visual, dialogue, tone, and style features → A Llama 2 agent synthesizes these features into a holistic text description.
quotes: the pipeline utilizes separate models to extract key elements that are essential for constructing the holistic description from di!erent modalities and aspects of the video, including visual, dialogue, tone, and style captioning." (Section 3.2 Multifacet Video Captioning)

With the visual, dialogue, tone, and style annotations obtained, we resort to using language to automatically combine them to generate a comprehensive and accurate video description." (Section 3.3 Language Model Enhancement)

dataset.answer: The paper uses the MSR-VTT dataset and creates an enhanced version, which it refers to as Holistic-MSR-VTT, by generating new, multifaceted annotations.
quotes: The base dataset we used for this work is MSR-VTT (Microsoft Research Video to Text)." (Section 3.1 The MSR-VTT Dataset)

paper_file: Traffic Video Event Retrieval via Text Query using Vehicle Appearance and Motion Attributes.md

video_segmentation.answer: Not mentioned. The method processes pre-segmented video tracks of individual vehicles provided by the AI City Challenge 2021 dataset.
quotes: For testing in this track, there are 530 video tracks, each contains the sequence of bounding boxes of a vehicle of interest, and 530 sets of text descriptions." (Section 1)

frame_sampling_method.answer: For training the attribute classifiers, four frames are sampled from each track at proportional intervals: the first, one-third, two-thirds, and the last frame.
quotes: About the training images, for a given track with T frames and T temporal bounding boxes, we sample four boxes: [B0, BT /3, B2T /3, BT −1] to handle this problem." (Section 3.3.1)

input_video_length.answer: Not mentioned. The paper processes video tracks of unspecified length from the AI City Challenge 2021 dataset.
quotes: In the CityFlow-NL dataset, each query contains three different captions in text and may provide information from various aspects for a specific track. And the video is not segmented as the COOT setting." (Section 3.4)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Yes, motion is analyzed via a trajectory-based action detector for stop/turn events and by adapting the COOT model, which uses transformers to model temporal relationships between frame features.
quotes: Our method analyzes the trajectory of a vehicle, a sequence of points p1, p2, ..., pn, where pi is the center of the vehicle's bounding box at the i th frame." (Section 3.3.2)
The original COOT aims to tackle the long-range temporal problem in video-text representation learning." (Section 3.4)

visual_analysis.answer: Yes, the analysis is visual-only (paired with text), using EfficientNet-b5 for vehicle/color classifiers and ResNet-152 for extracting global frame features.
quotes: We build two modules, color encoder Ecol and vehicle encoder Eveh, with EfficientNet [17] backbone to learn the visual features representation for each target vehicle from the video tracks." (Section 3.3.1)
For the vglobal, we apply the same approach as in COOT, which is the global feature extracted by the ResNet-152 pretrained on ImageNet [8]." (Section 3.4)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned.

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned.

qa_interaction.answer: Not mentioned. The system performs text-to-video retrieval based on a fixed query, not interactive Q&A.
quotes: The objective in this track is to return a rank list of video tracks corresponding to each text query." (Section 1)

retrieval_level.answer: The system returns a ranked list of video tracks corresponding to a text query.
quotes: The objective in this track is to return a rank list of video tracks corresponding to each text query." (Section 1)

inferring_method.answer: Vehicle type/color are inferred using EfficientNet-b5 classifiers, while motion (stop/turn) is inferred using a rule-based analysis of the vehicle's trajectory based on speed and algebraic area.
quotes: We build two modules, color encoder Ecol and vehicle encoder Eveh, with EfficientNet [17] backbone to learn the visual features representation for each target vehicle from the video tracks." (Section 3.3.1)
We use algebraic area to classify the motion direction of a vehicle's trajectory into 3 categories: 'go straight', 'turn right', and 'turn left'." (Section 3.3.2)

We consider a stop event occurs when the speed is significant small, comparing to the average speed. We choose a simple yet efficient formula to detect a stop event if the motion speed vi < α × mean{vi} where α = 0.15." (Section 3.3.2)

quotes: Let v_frame_i denotes the feature vector for each frames in a video track, i ∈ [1, F]. We define this feature as a combination of three sources: Global context information (vglobal). Attributes representation (vveh). Target vehicle location (vloc)." (Section 3.4)

model_pipeline.answer: Text queries and video tracks → Attribute extraction (SRL, classifiers, trajectory analysis) and feature encoding (BERT, ResNet) → Modified COOT model generates embeddings for similarity matching → A ranked list of videos is produced and refined using extracted attributes.

Then we utilize a representation learning-based retrieval model 3.4 to produce a list of candidate tracks for each query. dataset.answer: The dataset of Track 5 in AI City Challenge 2021, also referred to as the CityFlow-NL benchmark.

We apply the idea from the state-of-the-art COOT model with some modification for the CityFlow-NL benchmark [5]." (Section 3.4)

comparisons.answer: An ablation study compares four internal configurations (V1-V4), with the baseline being a raw COOT model. The final model (V4) is compared against other teams on the AI City Challenge 2021 Track 5, achieving 6th place with a 0.1560 MRR score.
quotes: In V1, we use the raw COOT model with default configurations... Then we refine the V1 result on color and vehicle type attribute... to obtain V2... we choose the best retrieval model with the highest validation score and apply all the refinement methods to produce the final version, V4." (Section 4.2.1)
The V4 version is our best result for Track 5 of AI City Challenge 2021, which gains a 0.156 MRR score, 0.2321, and 0.3302 for R@5, R@10 respectively." (Section 4.2.2)

hyperparameters.answer: Not mentioned. The paper specifies some model architecture dimensions and parameters for the action detector, but not training hyperparameters like learning rate, batch size, or epochs.
quotes: To detect a stop event, we first calculate a sequence of motion speed vi = dist(pi+k, pi) where dist is the Euclidean distance and k = 5 in our implementation." (Section 3.3.2)
For the global context vglobal, we use ResNet152 [8] pretrained on ImageNet [8] to extract 2048-d feature vector for each frame." (Section 4.1)

Following the same approach of the COOT model, paragraphs are encoded by a pretrained BERT-based model [2] and extract the last 2 layers to construct the final representation for each token as 1536-d features." (Section 4.1)

raw_output:
1) video_segmentation
Answer: Not mentioned. The method processes pre-segmented video tracks of individual vehicles provided by the AI City Challenge 2021 dataset.
Quotes:
- "For testing in this track, there are 530 video tracks, each contains the sequence of bounding boxes of a vehicle of interest, and 530 sets of text descriptions." (Section 1)

2) frame_sampling_method
Answer: For training the attribute classifiers, four frames are sampled from each track at proportional intervals: the first, one-third, two-thirds, and the last frame.
Quotes:
- "About the training images, for a given track with T frames and T temporal bounding boxes, we sample four boxes: [B0, BT /3, B2T /3, BT −1] to handle this problem." (Section 3.3.1)

3) input_video_length
Answer: Not mentioned. The paper processes video tracks of unspecified length from the AI City Challenge 2021 dataset.
Quotes:
- "In the CityFlow-NL dataset, each query contains three different captions in text and may provide information from various aspects for a specific track. And the video is not segmented as the COOT setting." (Section 3.4)

4) spatiotemporal_analysis
Answer: Yes, motion is analyzed via a trajectory-based action detector for stop/turn events and by adapting the COOT model, which uses transformers to model temporal relationships between frame features.
Quotes:
- "Our method analyzes the trajectory of a vehicle, a sequence of points p1, p2, ..., pn, where pi is the center of the vehicle's bounding box at the i th frame." (Section 3.3.2)
- "The original COOT aims to tackle the long-range temporal problem in video-text representation learning." (Section 3.4)

5) visual_analysis
Answer: Yes, the analysis is visual-only (paired with text), using EfficientNet-b5 for vehicle/color classifiers and ResNet-152 for extracting global frame features.
Quotes:
- "We build two modules, color encoder Ecol and vehicle encoder Eveh, with EfficientNet [17] backbone to learn the visual features representation for each target vehicle from the video tracks." (Section 3.3.1)
- "For the vglobal, we apply the same approach as in COOT, which is the global feature extracted by the ResNet-152 pretrained on ImageNet [8]." (Section 3.4)

6) speech_audio_analysis
Answer: Not mentioned.
Quotes: None.

7) sound_audio_analysis
Answer: Not mentioned.
Quotes: None.

8) qa_interaction
Answer: Not mentioned. The system performs text-to-video retrieval based on a fixed query, not interactive Q&A.
Quotes:
- "The objective in this track is to return a rank list of video tracks corresponding to each text query." (Section 1)

9) retrieval_level
Answer: The system returns a ranked list of video tracks corresponding to a text query.
Quotes:
- "The objective in this track is to return a rank list of video tracks corresponding to each text query." (Section 1)

10) inferring_method
Answer: Vehicle type/color are inferred using EfficientNet-b5 classifiers, while motion (stop/turn) is inferred using a rule-based analysis of the vehicle's trajectory based on speed and algebraic area.
Quotes:
- "We build two modules, color encoder Ecol and vehicle encoder Eveh, with EfficientNet [17] backbone to learn the visual features representation for each target vehicle from the video tracks." (Section 3.3.1)
- "We use algebraic area to classify the motion direction of a vehicle's trajectory into 3 categories: 'go straight', 'turn right', and 'turn left'." (Section 3.3.2)
- "We consider a stop event occurs when the speed is significant small, comparing to the average speed. We choose a simple yet efficient formula to detect a stop event if the motion speed vi < α × mean{vi} where α = 0.15." (Section 3.3.2)

11) video_representation
Answer: Videos are represented by concatenating frame-level features, which combine global context (ResNet-152), vehicle/color attributes (EfficientNet), and location information, processed by a modified COOT model to create a final embedding.
Quotes:
- "Let v_frame_i denotes the feature vector for each frames in a video track, i ∈ [1, F]. We define this feature as a combination of three sources: Global context information (vglobal). Attributes representation (vveh). Target vehicle location (vloc)." (Section 3.4)

12) model_pipeline
Answer: Text queries and video tracks → Attribute extraction (SRL, classifiers, trajectory analysis) and feature encoding (BERT, ResNet) → Modified COOT model generates embeddings for similarity matching → A ranked list of videos is produced and refined using extracted attributes.
Quotes:
- "With a query and list of video tracks, our method first extracts essential features in both branches... Then we utilize a representation learning-based retrieval model 3.4 to produce a list of candidate tracks for each query. The final result is re-ranked by a refinement block 3.5, using the extracted attributes from previous steps." (Section 3.1)

13) dataset
Answer: The dataset of Track 5 in AI City Challenge 2021, also referred to as the CityFlow-NL benchmark.
Quotes:
- "Experiments with the test dataset of Track 5 in AI City Challenge 2021 show that our method is among the top 6 with a score of 0.1560." (Abstract)
- "We apply the idea from the state-of-the-art COOT model with some modification for the CityFlow-NL benchmark [5]." (Section 3.4)

14) comparisons
Answer: An ablation study compares four internal configurations (V1-V4), with the baseline being a raw COOT model. The final model (V4) is compared against other teams on the AI City Challenge 2021 Track 5, achieving 6th place with a 0.1560 MRR score.
Quotes:
- "In V1, we use the raw COOT model with default configurations... Then we refine the V1 result on color and vehicle type attribute... to obtain V2... we choose the best retrieval model with the highest validation score and apply all the refinement methods to produce the final version, V4." (Section 4.2.1)
- "The V4 version is our best result for Track 5 of AI City Challenge 2021, which gains a 0.156 MRR score, 0.2321, and 0.3302 for R@5, R@10 respectively." (Section 4.2.2)

15) hyperparameters
Answer: Not mentioned. The paper specifies some model architecture dimensions and parameters for the action detector, but not training hyperparameters like learning rate, batch size, or epochs.
Quotes:
- "To detect a stop event, we first calculate a sequence of motion speed vi = dist(pi+k, pi) where dist is the Euclidean distance and k = 5 in our implementation." (Section 3.3.2)
- "For the global context vglobal, we use ResNet152 [8] pretrained on ImageNet [8] to extract 2048-d feature vector for each frame." (Section 4.1)
- "Following the same approach of the COOT model, paragraphs are encoded by a pretrained BERT-based model [2] and extract the last 2 layers to construct the final representation for each token as 1536-d features." (Section 4.1)
