paper_file: Type-to-Track -  Retrieve Any Object via Prompt-based Tracking.md

video_segmentation.answer: Not mentioned. The paper uses pre-existing video sequences from standard tracking datasets without describing any additional video segmentation process.

frame_sampling_method.answer: Not mentioned. The paper implies frame-by-frame processing for tracking but does not specify any frame sampling method or rate.
quotes: Let V be a video sample lasts t frames, where and be the image sample at a particular time step t." (Section 3.2)

input_video_length.answer: The paper uses short video sequences from datasets like MOT17 and TAO, which typically last from seconds to a few minutes; no videos longer than 60 minutes are mentioned.
quotes: The MOT17 dataset comprises 14 sequences with diverse environmental conditions such as crowded scenes, varying viewpoints, and camera motion." (Section 3.1)
OxUvA spans 14 hours of video in 337 videos, comprising 366 object tracks." (Section 2.1)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Yes, the MENDER model is an auto-regressive framework that models temporal changes by using tracklet information from the previous frame to predict object locations in the current frame.
quotes: an **auto-regressive single-stage end-to-end framework** can be formulated via third-order tensor modeling." (Section 4.1, Remark 1)
The tracklet set is defined in the *auto-regressive* manner to adjust to the movements of the object being tracked as in Eqn. (8)." (Section 4.2)
To model the track's temporal changes, our network learns from training samples that capture both appearance and motion generated by two adjacent frames" (Section 4.3)

visual_analysis.answer: The model uses a ResNet-101 backbone followed by a Deformable DETR encoder to extract visual features, but it is not a visual-only model as it also processes text prompts.
quotes: is implemented using a ResNet-101 [66] as the backbone to extract visual features from the input image." (Section 5.1)
The output of the ResNet is processed by a Deformable DETR encoder [67] to generate visual tokens." (Section 5.1)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned. The paper focuses exclusively on visual and textual modalities.

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned. The paper focuses exclusively on visual and textual modalities.

qa_interaction.answer: Not mentioned. The system is described as "responsive" but lacks true Q&A capabilities, which is noted as a limitation.
quotes: It allows for more intuitive and conversational tracking, as users can simply type in the name or description of the object they wish to track, as illustrated in Fig. 1." (Section 1)

Additionally, the lack of the question-answering task in data and problem formulation may limit the algorithm to not being able to provide language feedback such as clarification or alternative suggestions." (Section 6, Limitations)

retrieval_level.answer: The system returns tracklets, which are bounding boxes with unique identities for the described objects, tracked frame-by-frame through the video sequence.
quotes: ...the system is able to discriminate appearance attributes to track the target subjects accordingly and iteratively responds to the user's tracking request. Each box color represents a unique identity." (Figure 1)
...the decoder transforms the object representation by a 3-layer FFN to predict bounding boxes and confidence scores for frame t" (Section 4.2)

inferring_method.answer: Inference is performed by the MENDER model, a transformer-based framework that uses multi-head cross-attention to correlate visual features from a ResNet-101 with textual prompt embeddings from RoBERTa to predict object tracklets.
quotes: a new transformer-based eMbed-ENcoDE-extRact framework (*MENDER*) is introduced with third-order tensor decomposition as the first efficient approach for this task." (Section 1)
In our approach, the correlation operation is modelled by the *multi-head cross-attention* mechanism [57], as depicted in Fig. 4(b)." (Section 4.2)

video_representation.answer: Video content is represented as visual tokens from a ResNet-101 and Deformable DETR encoder, while text prompts are represented as embeddings from RoBERTa, with the system correlating these representations.
quotes: is implemented using a ResNet-101 [66] as the backbone to extract visual features from the input image. The output of the ResNet is processed by a Deformable DETR encoder [67] to generate visual tokens." (Section 5.1)
utilizes RoBERTa [65] to convert the text input into a sequence of numerical tokens. The tokens are fed into the RoBERTa-base model for text encoding..." (Section 5.1)

model_pipeline.answer: Video frames and a text prompt are input to the MENDER model, which uses a ResNet-101/Deformable DETR for visual features and RoBERTa for text embeddings, correlating them via cross-attention to output tracked object bounding boxes.
quotes: The structure of our proposed *MENDER*. It employs a visual backbone to extract visual features and a word embedding to extract textual features. We model the tracklet-prompt correlation instead of the region-prompt to avoid unnecessary computation caused by no-object tokens [56]." (Figure 4b caption)

dataset.answer: The paper introduces a new dataset, GroOT, created from MOT17, TAO, and MOT20 videos with new textual annotations, and uses RefCOCO+ and Flickr30k for pre-training.
quotes: We present a new Grounded Multiple Object Tracking dataset named GroOT that is more advanced than existing tracking datasets [6, 7]." (Section 1)
To cover a diverse range of scenes, *GroOT* was created using official videos and bounding box annotations from the MOT17 [], TAO [] and MOT20 []." (Section 3.1)
RefCOCO+ [63] and Flickr30k [64] serve as pre-trained datasets for acquiring a vocabulary of visual-textual concepts [37]." (Section 5.1)

comparisons.answer: MENDER is compared to a two-stage baseline (MDETR + TrackFormer) on the GroOT dataset, showing up to a 14.7% gain in CA-MOTA and 4x speed improvement, and also to SOTA trackers like ByteTrack on the MOT17 benchmark.
quotes: The experiments in five scenarios show that our *MENDER* approach outperforms another two-stage design in terms of accuracy and efficiency, up to 14.7% accuracy and 4× speed faster." (Abstract)
We use current SOTA methods to develop this approach, i.e., MDETR [36] for the grounded detector, while TrackFormer [4] for the object tracker." (Section 5.3)

Table 6 is the comparison on the *category name* setting on the official leaderboard of MOT17, comparing our proposed *MENDER* with other state-of-the-art approaches, including ByteTrack [69] and TrackFormer [4]." (Section 5.4)

hyperparameters.answer: Not mentioned. The paper specifies model architecture details like feature dimension D=512 and loss coefficients (0.3), but does not state training hyperparameters like learning rate, batch size, or epochs.
quotes: A feature resizer combining a list of (Linear, LayerNorm, Dropout) is used to map to size D=512 for all token producers." (Section 5.1)
where , , and are corresponding coefficients, which are set to 0.3 by default." (Section 4.3)

environment.answer: Inference speed was measured on a single NVIDIA RTX 3060 12GB GPU, and the Arkansas High-Performance Computing Center provided GPUs for the research.
quotes: The performance is reported with frame per second (FPS), which is self-measured on one GPU NVIDIA RTX 3060 12GB." (Section 5.2)
We also thank Utsav Prabhu and Chi-Nhan Duong for their invaluable discussions and suggestions and acknowledge the Arkansas High-Performance Computing Center for providing GPUs." (Acknowledgment)

repository.answer: Yes, a project page link is provided: uark-cviu.github.io/Type-to-Track.
quotes: uark-cviu.github.io/Type-to-Track" (Page 1)

paper_file: Understanding Co-speech Gestures in-the-wild.md

video_segmentation.answer: Video clips are randomly sampled to be between 2-10 seconds in length, using the start and end times of word boundaries from the transcriptions.
quotes: Using the start-end time of the word boundaries, we randomly sample a video clip between 2-10 seconds in length." (Section 3.8)

frame_sampling_method.answer: All videos are resampled to a uniform 25 FPS.
quotes: We resample all videos to 25 FPS, and the speech to 16kHz." (Section 3.9)

input_video_length.answer: The model processes short video clips randomly sampled to be 2-10 seconds long; no videos longer than 60 minutes are mentioned.
quotes: Using the start-end time of the word boundaries, we randomly sample a video clip between 2-10 seconds in length." (Section 3.8)
avg. clipduration (s)" (Table 1, showing all dataset average clip durations are under 16 seconds)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Spatiotemporal analysis is performed using a stack of 3D convolution layers with a 5-frame temporal receptive field, followed by a stack of Transformer encoder layers to model motion.
quotes: Given a gesture clip of T frames, we encode it using a stack of 3D convolution layers, similar to previous audio-visual networks [4, 38, 39], where the first layer has a temporal receptive field of 5 frames to capture the motion information." (Section 3.2)
These feature vectors are further encoded using a stack of Transformer encoder layers to get Vg." (Section 3.2)

visual_analysis.answer: The model is not visual-only; it uses a 3D CNN backbone initialized from GestSync for visual analysis, with the face region masked out to prevent learning from lip movements.
quotes: we encode it using a stack of 3D convolution layers... We initialize the backbone weights from GestSync [23] and keep it frozen." (Section 3.2)
We also make sure to mask out the face region to avoid leakage from lip movements." (Section 3.9)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Speech content is analyzed by encoding mel-spectrograms with a 2D-CNN and using WhisperX to obtain word-aligned text transcripts.
quotes: Given a speech waveform S, we convert it into melspectrograms which is encoded using a stack of 2D-CNN layers following previous works [4, 14]." (Section 3.4)
We leverage WhisperX [9] in cases where datasets lack word-aligned text transcripts." (Section 3.9)

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned. The paper focuses on co-speech audio and does not discuss non-speech sound analysis.

qa_interaction.answer: Not mentioned. The paper does not describe any interactive Q&A or chat capabilities.

retrieval_level.answer: The system retrieves gesture clips given a speech/text query (and vice-versa) by ranking embeddings in a gallery based on cosine similarity.
quotes: Given a gallery of gesture-speech-text samples, the task is to retrieve a gesture clip given a speech segment and/or text and vice-versa. Concretely, given a speech or text as query, we obtain a speech-text embedding, and rank the gesture embeddings in the gallery by cosine similarity, highest being at the top." (Section 4.1)

inferring_method.answer: Gestured words are inferred by computing the cosine similarity between a word's speech-text embedding and all frame-level gesture embeddings to localize the word in the video.
quotes: To localize the i-th word, wi, we compute the cosine similarity of the word embedding ci with all the gesture frame embeddings. The localization of the word in the video is simply obtained by keeping only the locations with similarity scores > τ." (Section 4.2)

video_representation.answer: Video content is represented through a learned joint tri-modal embedding space for gestures, audio, and language, using modality-specific encoders (3D CNN, 2D CNN, Roberta XLM-Base) and a fusion block.
quotes: we propose a model termed Joint Embedding space for Gestures, Audio, and Language (JEGAL) that facilitates matching gestures to words and phrases in the accompanying speech." (Section 1)
The three input modalities (video, text, speech) are each encoded with a modality-specific encoder, followed by a fusion block to merge speech and text representations." (Figure 2)

model_pipeline.answer: Video, speech, and text inputs are processed by separate encoders, fused, and trained with global phrase contrastive and local gesture-word coupling losses to produce joint embeddings for retrieval, spotting, and speaker detection.
quotes: The three input modalities (video, text, speech) are each encoded with a modality-specific encoder, followed by a fusion block to merge speech and text representations... The two losses encourage the learning of global and local correspondences between the three modalities." (Figure 2)

dataset.answer: The model is trained on the PATS and a subset of the MultiVSR datasets, and evaluated on three newly curated benchmarks (AVS-Ret, AVS-Spot, AVS-Asd) from the AVSpeech dataset.
quotes: We train our model using the following datasets: (i) PATS [22], and (ii) a subset of the MultiVSR dataset [40]." (Section 3.9)
Using the AVSpeech official test set [18], we manually curate three separate evaluation benchmarks for the three downstream gesture tasks." (Section 4.4)

comparisons.answer: The model is compared against Clip4Clip, Language-Bind, GestSync, and GestureDiffuCLIP on the AVS-Ret, AVS-Spot, and AVS-Asd benchmarks, showing superior performance, such as achieving 18.80 R@5 on retrieval versus the best fine-tuned baseline's 10.00.
quotes: For baselines, we report performance of zero-shot pre-trained vision-language models [29, 64] and pre-trained GestSync [23]... In addition, we compare with the semantic encoder of GestureDiffuCLIP [8], by training it on our dataset." (Section 5.1)
JEGAL | 1 | 1 | 18.80 | 30.80 | 46.40 | 62.00 | 31.0" (Table 2, showing JEGAL's performance on Speech-text to Gesture retrieval)

hyperparameters.answer: Key hyperparameters include an AdamW optimizer with a 1e-4 learning rate, 1e-4 weight decay, 224x224 pixel frame inputs, and Transformer heads with a 512 hidden dimension and 8 attention heads.
quotes: For the gesture frame inputs, we resize the frames to 224 × 224 pixels." (Section 3.8)
We use the AdamW optimizer [28] with a learning rate of 1e-4, weight decay of 1e-4 and betas (0.9,0.98)." (Section 3.8)
The Transformer uses a hidden dimension of 512 and a feed-forward dimension of 2048 with 8 attention heads." (Section 3.8)

raw_output:
1) video_segmentation
Answer: Video clips are randomly sampled to be between 2-10 seconds in length, using the start and end times of word boundaries from the transcriptions.
Quotes:
- "Using the start-end time of the word boundaries, we randomly sample a video clip between 2-10 seconds in length." (Section 3.8)

2) frame_sampling_method
Answer: All videos are resampled to a uniform 25 FPS.
Quotes:
- "We resample all videos to 25 FPS, and the speech to 16kHz." (Section 3.9)

3) input_video_length
Answer: The model processes short video clips randomly sampled to be 2-10 seconds long; no videos longer than 60 minutes are mentioned.
Quotes:
- "Using the start-end time of the word boundaries, we randomly sample a video clip between 2-10 seconds in length." (Section 3.8)
- "avg. clipduration (s)" (Table 1, showing all dataset average clip durations are under 16 seconds)

4) spatiotemporal_analysis
Answer: Spatiotemporal analysis is performed using a stack of 3D convolution layers with a 5-frame temporal receptive field, followed by a stack of Transformer encoder layers to model motion.
Quotes:
- "Given a gesture clip of T frames, we encode it using a stack of 3D convolution layers, similar to previous audio-visual networks [4, 38, 39], where the first layer has a temporal receptive field of 5 frames to capture the motion information." (Section 3.2)
- "These feature vectors are further encoded using a stack of Transformer encoder layers to get Vg." (Section 3.2)

5) visual_analysis
Answer: The model is not visual-only; it uses a 3D CNN backbone initialized from GestSync for visual analysis, with the face region masked out to prevent learning from lip movements.
Quotes:
- "we encode it using a stack of 3D convolution layers... We initialize the backbone weights from GestSync [23] and keep it frozen." (Section 3.2)
- "We also make sure to mask out the face region to avoid leakage from lip movements." (Section 3.9)

6) speech_audio_analysis
Answer: Speech content is analyzed by encoding mel-spectrograms with a 2D-CNN and using WhisperX to obtain word-aligned text transcripts.
Quotes:
- "Given a speech waveform S, we convert it into melspectrograms which is encoded using a stack of 2D-CNN layers following previous works [4, 14]." (Section 3.4)
- "We leverage WhisperX [9] in cases where datasets lack word-aligned text transcripts." (Section 3.9)

7) sound_audio_analysis
Answer: Not mentioned. The paper focuses on co-speech audio and does not discuss non-speech sound analysis.
Quotes: None.

8) qa_interaction
Answer: Not mentioned. The paper does not describe any interactive Q&A or chat capabilities.
Quotes: None.

9) retrieval_level
Answer: The system retrieves gesture clips given a speech/text query (and vice-versa) by ranking embeddings in a gallery based on cosine similarity.
Quotes:
- "Given a gallery of gesture-speech-text samples, the task is to retrieve a gesture clip given a speech segment and/or text and vice-versa. Concretely, given a speech or text as query, we obtain a speech-text embedding, and rank the gesture embeddings in the gallery by cosine similarity, highest being at the top." (Section 4.1)

10) inferring_method
Answer: Gestured words are inferred by computing the cosine similarity between a word's speech-text embedding and all frame-level gesture embeddings to localize the word in the video.
Quotes:
- "To localize the i-th word, wi, we compute the cosine similarity of the word embedding ci with all the gesture frame embeddings. The localization of the word in the video is simply obtained by keeping only the locations with similarity scores > τ." (Section 4.2)

11) video_representation
Answer: Video content is represented through a learned joint tri-modal embedding space for gestures, audio, and language, using modality-specific encoders (3D CNN, 2D CNN, Roberta XLM-Base) and a fusion block.
Quotes:
- "we propose a model termed Joint Embedding space for Gestures, Audio, and Language (JEGAL) that facilitates matching gestures to words and phrases in the accompanying speech." (Section 1)
- "The three input modalities (video, text, speech) are each encoded with a modality-specific encoder, followed by a fusion block to merge speech and text representations." (Figure 2)

12) model_pipeline
Answer: Video, speech, and text inputs are processed by separate encoders, fused, and trained with global phrase contrastive and local gesture-word coupling losses to produce joint embeddings for retrieval, spotting, and speaker detection.
Quotes:
- "The three input modalities (video, text, speech) are each encoded with a modality-specific encoder, followed by a fusion block to merge speech and text representations... The two losses encourage the learning of global and local correspondences between the three modalities." (Figure 2)

13) dataset
Answer: The model is trained on the PATS and a subset of the MultiVSR datasets, and evaluated on three newly curated benchmarks (AVS-Ret, AVS-Spot, AVS-Asd) from the AVSpeech dataset.
Quotes:
- "We train our model using the following datasets: (i) PATS [22], and (ii) a subset of the MultiVSR dataset [40]." (Section 3.9)
- "Using the AVSpeech official test set [18], we manually curate three separate evaluation benchmarks for the three downstream gesture tasks." (Section 4.4)

14) comparisons
Answer: The model is compared against Clip4Clip, Language-Bind, GestSync, and GestureDiffuCLIP on the AVS-Ret, AVS-Spot, and AVS-Asd benchmarks, showing superior performance, such as achieving 18.80 R@5 on retrieval versus the best fine-tuned baseline's 10.00.
Quotes:
- "For baselines, we report performance of zero-shot pre-trained vision-language models [29, 64] and pre-trained GestSync [23]... In addition, we compare with the semantic encoder of GestureDiffuCLIP [8], by training it on our dataset." (Section 5.1)
- "JEGAL | 1 | 1 | 18.80 | 30.80 | 46.40 | 62.00 | 31.0" (Table 2, showing JEGAL's performance on Speech-text to Gesture retrieval)

15) hyperparameters
Answer: Key hyperparameters include an AdamW optimizer with a 1e-4 learning rate, 1e-4 weight decay, 224x224 pixel frame inputs, and Transformer heads with a 512 hidden dimension and 8 attention heads.
Quotes:
- "For the gesture frame inputs, we resize the frames to 224 × 224 pixels." (Section 3.8)
- "We use the AdamW optimizer [28] with a learning rate of 1e-4, weight decay of 1e-4 and betas (0.9,0.98)." (Section 3.8)
- "The Transformer uses a hidden dimension of 512 and a feed-forward dimension of 2048 with 8 attention heads." (Section 3.8)

16) environment
Answer: Not mentioned.
Quotes: None.

17) repository
Answer: The dataset, model, and code are available at https://www.robots.ox.ac.uk/~vgg/research/jegal.
Quotes:
- "The dataset, model, and code are available at: https://www.robots.ox.ac.uk/˜vgg/research/jegal." (Abstract)

18) authors
Answer: The authors are from the Visual Geometry Group, Department of Engineering Science, University of Oxford.
Quotes:
- "Sindhu B Hegde\*, K R Prajwal\*, Taein Kwon, Andrew Zisserman Visual Geometry Group, Dept. of Engineering Science, University of Oxford" (Title Block)

paper_file: Unified Static and Dynamic Network - Efficient Temporal Filtering for Video Grounding.md

video_segmentation.answer: Videos are uniformly sampled into a fixed number of clips (e.g., 16, 64, or 128) depending on the dataset and visual feature extractor used.
quotes: For data preparation, we evenly sample 64 and 128 video clips for ActivityNet Captions dataset with C3D features, and 16, 16, and 64 video clips for the Charades-STA dataset with VGG, C3D, and I3D features, respectively." (Section 5.1, Hyperparameter Settings)

frame_sampling_method.answer: Not mentioned. The paper uses pre-extracted clip-level features and does not specify the underlying frame sampling method.

input_video_length.answer: The method processes untrimmed videos with average durations ranging from 30.60s to 286.59s; no processed videos are mentioned to be ≥ 60 minutes long.
quotes: The average video duration is counted in Table 2 with the datasets ActivityNet, Charades-STA, and TACoS of 117.60s, 30.60s, and 286.59s, respectively." (Section 4.3)
TACoS (Cooking dataset) has the longest video length (approx. 5 min) and the highest number of events (>100)per video" (Section 5.2.2)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Yes, spatiotemporal analysis is performed using a Dynamic Temporal Filtering Network (DTFNet), a GCN-based architecture that models context correlations between video clips using a multi-kernel Temporal Gaussian Filter.
quotes: For the dynamic modeling, we design a Dynamic Temporal Filtering Network (DTFNet) based on a Gaussian filtering GCN architecture to capture more useful contextual information in the video sequence" (Section 3, Introduction)

DTFNet is a 2-layer graph network with a dynamic Gaussian filtering convolution mechanism, which is designed to control message passing between nodes by considering temporal distance and semantic relevance as the Gaussian filtering clues when updating node features." (Figure 3, Caption)

visual_analysis.answer: The paper does not use a visual-only modality as it is a cross-modal task, but it employs pre-extracted visual features from C3D, VGG, and I3D backbones.
quotes: For a fair comparison, we utilize the same video features provided by 2D-TAN [11], which includes 500-dim C3D feature [48] on ActivityNet Captions, 4096-dim VGG feature [64] on Charades-STA, and 500-dim C3D feature on TACoS from [9]." (Section 5.1, Implementation Details)

Besides, there are currently other popular C3D feature and I3D feature [65] available on Charades-STA, so we also use the 4096-dim C3D feature from [18] and 1024-dim I3D feature provided by [19]." (Section 5.1, Implementation Details)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Yes, speech audio is analyzed for the Spoken Language Video Grounding (SLVG) task using the Data2vec model to extract audio sequence embeddings.
quotes: For the audio feature, we use the HuggingFace [66] implementation of Data2vec [50] with pre-trained model "facebook/data2vecaudio-base-960h" for SLVG." (Section 5.1, Implementation Details)

Specifically, we set the audio sampling rate to 16,000 Hz, and use the python audio standard library "librosa" to read the original audio and input it into the Data2vec model to obtain the audio sequence embedding." (Section 5.1, Implementation Details)

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned. The paper focuses on spoken language queries and does not discuss the analysis of non-speech audio.

qa_interaction.answer: Not mentioned. The system performs video grounding from a single query and does not have interactive Q&A or chat capabilities.

retrieval_level.answer: The system retrieves temporal boundaries (start and end timestamps) by generating and ranking candidate moment proposals from a 2D temporal map.
quotes: The goal of the NLVG (natural language video grounding) and SLVG (spoken language video grounding) is to predict the temporal boundary of the specific moment in the video in response to a given query in text or audio modality." (Section 3.1)
Finally, we perform semantic matching between the queries and proposals and rank the best ones as the predictions." (Figure 3, Caption)

inferring_method.answer: The model infers the target moment by learning cross-modal associations between queries and video clips using a Static Semantic Supplement Network (S3Net) and a Dynamic Temporal Filtering Network (DTFNet) to rank proposals.
quotes: Our UniSDNet comprises the *Static Semantic Supplement Network* (S3Net) and Dynamic Temporal Filtering Network (DTFNet). It adopts a two-stage information aggregation strategy, beginning with a global interaction mode to perceive all multimodal information, followed by a graph filter to purify key visual information." (Section 3.1)

video_representation.answer: Videos are represented as a sequence of pre-extracted clip-level embeddings (C3D, VGG, I3D), which are refined by the model and used to generate a 2D temporary proposal map representing all candidate moments.
quotes: Denote the input video as V = {v_t}^T_{t=1} ∈ R^{D_v×T}, where D_v and T are the feature dimension and total number of video clips, respectively." (Section 3.1)

After obtaining the updated video clip features from the above DTFNet module, we implement the moment sampling [11] on the features to generate a 2D temporary proposal map that indicates all candidate moments" (Section 3.4)

model_pipeline.answer: Pre-extracted video and query features → Static S3Net (ResMLP) for global interaction → Dynamic DTFNet (GCN) for temporal filtering → 2D proposal map generation and ranking → Predicted temporal boundary.
quotes: The architecture of the Unified Static and Dynamic Network (UniSDNet). It mainly consists of static and dynamic networks: Static Semantic Supplement Network (S³Net) and Dynamic Temporal Filtering Network (DTFNet)." (Figure 3, Caption)

S³Net concatenates video clips and multiple queries into a sequence and encodes them through a lightweight single-stream ResMLP network. DTFNet is a 2-layer graph network with a dynamic Gaussian filtering convolution mechanism..." (Figure 3, Caption)

The role of 2D temporal map is to retain possible candidate proposals... Finally, we perform semantic matching between the queries and proposals and rank the best ones as the predictions." (Figure 3, Caption)

dataset.answer: The paper uses ActivityNet Captions, Charades-STA, TACoS, and QVHighlights for NLVG, and ActivityNet Speech for SLVG. It also introduces two new datasets: Charades-STA Speech and TACoS Speech.
quotes: There are three classic benchmarks for NLVG task, i.e., ActivityNet Captions [57], Charades-STA [3], and TACoS [46] datasets." (Section 4)
To accelerate SLVG development, we collect two new Speech datasets: Charades-STA Speech and TACoS Speech based on the original Charades-STA and TACoS datasets." (Section 4)
Charades-STA Speech dataset is available at https://zenodo.org/records/8019213 and TACoS Speech dataset is available at https://zenodo.org/records/8022063" (Section 4.2, Footnote 1)
We also validate our model on the most recently publicized NLVG dataset QVHighlights [38] for multi-tasks" (Section 5.6)

comparisons.answer: The model is compared to baselines like 2D-TAN, MMN, and PTRM on datasets including ActivityNet Captions and TACoS, reporting state-of-the-art results such as a 10.31% gain in R@1, IoU@0.5 over PTRM.
quotes: Compared to the PTRM approach that employs multiquery training, our UniSDNet exhibits notable accuracy enhancement, with an increase of 10.31% in R@1, IoU@0.5." (Section 5.4)
our proposed UniSDNet-M achieves 10. 31% performance gain on the R@1, IoU@0.5 metric while being 1.56× faster than multi-query training SOTA methods PTRM [] and MMN []" (Section 1, Introduction)

hyperparameters.answer: For ActivityNet Captions: batch size 12, LR 1e-4, 15 epochs, 64 clips. S3Net has 2 layers/1024 hidden size; DTFNet has 4 layers/256 hidden size. NMS threshold is 0.5.
quotes: For ActivityNet Captions and TACoS datasets, the learning rate and batch size are set to 1e-4 and 12, respectively." (Section 5.1, Training and Inference Settings)
We train the model... with the upper-limit of 15 epochs on ActivityNet Captions and Charades-STA datasets" (Section 5.1, Training and Inference Settings)
Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) threshold is 0.5 during inference." (Section 3.5)
ActivityNet Captions (C3D) | #Clips 64 | Static S 3 Net Hidden size 1024 #Layers 2 | Dynamic DTFNet Hidden size 256 #Layers 4 | 2D Proposal Generation Kernel size 9 Hidden size 512" (Table 3)

environment.answer: All experiments were conducted on a single GeForce RTX 2080Ti GPU; training duration is not specified, but inference speed is reported as 0.009 s/query.
quotes: All experiments are conducted with a GeForce RTX 2080Ti GPU." (Section 5.1, Training and Inference Settings)
it is evident that our UniSDNet offers moderate parameters and exhibits the fastest inference speed 0.009 s/query, regardless of the training mode (single-query or multi-query)." (Section 5.4)

repository.answer: A code repository is available at https://github.com/xian-sh/UniSDNet.
quotes: Code is available at: https://github.com/xian-sh/UniSDNet." (Abstract)

authors.answer: The authors are affiliated with Hefei University of Technology (HFUT), Anhui University, and the University of Science and Technology of China. The publication venue and year are not explicitly mentioned.
quotes: J. Hu, D. Guo, K. Li, and M. Wang are with Key Laboratory of Knowledge Engineering with Big Data (HFUT), Ministry of Education, School of Computer Science and Information Engineering (School of Artificial Intelligence), Hefei University of Technology (HFUT)..." (Page 1, Footnote)

- "Z. Si is with the Department of Chemistry and Centre for Atomic Engineering of Advanced Materials, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui 230601, P.R. China" (Page 1, Footnote)

paper_file: Unified Video-Language Pre-training with Synchronized Audio.md

video_segmentation.answer: Not mentioned. The paper uses pre-existing video clips from standard datasets without describing any new video segmentation method.

frame_sampling_method.answer: Randomly sampling 8 frames per video clip.
quotes: For each video clip, we randomly sample 8 frames as inputs and resize each frame to 224x224, i.e., V = 8, H = W = 224." (Section 4.1 Implementation)

input_video_length.answer: Not mentioned. The paper uses video clips but does not specify their duration or mention processing videos longer than 60 minutes.

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: A unified transformer encoder aggregates patch-level features from a sequence of 8 frames, implicitly modeling spatiotemporal interactions via self-attention.
quotes: VLSA aggregates local patches and global token representations for each modality through a joint transformer." (Section 1 Introduction)
With local-patch representations for each modality, we first apply an unified transformer encoder to aggregate patch-level features from the raw input..." (Section 3.2 Local-Patch Masked Modeling)

visual_analysis.answer: The model is tri-modal (video, text, audio), using a ViT-base model as the shared backbone for visual feature extraction from video frames.
quotes: We use a ViT-base [77] model for the masked autoencoder same as in MAE [75]." (Section 4.1 Implementation)
Following prior work [75, 76], we apply a patch size of 16x16 for both audio and video frames." (Section 4.1 Implementation)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned. The model uses provided text transcripts and analyzes general audio spectrograms, without performing ASR or using speech-specific audio models.

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Yes, the model processes general audio by converting waveforms into log-frequency spectrograms and using them as input to the unified transformer.
quotes: For each audio waveform, we follow the prior work [74] and sub-sample the audio signal to 11kHz. A Short-time Fourier transform... is further applied to generate a Time-Frequency representation of the audio, which is resampled to a log-frequency scale... as the input audio spectrogram" (Section 4.1 Implementation)

qa_interaction.answer: Not mentioned. The paper focuses on cross-modal retrieval tasks and does not describe any interactive question-answering or chat capabilities.

retrieval_level.answer: The system retrieves video clips or audio clips by computing the cosine similarity between global query and item embeddings.
quotes: We conduct extensive experiments on retrieval across text, video, and audio." (Section 1 Introduction)
During inference, we simply compute the cosine-similarity s_tv, s_ta, s_va for retrieval across text-video, text-audio, and video-audio settings." (Section 3.3 Global Audio Matching)

inferring_method.answer: Not mentioned. The model learns joint embeddings for retrieval but does not perform explicit inference of roles or activities.

video_representation.answer: Video content is represented by global embeddings generated by a unified transformer, which averages the local patch-level features of sampled frames.
quotes: ...we propose a novel and explicit global audio matching mechanism on global embeddings for each modality in the unified transformer to generate global modality-aware features..." (Section 3.3 Global Audio Matching)
That is, we average local-level patches for three modalities along each total number of patches (VI, S, A) to generate the modality-aware global embeddings." (Section 3.3 Global Audio Matching)

model_pipeline.answer: Video frames, text, and audio spectrograms are tokenized into patches, processed by a unified transformer encoder, and trained with local-patch masking and global audio matching to produce modality-specific global embeddings for cross-modal retrieval.
quotes: The modality-aware patch embeddings are extracted from each linear projection layer. The Local-Patch Masked Modeling module is applied... Finally, the Global Audio Matching module... is leveraged on modality-aware global embeddings... to capture the cross-modal alignment..." (Figure 2)

dataset.answer: Pre-trained on an intersection of HowTo100M and AudioSet (0.9M triplets), and evaluated on MSR-VTT, LSMDC, AudioCaps, SoundDescs, and Yoocook2.
quotes: An intersection of HowTo100M and AudioSet with 0.9M audio-video-text triplets is used for pre-training." (Section 4.1 Datasets)

MSR-VTT [70] includes 10K YouTube videos... LSMDC [71] contains 118,081 video clips... AudioCaps [72] is filtered out for 49,291 clips... SoundDescs [55] consists of 32,979 audio clips... Yoocook2 [73] comprises 13K video clips..." (Section 4.1 Datasets)

comparisons.answer: Compared to baselines like AllinOne, TVLT, and Frozen on MSR-VTT, achieving gains such as +5.0 R@1 over AllinOne and +5.1 R@1 over TVLT.
quotes: When pre-trained on the same amount of data, the proposed VLSA achieves performance gains of 5.0 R@1, 8.2 R@5, and 8.3 R@10, compared to AllinOne [35], the unified video-language pre-training model." (Section 4.2 Comparison to Prior Work)

In particular, the proposed VLSA significantly outperforms TVLT [58], the only video-audio pre-training approach with a single transformer, where we achieve the performance gains of 5.1 R@1." (Section 4.2 Comparison to Prior Work)

hyperparameters.answer: Pre-training: batch size 2048, LR 1e-4, 200k steps. Fine-tuning: batch size 256, 20 epochs. Input: 8 frames, 224x224 resolution, 16x16 patch size.
quotes: The model is trained for 200k steps with the AdamW [78] optimizer with a learning rate of 1e-4, a decay rate of 0.01, and a batch size of 2048." (Section 4.1 Implementation)
For fine-tuning, the model is trained for 20 epochs with a batch size of 256." (Section 4.1 Implementation)
For each video clip, we randomly sample 8 frames as inputs and resize each frame to 224x224... we apply a patch size of 16x16 for both audio and video frames." (Section 4.1 Implementation)

environment.answer: Not mentioned. The paper does not specify the hardware (e.g., GPUs/TPUs) or the total training time.

repository.answer: Not mentioned. The paper does not provide any links to code or data repositories.

authors.answer: The authors are affiliated with CMU, MBZUAI, and Xiaohongshu. The publication venue and year are not stated.
quotes: Shentong Mo1,2∗ Haofan Wang3 Huaxia Li3 Xu Tang3 1CMU, 2MBZUAI, 3Xiaohongshu" (Title Block)

paper_file: Use What You Have - Video Retrieval Using Representations From Collaborative Experts.md

video_segmentation.answer: Not mentioned. The paper uses pre-existing video clips from standard datasets without describing how they were segmented.

frame_sampling_method.answer: Not mentioned. The paper states that features are extracted at a frame-level and then aggregated, but does not specify a sampling rate or method.
quotes: These are first extracted at a frame-level, then aggregated to produce a single feature vector per modality per video." (Section 4.1)
Each frame is passed through an SSD face detector [4, 35] to extract bounding boxes..." (Section 4.1)
Each frame is passed through the Pixel Link [10] text detection model to extract bounding boxes for text..." (Section 4.1)

input_video_length.answer: Not mentioned. The paper refers to "video clips" and "video segments" from standard retrieval datasets but does not specify their duration or any length constraints.

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Yes, motion embeddings are generated using 3D CNN models I3D and R(2+1)D to capture temporal dynamics.
quotes: Motion embeddings are generated using the I3D inception model [6] and a 34-layer R(2+1)D model [56] trained on IG-65m [19]." (Section 4.1)

visual_analysis.answer: No, the model is multi-modal; visual backbones include SENet-154, ResNext-101 for objects, I3D and R(2+1)D for motion, and ResNet50 for faces.
quotes: RGB 'object' frame-level embeddings of the visual data are generated with two models: an SENet-154 model [24] (pretrained on ImageNet for the task of image classification), and a ResNext-101 [60] pretrained on Instagram hashtags [37]." (Section 4.1)
Motion embeddings are generated using the I3D inception model [6] and a 34-layer R(2+1)D model [56] trained on IG-65m [19]." (Section 4.1)

Face embeddings are extracted in two stages: ... (2) The image region of each box is passed through a ResNet50 [22] that has been trained for the task of face classification on the VGGFace2 dataset [5]." (Section 4.1)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Yes, speech-to-text features are extracted using the Google Cloud speech API and then encoded with word2vec embeddings.
quotes: Speech-to-Text features are extracted using the Google Cloud speech API, to extract word tokens from the audio stream, which are then encoded via pretrained word2vec embeddings [41]." (Section 4.1)

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Yes, non-speech audio embeddings are generated using a VGGish model trained on the YouTube-8m dataset.
quotes: Audio embeddings are obtained with a VGGish model, trained for audio classification on the YouTube-8m dataset [23]." (Section 4.1)

qa_interaction.answer: Not mentioned. The system performs one-shot text-to-video retrieval without any interactive or conversational capabilities.

retrieval_level.answer: The system retrieves ranked video clips by computing cosine similarity between pre-computed video embeddings and a text query embedding.
quotes: our objective is to be able to retrieve video clips using a free form text query that may contain both general and specific information." (Section 1)
Our joint video embeddings are independent of the retrieval text-query and can be pre-computed offline and indexed for efficient retrieval" (Section 1)
When assessing retrieval performance, at test time the embedding distances are simply computed via their inner product, as described above." (Section 3.2)

inferring_method.answer: Roles and activities are implicitly captured by combining embeddings from a collection of pre-trained expert models for tasks like action recognition, object detection, and scene classification.
quotes: the discriminative content of the multi-modal video embedding can be well approximated by the set of semantic representations of the video data learnt by individual experts (in audio, scenes, actions, etc)." (Section 1)

a framework that seeks to make effective use of embeddings from different 'experts' (e.g. objects, actions, speech) by learning their combination in order to render them more discriminative." (Section 1)

video_representation.answer: A single, compact fixed-length vector is created by aggregating and combining embeddings from multiple pre-trained experts (e.g., motion, object, audio, speech, OCR) using a collaborative gating module.
quotes: Our goal is to condense the multi-modal, extremely high dimensional information from videos into a single, compact video representation for the task of video retrieval using free-form text queries..." (Abstract)

We propose the Collaborative Experts framework for learning a joint embedding of video and text by combining a collection of pretrained embeddings into a single, compact video representation." (Section 1)

model_pipeline.answer: Multi-modal video data and a text query are fed into separate encoders; pre-trained experts extract video features which are fused by a collaborative gating module into a single embedding, then compared against the text embedding for retrieval.
quotes: The proposed Collaborative Experts framework for learning a joint video-text embedding... The information provided by each pretrained 'expert' ... is temporally aggregated as it enters the video encoder and then refined through the use of a collaborative gating mechanism ... to obtain the video-embedding" (Figure 2 Caption)

dataset.answer: MSR-VTT, LSMDC, MSVD, DiDeMo, and ActivityNet-captions were used for experiments; a new resource with code and data is provided.
quotes: We perform experiments on five video datasets: MSR-VTT [61], LSMDC [50], MSVD [8], DiDeMo [1] and ActivityNet-captions [32]..." (Section 4.1)
Code and data can be found at www.robots.ox.ac.uk/~vgg/research/collaborative-experts/." (Abstract)

comparisons.answer: The Collaborative Experts (CE) model was compared against baselines like MoEE, JSFusion, and VSE++ on MSR-VTT, LSMDC, MSVD, DiDeMo, and ActivityNet, showing improved R@K and rank metrics.
quotes: Driven by strong expert features, we observe that Collaborative Experts (CE) consistently improves retrieval performance for both sentence and video queries." (Section 4.2)
CE | 1k-A | 20.9±1.2 | 48.8±0.6 | 62.4±0.8 | 6±0 | 28.2±0.8" (Table 1, showing R@1 of 20.9 vs 10.2 for JSFusion on MSR-VTT)
we observe that CE matches or outperforms all prior work, including the prior state-of-the-art method [39]..." (Section 4.2, on LSMDC)

hyperparameters.answer: Not mentioned. The paper states that optimization settings and hyperparameters are described in an appendix, which is not included in the provided text.
quotes: Optimisation settings and the hyperparameter selection procedure is described in the appendix." (Section 4.1)

environment.answer: Not mentioned. The paper does not specify the hardware (GPUs/TPUs) or the time required for training.
quotes: We would also like to thank Zak Stone and Susie Lim for their help with cloud computing." (Acknowledgements)

repository.answer: Yes, a link to code and data is provided: www.robots.ox.ac.uk/~vgg/research/collaborative-experts/.
quotes: Code and data can be found at www.robots.ox.ac.uk/~vgg/research/collaborative-experts/." (Abstract)

authors.answer: The authors are from the Visual Geometry Group at the University of Oxford, UK; the paper is from 2019.
quotes: yang Liu\*, Samuel Albanie\*, Arsha Nagrani\*, Andrew Zisserman" (Authors list)
Visual Geometry Group University of Oxford UK" (Affiliation)
© 2019. The copyright of this document resides with its authors." (Page 1 Footer)

paper_file: VALOR Vision-Audio-Language Omni-Perception Pretraining Model and Dataset.md

video_segmentation.answer: Uses pre-segmented 10-second video clips from the AudioSet dataset for pretraining and splits audio waveforms into 5-second clips.
quotes: AudioSet contains over 2 million 10-second video clips excised from YouTube videos and each video is labeled from 527 audio classes, according to a hierarchical ontology." (Section 3.1)
Given an audio waveform, we split it into multiple 5 seconds long audio clips and random sample clips as input." (Section 4.1, Audio Encoder)

frame_sampling_method.answer: Sparsely samples frames from video clips, specifically sampling one video frame per video during pretraining.
quotes: For video inputs, we sparsely sample frames from a video clip, and use patch embedding layers to encode patches." (Section 4.1, Vision Encoder)
For each video, we sample 1 video frame and 1 audio clip during pretraining." (Section 5.1.3)

input_video_length.answer: Pretrained on short video clips around 10 seconds long sourced from the AudioSet dataset; no processing of videos ≥ 60 minutes is mentioned.
quotes: AudioSet contains over 2 million 10-second video clips excised from YouTube videos..." (Section 3.1)
...VALOR only saw short videos (around 10s) during pretraining." (Section 5.2.1)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Models temporal interactions using VideoSwin Transformer, which contains temporal window attention, while also using a CLIP encoder that processes frames independently.
quotes: We have tried two vision encoders including CLIP [37] and VideoSwin Transformer [67]." (Section 4.1, Vision Encoder)
Frames are independently passed through CLIP encoder, while make interactions via temporal window attention in VideoSwin Transformer." (Section 4.1, Vision Encoder)

visual_analysis.answer: Uses vision as a key modality in a multimodal system, not visual-only, employing CLIP and VideoSwin Transformer as visual backbones.
quotes: We have tried two vision encoders including CLIP [37] and VideoSwin Transformer [67]. Both models can take image or video singals as input." (Section 4.1, Vision Encoder)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned. The paper explicitly contrasts its approach with methods that rely on ASR transcriptions and human speech.
quotes: Even if some video-language datasets like HowTo100M [] and HD VILA 100M [] contain audio modality, their audios are limited to human speech with less diversity, and their texts are ASR transcriptions instead of objective descriptions, which are overlapped with speech." (Section 1)

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Analyzes non-speech audio using an Audio Spectrogram Transformer (AST) pretrained on AudioSet to encode general audio events.

raw_output:
1) video_segmentation
Answer: Uses pre-segmented 10-second video clips from the AudioSet dataset for pretraining and splits audio waveforms into 5-second clips.
Quotes:
- "AudioSet contains over 2 million 10-second video clips excised from YouTube videos and each video is labeled from 527 audio classes, according to a hierarchical ontology." (Section 3.1)
- "Given an audio waveform, we split it into multiple 5 seconds long audio clips and random sample clips as input." (Section 4.1, Audio Encoder)

2) frame_sampling_method
Answer: Sparsely samples frames from video clips, specifically sampling one video frame per video during pretraining.
Quotes:
- "For video inputs, we sparsely sample frames from a video clip, and use patch embedding layers to encode patches." (Section 4.1, Vision Encoder)
- "For each video, we sample 1 video frame and 1 audio clip during pretraining." (Section 5.1.3)

3) input_video_length
Answer: Pretrained on short video clips around 10 seconds long sourced from the AudioSet dataset; no processing of videos ≥ 60 minutes is mentioned.
Quotes:
- "AudioSet contains over 2 million 10-second video clips excised from YouTube videos..." (Section 3.1)
- "...VALOR only saw short videos (around 10s) during pretraining." (Section 5.2.1)

4) spatiotemporal_analysis
Answer: Models temporal interactions using VideoSwin Transformer, which contains temporal window attention, while also using a CLIP encoder that processes frames independently.
Quotes:
- "We have tried two vision encoders including CLIP [37] and VideoSwin Transformer [67]." (Section 4.1, Vision Encoder)
- "Frames are independently passed through CLIP encoder, while make interactions via temporal window attention in VideoSwin Transformer." (Section 4.1, Vision Encoder)

5) visual_analysis
Answer: Uses vision as a key modality in a multimodal system, not visual-only, employing CLIP and VideoSwin Transformer as visual backbones.
Quotes:
- "We have tried two vision encoders including CLIP [37] and VideoSwin Transformer [67]. Both models can take image or video singals as input." (Section 4.1, Vision Encoder)

6) speech_audio_analysis
Answer: Not mentioned. The paper explicitly contrasts its approach with methods that rely on ASR transcriptions and human speech.
Quotes:
- "Even if some video-language datasets like HowTo100M [] and HD VILA 100M [] contain audio modality, their audios are limited to human speech with less diversity, and their texts are ASR transcriptions instead of objective descriptions, which are overlapped with speech." (Section 1)

7) sound_audio_analysis
Answer: Analyzes non-speech audio using an Audio Spectrogram Transformer (AST) pretrained on AudioSet to encode general audio events.
Quotes:
- "Audio spectrogram transformer (AST) [68], [69] pretrained on AudioSet is used as audio encoder." (Section 4.1, Audio Encoder)

8) qa_interaction
Answer: Supports open-ended question answering by formulating it as a generative, autoregressive task, but no interactive chat capabilities are mentioned.
Quotes:
- "For question answering tasks (AVQA, VQA, AQA), we formulate them as generative problem, so answers can be predicted from the whole vocabulary instead of predefined top-k high frequency answer candidate sets." (Section 4.3)
- "The answer inference process is also autoregressive." (Section 4.3)

9) retrieval_level
Answer: Returns ranked videos or audio clips to the user based on computed similarity scores between the query and all candidates.
Quotes:
- "For retrieval tasks (AVR, VR, AR), we use LMGA(T −AV ) , LMGA(T −V ) , LMGA(T −A) as training objective, respectively, and multimodal decoder is not used. At inference time, we compute the similarity scores between each query and all candidates through Eqn. 3, and rank all candidates." (Section 4.3)

10) inferring_method
Answer: Infers activities and concepts end-to-end via learned multimodal representations from alignment (MGA) and generative (MGC) pretraining tasks, without explicit rules.
Quotes:
- "We propose Multimodal Grouping Alignment (MGA) and Multimodal Grouping Captioning (MGC) tasks to conduct unified vision-audio-language learning. They are separately implemented by contrastive learning and causal masked language modeling, based on modality grouping strategy." (Section 4.2)

11) video_representation
Answer: Represents content as learned embeddings from separate encoders (VideoSwin/CLIP, AST, BERT) projected into a common semantic space for contrastive alignment and conditional generation.
Quotes:
- "MGA projects three modalities into the same common space, and establishes fine-grained alignment between three modality groups including vision-language, audiolanguage and audiovisual-language via contrastive learning." (Section 1)
- "VALOR consists of a text encoder, a vision encoder, an audio encoder and a multimodal decoder." (Section 4.1)

12) model_pipeline
Answer: Video/Audio/Text → Separate Encoders (VideoSwin/CLIP, AST, BERT) → Multimodal Grouping Alignment (MGA) for contrastive learning and Multimodal Grouping Captioning (MGC) with a decoder for generation → Retrieved clips or generated text (captions/answers).
Quotes:
- "VALOR uses three separate encoders to achieve single modality representations, and a multimodal decoder which partly shares parameters with text encoder is used for text generation. MGA and MGC tasks based on modality grouping strategy are used to improve VALOR's generalization capability..." (Figure 5, Caption)

13) dataset
Answer: Created the VALOR-1M pretraining dataset and VALOR-32K benchmark; also used WebVid-2.5M, CC14M, and HD_VILA_10M for pretraining and evaluated on numerous public benchmarks like MSRVTT, DiDeMo, AudioCaps, and MSVD-QA.
Quotes:
- "To promote vision-audio-language pretraining research, we construct a large-scale high-quality tri-modality dataset named VALOR-1M, which contains 1M audiable videos with human annotated audiovisual captions." (Abstract)
- "Besides VALOR-1M, we also establish a new benchmark VALOR-32K for evaluations on audiovisuallanguage capabilities." (Section 1)
- "The following 4 datasets are used for VALOR's pretraining. VALOR-1M... WebVid-2.5M... CC14M... HD VILA 100M..." (Section 5.1.1)

14) comparisons
Answer: Compared against SOTA models like CLIP-VIP, InternVideo, GIT2, and Flamingo on retrieval, captioning, and QA tasks across datasets like MSRVTT, DiDeMo, ActivityNet, and AudioCaps, reporting significant performance gains.
Quotes:
- "VALOR outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods by 3.8%, 6.2%, 12.7%, 0.6%, 10.4% (R@1) on textto-video retrieval benchmarks including MSRVTT, DiDeMo, ActivityNet, LSMDC and VATEX..." (Section 1)
- "VALOR outperforms GIT2 big model [] on VATEX captioning benchmark with only 0.26% training data and 11.6% parameters." (Section 1)
- "...surpasses previous SOTA methods by 3.8%, 3.4%, 5.1%, 12.5%, respectively." (Section 5.2.1, Open-Ended Video QA)

15) hyperparameters
Answer: Pretrained with a learning rate of 1e-4, batch sizes of 512 (VALORB) or 1024 (VALORL), 224 resolution, sampling 1 frame per video, and using a 60% mask probability for MGC.
Quotes:
- "The pretraining learning rate is 1e-4." (Section 5.1.3)
- "VALORB | VALOR-1M | WebVid-2.5M+CC3M | 6.5M | Video SwinB | 512 | 200K | 342M | 224" (Table 2)
- "VALORL | VALOR-1M | WebVid-2.5M+CC14M+HD VILA 10M | 33.5M | CLIPL | 1024 | 500K | 593M | 224" (Table 2)
- "For each video, we sample 1 video frame and 1 audio clip during pretraining." (Section 5.1.3)

16) environment
Answer: Trained using the PyTorch framework on 8 Tesla A100 GPUs.
Quotes:
- "All models are trained based on PyTorch framework and 8 Tesla A100 cards." (Section 5.1.3)

17) repository
Answer: https://casia-iva-group.github.io/projects/VALOR
Quotes:
- "Code and data are available at project page https://casia-iva-group.github.io/projects/VALOR." (Abstract)

18) authors
Answer: Authors are affiliated with the University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Institute of Automation (Chinese Academy of Sciences), and Nanjing University of Science and Technology; venue and year are not mentioned.
Quotes:
- "Jing Liu, Sihan Chen, Xingjian He, Longteng Guo, Xinxin Zhu, Weining Wang, Jinhui Tang, Senior Member, IEEE" (Title Block)

raw_output:
1) video_segmentation
Answer: Not mentioned. The paper uses pre-segmented clips from the HD_VILA_100M dataset and filters them by length, but does not describe the original segmentation method.
Quotes:
- "we select a 27M video clips from the HD_VILA_100M dataset instead of using all of them." (Section 3.1)
- "Clips whose length are shorter than 5 seconds or longer than 30 seconds are dropped." (Section 3.1)

2) frame_sampling_method
Answer: One video frame is randomly sampled for each video clip during the pretraining stage.
Quotes:
- "During the pertaining stage, one video frame and two 10s long audio clips are randomly sampled for each video clip." (Section 5.1)

3) input_video_length
Answer: The model processes video clips with lengths between 5 and 30 seconds, selected from long-form videos. No processed video is longer than 60 minutes.
Quotes:
- "Clips whose length are shorter than 5 seconds or longer than 30 seconds are dropped." (Section 3.1)
- "Clips are evenly chosen from the original 3.3M long-form videos in HD_VILA_100M." (Section 3.1)

4) spatiotemporal_analysis
Answer: Not mentioned. The model uses a ViT vision encoder on a single randomly sampled frame, suggesting no explicit spatiotemporal analysis is performed.
Quotes:
- "The vision encoder takes raw images or sparsely sampled video frames as input and produces the output feature ." (Section 4.1)
- "During the pertaining stage, one video frame... are randomly sampled for each video clip." (Section 5.1)

5) visual_analysis
Answer: The paper uses a multi-modal approach, not visual-only. The primary visual backbone is EVAClip-ViT-G, with CLIP-ViT-B used for ablation studies.
Quotes:
- "VAST employs a fully end-to-end Transformer architecture, comprising a vision encoder (ViT [35]), an audio encoder (BEATs [36]), and a text encoder (BERT [2])." (Section 4.1)
- "The vision, audio, and text encoders are initialized from EVAClip-ViT-G [39], BEATs, and BERT-B, respectively, resulting in a total parameter size of 1.3B." (Section 5.1)

6) speech_audio_analysis
Answer: The model processes pre-existing subtitles, which are often derived from ASR, as a distinct input modality but does not perform ASR or use speech embeddings itself.
Quotes:
- "Clips which have missing modalities of vision, audio or subtitle are dropped." (Section 3.1)
- "This dataset consists of 136 million video clips from 1.22 million instructional YouTube videos, and employs automatically generated subtitles from ASR transcription tools as corresponding clip captions." (Section 2.1)

7) sound_audio_analysis
Answer: Yes, non-speech audio is analyzed using a BEATs audio encoder to process audio clips and generate audio captions.
Quotes:
- "VAST employs a fully end-to-end Transformer architecture, comprising... an audio encoder (BEATs [36])" (Section 4.1)
- "For audio clips, they are first divided into multiple 10-second-long clips, padded with zeros, converted to 64-dimensional log Mel filterbank spectrograms using a 25ms Hamming window, and then fed into the audio encoder to obtain the output feature ." (Section 4.1)

8) qa_interaction
Answer: No, the model is evaluated on standard QA benchmarks by formulating them as open-ended generative problems, but it does not have interactive chat capabilities.
Quotes:
- "For QA tasks, they are formulated as open-ended generative problems, where questions serve as prefixes, and answers are predicted without any constraints." (Section 5.1)
- "we employ Vicuna-13b [8], an off-the-shelf Large Language Model (LLM), as a zero-shot omni-modality captioner." (Section 1)

9) retrieval_level
Answer: The system performs retrieval at the video clip level, returning a ranked list of clips based on a two-stage ranking process using contrastive and matching losses.
Quotes:
- "For retrieval tasks, all candidates are ranked using VCC, and then the Top-50 candidates are reranked using VCM." (Section 5.1)

10) inferring_method
Answer: Inferences for tasks like retrieval, captioning, and QA are made via the trained VAST foundation model's forward pass, using learned multi-modal representations for similarity scoring or generative decoding.
Quotes:
- "For retrieval tasks, all candidates are ranked using VCC, and then the Top-50 candidates are reranked using VCM. For captioning tasks, beam search with a beam size of 3 is employed. For QA tasks, they are formulated as open-ended generative problems..." (Section 5.1)

11) video_representation
Answer: Video content is represented via concatenated multi-modal embeddings from vision (EVAClip-ViT-G), audio (BEATs), and subtitle (BERT) encoders, which are then used for contrastive learning and other downstream tasks.
Quotes:
- "The global omni-modality representations of video clips, denoted as , are obtained by concatenating , , and ." (Section 4.2)
- "Subsequently, and are projected into the same semantic space using two linear layers and are then normalized." (Section 4.2)

12) model_pipeline
Answer: Video (vision, audio, subtitle) and text modalities are fed into separate encoders, whose outputs are fused via cross-attention in a text encoder and trained with contrastive, matching, and generation losses to produce retrieved clips, captions, or QA answers.
Quotes:
- "VAST consists of three single modality encoders while text encoder can fulfill cross-modality fusion through cross-attention layers. VAST is trained with three objectives including OM-VCC, OM-VCM, and OM-VCG (detailed in section 4.2) to enhance omnimodality understanding and generation capabilities." (Abstract)

13) dataset
Answer: The paper introduces the VAST-27M dataset, created from HD_VILA_100M. The model is trained on a combination of VAST-27M, VALOR-1M, WavCaps, CC14M, and LAION-400M, and evaluated on numerous standard benchmarks.
Quotes:
- "Through this process, we create VAST-27M, a dataset that consists of 27 million video clips, each paired with 11 captions (including 5 vision, 5 audio, and 1 omni-modality caption)." (Section 1)
- "The training is conducted on a combination corpus consisting of VAST-27M, VALOR-1M, WavCaps, CC14M, and 110M randomly sampled pairs from LAION-400M, for a total of 200K training steps." (Section 5.1)

14) comparisons
Answer: VAST is compared to numerous state-of-the-art models (e.g., VALOR, GIT2) across 20+ benchmarks, achieving significant gains, such as +5.1 R@1 on MSRVTT retrieval and +7.6 R@1 on Clothov1 retrieval.
Quotes:
- "VAST has achieved **22 new SOTA results**." (Table 3)
- "SOTA_VisSOTA_MMVAST 58.8[54]54.4[7]63.9 (+5.1)" (Table 3, MSRVTT Retrieval)
- "SOTA VAST 17.5[7] 25.1 (+7.6)" (Table 3, Clothov1 Retrieval)

15) hyperparameters
Answer: Key hyperparameters include a batch size of 1024, an initial learning rate of 1e-4 with linear decay, 200K training steps, 1 sampled frame, and two 10s audio clips per video.
Quotes:

we implemented RAG by utilizing the advanced text encoding model Contriever [] to encode the fetched OCR texts into text embeddings Eocr. These embeddings are then stored in a database with the FAISS index []" (Section 3.3)

dataset.answer: The method was evaluated on the Video-MME, MLVU, and LongVideoBench benchmarks; no new dataset was created.

paper_file: VideoStory Embeddings Recognize Events when Examples are Scarce.md

video_segmentation.answer: Not mentioned. The paper appears to process videos as single units without segmenting them into shots or scenes.

frame_sampling_method.answer: Frames are extracted by uniformly sampling every two seconds.
quotes: For each video the frames are extracted by uniformly sampling every two seconds." (Section 4.2)

input_video_length.answer: Not mentioned. The paper uses datasets of user-generated web videos with large variations in length but does not specify per-video durations or length-based filtering.
quotes: This collection encompass 45,826 videos harvested from YouTube, with a total length of 743 hours." (Section 4.1.1)
These corpora contain more than 42K videos in total, including user generated web videos with a large variation in quality, length and content." (Section 4.1.2)

spatiotemporal_analysis.answer: Yes, motion is modeled using MBH descriptors along motion trajectories, which are aggregated per video using a Fisher vector.
quotes: We use the MBH descriptors along the motion trajectories [72] as motion features." (Section 4.2)

The extracted 288 dimensional descriptors are reduced to 128 dimensions using PCA and are then aggregated per video using a Fisher vector [59], with 128 Gaussians resulting in a 32,768 dimensional vector." (Section 4.2)

visual_analysis.answer: No, the paper is not visual-only as it also analyzes motion and audio; the visual backbone is a Google Inception network pre-trained on ImageNet.
quotes: We adopt the *video CNN* representation [78] as appearance features for event recognition, but found the very deep network of [64] to perform slightly better than [60]." (Section 4.2)
Then, the CNN descriptors are extracted per frame as the 1K dimensional responses from the last fully connected layer (pool5) of the Google Inception network [64]." (Section 4.2)
In general any video contains appearance, motion, and audio cues and sometimes even textual information in the form of subtitles or speech recognition scripts." (Section 3.2)

speech_audio_analysis.answer: Not mentioned. The paper mentions speech recognition as a possibility but implements a general audio feature extractor (MFCCs) without specific speech analysis.
quotes: In general any video contains appearance, motion, and audio cues and sometimes even textual information in the form of subtitles or speech recognition scripts." (Section 3.2)

sound_audio_analysis.answer: Yes, non-speech audio is analyzed using MFCC descriptors aggregated by Fisher vectors.
quotes: We extract MFCCs descriptors [1] over a 10ms window. The descriptors consist of 13 values, 30 coefficients and the log-energy, along with their derivatives and the second derivatives." (Section 4.2)
The MFCC descriptors are aggregated by Fisher vectors using a Gaussian Mixture Model with 256 components, resulting in a 46,080 dimensional vector." (Section 4.2)

qa_interaction.answer: Not mentioned. The system performs event recognition from a textual query but does not feature interactive Q&A or chat capabilities.

retrieval_level.answer: The system returns a ranked list of videos based on cosine similarity to an event query.
quotes: ...and to rank all video representations based on the cosine similarity with the event query." (Section 2.3)
Finally, the ranking is obtained by measuring the similarity between the video representations and the event query based on the cosine similarity:..." (Section 3.3)

inferring_method.answer: Events are inferred by learning a "VideoStory" embedding that maps low-level video features to a semantic space, then using SVMs for few-shot recognition or cosine similarity for zero-shot recognition.
quotes: Our goal is to learn a representation function , which maps each low-level video representation into the semantic representation ." (Section 3)
For few-example event recognition, the event classifiers are trained as binary SVM with RBF kernels..." (Section 4.3)
Finally, the ranking is obtained by measuring the similarity between the video representations and the event query based on the cosine similarity..." (Section 3.3)

video_representation.answer: Content is represented via a learned semantic embedding called "VideoStory", which maps low-level appearance, motion, and audio features into a joint semantic space derived from video descriptions.
quotes: In this paper we present a semantic representation learning algorithm for videos. Instead of relying on pre-specified attribute labels, we learn the representation from freely available web videos and their descriptions." (Section 1)
We call our video representation *VideoStory*, since it strives to encode the story of a video from its description." (Section 1)

model_pipeline.answer: Low-level video features (appearance, motion, audio) and text descriptions are used to train a VideoStory embedding, which then maps unseen videos into a semantic space for event recognition via SVMs or cosine similarity ranking.
quotes: Our goal is to learn a representation function , which maps each low-level video representation into the semantic representation . The representation function is trained on a collection of videos and their semantic descriptions..." (Section 3)
After training the visual and textual projection matrices, they are used to predict the VideoStory representation and the term vector of each video." (Section 3.1)

dataset.answer: The VideoStory embedding is learned on the newly created VideoStory46K dataset; evaluation is performed on TRECVID MED 2013, MED 2014, and a modified Columbia Consumer Video (CCV10) dataset.
quotes: In all the experiments, we learn the VideoStory embeddings on the *VideoStory46K* [20] dataset. This collection encompass 45,826 videos harvested from YouTube, with a total length of 743 hours." (Section 4.1.1)
We perform our event recognition experiments on the challenging TRECVID Multimedia Event Detection corpus [62] and the Columbia Consumer Video collection [33]." (Section 4.1.2)
We denote our redefinition of the CCV dataset for few-example event recognition as *CCV*10." (Section 4.1.2)

comparisons.answer: Compared to prior state-of-the-art on MED 2013, VideoStoryF achieves 37.1 mAP (+6.2 vs. Chang et al. [8]) for few-example recognition, and VideoStoryF0 achieves 20.0 mAP (+1.7 vs. Jiang et al. [31]) for zero-example recognition.
quotes: The results are reported in Table 6. It shows that our proposed representation learning sets a new state-of-the-art for the both few- and zero-example event recognition." (Section 5.4)
This paper: VideoStoryF | | 37.1" (Table 6)
This paper: VideoStoryF0 | | 20.0" (Table 6)

hyperparameters.answer: The VideoStory embedding dimensionality is 2,048; SVM regularization and RBF kernel parameters are set to 1; other hyperparameters (λw, λa, λs, epochs, η) are set via validation on 25% of the VideoStory46K dataset.
quotes: From now on we use a fixed 2,048 dimensional Video-Story representation, which is optimal based on Figure 4." (Section 5.1.2)

We learn the VideoStory embeddings by using 75% of the VideoStory46K dataset for training and 25% for validation to set the hyper-parameters of our model (λw, λa, λs) and of SGD (number of epochs, η)." (Section 4.3)

Following [78], [81] we set the SVM regularization and the RBF kernel parameters by a default value of 1, as the train set is not big enough to reliably estimate them by crossvalidation." (Section 4.3)

environment.answer: Not mentioned, except for a learning time comparison performed on a CPU Intel Xeon E5-2690@2.90GHZ with 256GB of memory.
quotes: We also compare the learning time for VideoStory versus the term-attribute baselines on a CPU Intel Xeon E5-2690@2.90GHZ with 256GB of memory." (Section 5.1.1)

repository.answer: Not mentioned.

authors.answer: The authors are Amirhossein Habibian, Thomas Mensink, and Cees G. M. Snoek. A preliminary version was published at MM 2014. Author affiliations are not mentioned.
quotes: Amirhossein Habibian, Thomas Mensink, Cees G. M. Snoek" (Title Page)
A preliminary version of this article appeared as [20]." (Section 1)

Answer: Compared to prior state-of-the-art on MED 2013, VideoStoryF achieves 37.1 mAP (+6.2 vs. Chang et al. [8]) for few-example recognition, and VideoStoryF0 achieves 20.0 mAP (+1.7 vs. Jiang et al. [31]) for zero-example recognition.

- "The results are reported in Table 6. It shows that our proposed representation learning sets a new state-of-the-art for the both few- and zero-example event recognition." (Section 5.4)
- "This paper: VideoStoryF | | 37.1" (Table 6)
- "This paper: VideoStoryF0 | | 20.0" (Table 6)

15) hyperparameters
Answer: The VideoStory embedding dimensionality is 2,048; SVM regularization and RBF kernel parameters are set to 1; other hyperparameters (λw, λa, λs, epochs, η) are set via validation on 25% of the VideoStory46K dataset.
Quotes:
- "From now on we use a fixed 2,048 dimensional Video-Story representation, which is optimal based on Figure 4." (Section 5.1.2)
- "We learn the VideoStory embeddings by using 75% of the VideoStory46K dataset for training and 25% for validation to set the hyper-parameters of our model (λw, λa, λs) and of SGD (number of epochs, η)." (Section 4.3)
- "Following [78], [81] we set the SVM regularization and the RBF kernel parameters by a default value of 1, as the train set is not big enough to reliably estimate them by crossvalidation." (Section 4.3)

16) environment
Answer: Not mentioned, except for a learning time comparison performed on a CPU Intel Xeon E5-2690@2.90GHZ with 256GB of memory.
Quotes:
- "We also compare the learning time for VideoStory versus the term-attribute baselines on a CPU Intel Xeon E5-2690@2.90GHZ with 256GB of memory." (Section 5.1.1)

17) repository
Answer: Not mentioned.
Quotes: None.

18) authors
Answer: The authors are Amirhossein Habibian, Thomas Mensink, and Cees G. M. Snoek. A preliminary version was published at MM 2014. Author affiliations are not mentioned.
Quotes:
- "Amirhossein Habibian, Thomas Mensink, Cees G. M. Snoek" (Title Page)
- "A preliminary version of this article appeared as [20]." (Section 1)
- "Habibian et al. [20] | MM 2014 | 19.6" (Table 6)

video_representation.answer: Videos are represented by detected text from OCR and ASR, and a vector of conditional probabilities for visual concepts (object, scene, action), which are then embedded into a distributional semantic space.
