raw_output:
1) video_segmentation
Answer: Not mentioned. The paper refers to action concepts being detected per video chunk, but does not specify the segmentation method used to create these chunks.
Quotes:
- "We define object and scene concept probabilities per video frame, and action concepts per video chunks." (Visual Concept Detection functions Section)
- "is the video level probability of the action concept, pooled over a set of video chunks u." (Video level concept probabilities Section)
- "The chunk size is set to the mean chunk length of all concept training chunks." (Video level concept probabilities Section)

2) frame_sampling_method
Answer: Uniform sampling of one frame every 250 frames for object and scene concepts.
Quotes:
- "are selected every M frames in v (M= 250)." (Video level concept probabilities Section)

3) input_video_length
Answer: Not mentioned. The paper processes consumer-generated videos from the TRECVID MED dataset, but does not state specific video durations or mention processing videos longer than 60 minutes.
Quotes:
- "Every minute, hundreds of hours of video are uploaded to video archival site such as YouTube (Google2014 )." (Introduction)
- "We evaluated our method on the large TRECVID MED (Felzenszwalb, McAllester, and Ramanan 2013)." (Experiments Section)

4) spatiotemporal_analysis
Answer: Spatiotemporal analysis is performed via pre-trained action concept detectors adopted from Liu et al. (2013), but specific models are not detailed.
Quotes:
- "We use both manually annotated (i.e. strongly supervised) and automatically annotated (i.e. weekly supervised) concepts... We have 500 action concepts; please refer to (Liu et al. 2013) for the action concept detection method that we adopt." (Action Concepts Section)

5) visual_analysis
Answer: Yes, the method uses a visual-only modality for concept-based retrieval, employing Overfeat for objects and SIFT/HOG bag-of-words for scenes.
Quotes:
- "We leverage the information from three types of visual concepts in C: object concepts C_o, action concepts C_a, and scene concepts C_s." (Visual Concept Detection functions Section)
- "We involve 1000 Overfeat (Sermanet et al. 2014) object concept detectors which maps to 1000-ImageNet categories." (Object Concepts Section)
- "We represented scene concepts as bag of word representation on static features (i.e., SIFT (Lowe 2004) and HOG (Dalal and Triggs 2005)) with 10000 codebooks." (Scene Concepts Section)

6) speech_audio_analysis
Answer: Yes, the system uses Automated Speech Recognition (ASR) to extract text from speech, which is then embedded into the semantic space for retrieval.
Quotes:
- "a video v is defined by three pieces of information, which are video OCR denoted by 𝑣_𝑂𝐶𝑅, video ASR denoted by 𝑣_𝐴𝑆𝑅, and video concept representation denoted by 𝑣_𝑐." (Video Representation Section)
- "We used (van Hout et al. 2013) to extract 𝑣_𝐴𝑆𝑅." (Video Representation Section)

7) sound_audio_analysis
Answer: Not mentioned. Audio concepts trained on MFCC features were explored but excluded from the final experiments due to poor performance.
Quotes:
- "The audio concepts include acoustic related concepts like water sound. We performed an experiment on a set of audio concepts trained on MFCC audio features... However, we found their performance MAP, and hence we excluded them from our final experiments." (Concept Set c Section)

8) qa_interaction
Answer: Not mentioned. The system performs zero-shot event retrieval based on a free-text query, not interactive question-answering.
Quotes:
- "The main question addressed in this paper is how to use an event text query (i.e. just the event title like "birthday party" or "feeding an animal") to retrieve a ranked list of videos based on their content." (Introduction)

9) retrieval_level
Answer: The system retrieves a ranked list of videos relevant to a free-text event query.
Quotes:
- "The main question addressed in this paper is how to use an event text query (i.e. just the event title like "birthday party" or "feeding an animal") to retrieve a ranked list of videos based on their content." (Introduction)

10) inferring_method
Answer: Event relevance is inferred by embedding the text query and multimodal video content (concepts, ASR, OCR) into a distributional semantic space and measuring their similarity.
Quotes:
- "We embed videos into a distributional semantic space and then measure the similarity between videos and the event query in a free text form." (Abstract)
- "our goal is to compute p(e|v) by embedding both the event query e and information of video v of different modalities... into a distributional semantic space, where relevance of v to e could be directly computed" (Video Representation Section)

11) video_representation
Answer: Videos are represented by detected text from OCR and ASR, and a vector of conditional probabilities for visual concepts (object, scene, action), which are then embedded into a distributional semantic space.
Quotes:
- "For our zero-shot purpose, a video v is defined by three pieces of information, which are video OCR denoted by 𝑣_𝑂𝐶𝑅, video ASR denoted by 𝑣_𝐴𝑆𝑅, and video concept representation denoted by 𝑣_𝑐." (Video Representation Section)
- "The video concept based representation is defined as 𝑣_𝑐 = {𝑝(𝑐_1|𝑣), 𝑝(𝑐_2|𝑣), ..., 𝑝(𝑐_𝑁|𝑣)}, where 𝑝(𝑐_𝑖|𝑣) is a conditional probability of concept 𝑐_𝑖 given video v" (Video Representation Section)

12) model_pipeline
Answer: Free-text query and video → Extract visual concepts, ASR, and OCR from video → Embed query and video modalities into a word2vec semantic space and compute similarity → Ranked list of relevant videos.
Quotes:
- "Given an arbitrary event query e and a video v (e.g. just "birthday party"), our objective is to model p(e|v)... by embedding both the event query e and information of video v of different modalities... into a distributional semantic space, where relevance of v to e could be directly computed" (Problem Definition & Video Representation Sections)

13) dataset
Answer: The method was validated on the TRECVID MED challenge dataset (MEDTest set), using distributional semantic models trained on Wikipedia and GoogleNews text corpora.
Quotes:
- "We validated our method on the large TRECVID MED (Multimedia Event Detection) challenge." (Abstract)
- "We evaluated our method on the large TRECVID MED (Felzenszwalb, McAllester, and Ramanan 2013). We show the MAP (Mean Average Precision) and ROC AUC performance of the designated MEDTest set" (Experiments Section)
- "There are two distributional semantic models in our experiments, trained on Wikipedia and GoogleNews using (Mikolov et al. 2013b)." (Experiments Section)

14) comparisons
Answer: Compared against Dalton et al. (2013), Wu et al. (2014), and reranking systems like SPaR on TRECVID MED, achieving a 13.1% MAP and 0.830 AUC, outperforming Wu et al. (12.6% MAP, 0.730 AUC).
Quotes:
- "Using only the event title as a query, our method outperformed the state-of-the-art that uses big descriptions from 12.6% to 13.5% with MAP metric and 0.73 to 0.83 with ROC-AUC metric." (Abstract)
- "We further achieved the best performance of our system by fusing all Concepts, OCR, and ASR to achieve 13.1% MAP and 0.830 average AUC. We found our system achieves better than the state of the art system (Wu et al. 2014) 4.0% gain in MAP, but significantly in average AUC; see 13.6% gain to (Wu et al. 2014) in table 5." (Fusion Experiments and Related Systems Section)

15) hyperparameters
Answer: Word vector dimensions: 250 (Wikipedia model) or 300 (GoogleNews model); Frame sampling rate: M=250; Top concepts used for retrieval: R=5.
Quotes:
- "The Wikipedia model got trained on 1 billion words resulting in a vocabulary of size of≈120,000 words and word vectors of 250 dimensions. The GoogleNews model got trained on 100 billion words resulting in a vocabulary of size 3 million words and word vectors of 300 dimensions." (Experiments Section)
- "are selected every M frames in v (M= 250)." (Video level concept probabilities Section)
- "we only include 𝑝(𝑐_𝑖|𝑣) in 𝑣_𝑐 such that 𝑐_𝑖 is among the top R concepts with highest 𝑠(𝑒_𝑐, 𝑐_𝑖). ... we used R=5." (Estimating p(e_c|v) Section)

16) environment
Answer: Retrieval experiments were run on a 16-core Intel Xeon processor with 64GB RAM, taking approximately 270 seconds for 20 events.
Quotes:
- "our concept retrieval experiment on our whole concept set it takes ≈270 seconds on a 16 cores Intel Xeon processor (64GB RAM) to the retrieval task on 20 events altogether." (Fusion Experiments and Related Systems Section)

17) repository
Answer: A link to supplementary materials is provided, but no code repository is mentioned.
Quotes:
- "Supplementary Materials (SM) could be found here https:// sites.google.com/site/mhelhoseiny/EDiSE\_supp.zip" (Footnote 1)

18) authors
Answer: Authors are from Rutgers University and SRI International; the paper was published at the Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI) conference in 2016.
